topics.title: Overview of Programming Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT611 - OBJECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING IN JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Overview of Programming Languages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Programming Language** is a formal, structured vocabulary and set of syntactic and semantic rules used by programmers to communicate instructions to a computer. According to the KTU 2024 syllabus, a programming language acts as an intermediary layer between human-readable problem statements (high-level abstraction) and machine-executable binary code (low-level hardware instructions).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):** A programming language is a **notational system** for describing computations in a machine-readable and human-readable form, comprising **syntax** (grammar rules), **semantics** (meaning of expressions), and **pragmatics** (practical usability).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: "Programming Language = A Recipe Book"

Think of a programming language like a **recipe book written in different styles**:

| Human Activity | Computer Equivalent |
|----------------|---------------------|
| Recipe book in **English** | High-level language (Java, Python, C++) |
| Recipe book in **Hindi** | Another high-level language (C, Pascal) |
| Recipe book in **chef's shorthand** | Assembly language |
| **Binary code in the oven's microchip** | Machine code (0s and 1s) |

The chef (CPU) only understands one language (binary), so the recipe (source code) must either be **translated ahead of time (compiled)** or **translated line-by-line while cooking (interpreted)**.

## 1.3 Classification of Programming Languages (KTU High-Priority)

```
Programming Languages
├── 1. Low-Level Languages
│   ├── Machine Language (1GL) – Binary 0s and 1s
│   └── Assembly Language (2GL) – Mnemonics (MOV, ADD)
│
├── 2. High-Level Languages (3GL) – Java, C, C++, Python, FORTRAN
│
├── 3. Very High-Level Languages (4GL) – SQL, MATLAB
│
└── 4. Fifth-Generation Languages (5GL) – Prolog, AI-based logic languages
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Java belongs to the 3GL category** (High-Level, Object-Oriented). However, Java occupies a *unique hybrid position* because its **bytecode** is **NOT** machine code, yet **NOT** purely high-level either — this is why Java is called a **"Platform-Independent Intermediate Language."**

## 1.4 Core Terminology Cheat Block

- **Source Code** → Human-readable `.java` file written by the programmer.
- **Compiler** → A translator that converts the **entire source code** into machine code (or bytecode) **in one shot** (e.g., `javac` for Java).
- **Interpreter** → A translator that converts and executes the program **line by line** (e.g., traditional Python).
- **Bytecode** → The intermediate `.class` file produced by the Java compiler; **NOT** directly executable by hardware.
- **JVM (Java Virtual Machine)** → The runtime engine that interprets/converts bytecode into native machine instructions for the underlying OS.
- **Platform Independence** → "Write Once, Run Anywhere (WORA)" — the same compiled `.class` file runs on Windows, Linux, or Mac without recompilation.

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield KTU Fact:** Java is **NOT a purely compiled language** and **NOT a purely interpreted language**. It uses a **two-stage translation model**: **Compile → Bytecode → Interpret (JIT inside JVM)**. This hybrid model is a **favourite KTU board question.**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Generations of Programming Languages — Structured Breakdown

### 2.1.1 First Generation (1GL) — Machine Language
- **Why:** Direct execution by hardware, no translator needed.
- **How:** Uses binary digits `0` and `1`.
- **Drawback:** Error-prone, unreadable, hardware-specific.
- **Example:** `10110000 01100001` (Move the value 97 into the AL register on x86).

### 2.1.2 Second Generation (2GL) — Assembly Language
- **Why:** Mnemonics make it human-readable.
- **How:** Uses an **Assembler** to translate mnemonics to machine code.
- **Example (x86):** `MOV AL, 61h`

### 2.1.3 Third Generation (3GL) — High-Level Procedural & OOP
- **Why:** English-like syntax, hardware-independent, portable.
- **How:** Uses compilers or interpreters.
- **Examples:** C, C++, Java, Python, COBOL, FORTRAN, Pascal.

### 2.1.4 Fourth Generation (4GL) — Domain-Specific Declarative
- **Why:** Focus on **what** to do, not **how**.
- **How:** Closer to natural language / mathematical notation.
- **Examples:** SQL, MATLAB, R, LaTeX.

### 2.1.5 Fifth Generation (5GL) — Logic & AI-Based
- **Why:** Solve problems using constraints and logic, not step-by-step algorithms.
- **How:** Uses inference engines and AI techniques.
- **Examples:** Prolog, Mercury, OPS5.

## 2.2 Translation Mechanisms — KTU High-Yield Comparison

| Feature | Compiler | Interpreter | Java's Hybrid Model |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Translation Style** | Whole program at once | Line by line | Whole program → Bytecode, then JIT interprets at runtime |
| **Execution Speed** | Fast (pre-compiled) | Slow (translate-then-run) | Fast after warm-up (JIT optimises) |
| **Error Reporting** | Lists all errors after scanning full file | Stops at first error | Compile-time + Runtime |
| **Output** | Standalone executable (`.exe`) or object code | No standalone output | `.class` bytecode + JVM execution |
| **Memory** | Efficient | Heavy (interpreter stays in memory) | Moderate |
| **Example Tool** | `gcc`, `javac` | Python IDLE, Ruby | `java` command (JVM) |

## 2.3 Java Translation Pipeline — The Heart of the Module

The Java compilation-execution process follows **5 strict stages**:

1. **Editing** → Programmer writes `MyProgram.java` in any text editor / IDE.
2. **Compiling** → `javac MyProgram.java` translates source into `MyProgram.class` (bytecode).
3. **Loading** → ClassLoader in the JVM reads the `.class` file into memory.
4. **Bytecode Verification** → JVM's verifier ensures bytecode does not violate Java's safety rules (no pointer arithmetic, no illegal memory access).
5. **Execution** → JVM's Execution Engine interprets bytecode OR uses **JIT (Just-In-Time) compiler** to convert hot bytecode paths into native machine code.

## 2.4 The Java Buzzwords (KTU Repeated 14-Mark Question)

These 11 official Java "buzzwords" are mandatory for board exams:

1. **Simple** → No pointers, no operator overloading (was true historically), automatic garbage collection.
2. **Object-Oriented** → Everything is an `Object` (except primitives).
3. **Distributed** → Built-in support for RMI, sockets, URL handling.
4. **Robust** → Strong type-checking, exception handling, no memory corruption.
5. **Secure** → Sandbox model, bytecode verifier, security manager.
6. **Architecture-Neutral / Platform Independent** → Bytecode runs on any JVM.
7. **Portable** → Same primitive sizes (e.g., `int` is **always 32 bits** across platforms).
8. **High-Performance** → JIT compiler brings performance near C++.
9. **Multi-threaded** → `Thread` class, `synchronized` keyword.
10. **Dynamic** → Loads classes at runtime via reflection, RMI.
11. **Interpreted** → Hybrid (compiled + interpreted) execution model.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Conceptual Equivalents)

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Symbol Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Java Platform Independence | $$P_{\text{run}} = \text{JVM} \times \text{Bytecode} \times \text{OS}_{\text{any}}$$ | A program runs on any OS that has a compatible JVM |
| Compilation Time | $$T_{\text{compile}} = f(N, \text{optimisation level})$$ | $N$ = number of source lines |
| Execution Time (Java Hybrid) | $$T_{\text{exec}} = T_{\text{interp}} + \sum_{i=1}^{k} T_{\text{JIT}_i}$$ | Sum of interpreted + JIT-compiled paths |
| Primitive `int` Size | $$\text{sizeof(int)} = 32 \text{ bits} = 4 \text{ bytes} \quad (\forall \text{ OS})$$ | Platform-independent fixed width |
| WORA Principle | $$\text{Compile once} \rightarrow \text{Run on } n \text{ platforms}$$ | $n$ = number of OS/hardware combinations |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in Engineering:** Platform independence is the **#1 reason** Java dominates **enterprise backend systems, Android mobile apps, and embedded IoT firmware**. The WORA principle reduces the *Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)* of cross-platform deployments.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Programming Paradigm Comparison

This is the **core analytical framework** KTU expects for a 14-mark question on "Overview of Programming Languages."

| Paradigm | Core Idea | Key Concept | Code Organisation Unit | Example Language | Java's Stance |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Procedural** | Sequence of procedure calls | Procedure / Function | Function | C, Pascal | Java **supports** methods inside classes |
| **Object-Oriented** | Bundle data + behaviour | Class & Object | Class | Java, C++, Python | Java **is fundamentally OOP** (with primitives) |
| **Functional** | Computation as math functions | Lambda expressions, immutability | Function as first-class | Haskell, Erlang | Java 8+ adds **lambda & streams** |
| **Logic** | Declare facts & rules | Predicate logic | Clause | Prolog | Java does **not** support natively |
| **Event-Driven** | React to user/system events | Event handler / callback | Event loop | JavaScript, VB | Java Swing uses **delegation event model** |
| **Concurrent** | Multiple tasks at once | Thread / Process | Task | Erlang, Go | Java has **Thread, ExecutorService, Fork/Join** |

## 3.2 Detailed Comparison — Compiled vs Interpreted vs Java Hybrid

Let us formally derive **why Java's model is optimal** for cross-platform enterprise software.

### Step 1: Define pure compilation

$$
\text{Source Code} \xrightarrow{\text{Compiler}} \text{Native Machine Code} \xrightarrow{\text{CPU executes}} \text{Output}
$$

This is **fast** but **not portable** — the binary is tied to one OS/architecture.

### Step 2: Define pure interpretation

$$
\text{Source Code} \xrightarrow[\text{line by line}]{\text{Interpreter}} \text{Output}
$$

This is **portable** (interpreter is the only platform-specific part) but **slow** — translation happens every run.

### Step 3: Java's two-stage hybrid

$$
\text{Source Code} \xrightarrow{\text{javac (Compiler)}} \text{Bytecode (.class)} \xrightarrow[\text{at runtime}]{\text{JVM (Interpreter + JIT)}} \text{Output}
$$

### Step 4: Why this is better

$$
\text{Portability of Interpreter} + \text{Speed of Compiler (JIT)} = \text{Optimal Trade-off}
$$

Concretely:

$$
T_{\text{total}}^{\text{Java}} \approx T_{\text{compile}} + \max(T_{\text{interp}}, T_{\text{JIT}})$$

Since **JIT** compiles "hot" (frequently executed) bytecode paths into native code at runtime, the steady-state execution approaches **C++-level speed** while maintaining **Python-level portability**.

## 3.3 Numerical Illustration — Why `int` Size Matters

Let us prove Java's portability claim using the primitive `int`:

**Scenario:** A programmer writes:
```java
int x = 1000000;
System.out.println(x);
```

**In C (NOT portable):**

$$
\text{sizeof(int)}_{\text{Windows 32-bit}} = 4 \text{ bytes}
$$
$$
\text{sizeof(int)}_{\text{Linux 64-bit}} = 4 \text{ bytes}
$$
$$
\text{sizeof(int)}_{\text{Embedded ARM}} = 2 \text{ bytes} \quad \leftarrow \text{ OVERFLOW RISK}
$$

**In Java (portable):**

$$
\text{sizeof(int)}_{\text{ANY platform}} = 32 \text{ bits} = 4 \text{ bytes} \quad \text{(guaranteed by JLS §4.2)}
$$

This is why the Java Language Specification **explicitly fixes primitive sizes**, eliminating the `int` overflow bug class entirely.

## 3.4 The Java Program Lifecycle — Step-by-Step Trace

Given the source file `Hello.java`:

**Step 1 — Write Source**
```java
public class Hello {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Hello, KTU!");
    }
}
```

**Step 2 — Compile**
$$
\text{Terminal: } \texttt{javac Hello.java} \Rightarrow \text{produces } \texttt{Hello.class}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify Bytecode**
The JVM's **Bytecode Verifier** performs 4 mandatory checks:
1. Valid class file format (magic number `0xCAFEBABE`).
2. No stack underflow/overflow.
3. Type safety of operands.
4. No illegal data type conversions.

**Step 4 — Load & Link**
The **ClassLoader Subsystem** loads `Hello.class` into the method area, and the JVM resolves symbolic references to direct references.

**Step 5 — Execute**
The `main(String[] args)` method is pushed onto the **JVM stack**, and bytecode instructions are executed one by one. The `println` call is resolved via the standard library's `rt.jar` (now modular `.jmod` in Java 9+).

**Output:**
```
Hello, KTU!
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** In KTU board answers, **always draw this 5-stage pipeline** when explaining Java's execution — it fetches **full 7 marks** on its own in Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Java Compilation-Execution Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["Source File MyProgram.java"]
    editor["Text Editor / IDE"]
    compiler["javac Compiler"]
    bytecode["Bytecode File MyProgram.class"]
    classloader["JVM ClassLoader Subsystem"]
    verifier["Bytecode Verifier"]
    interpreter["Interpreter Engine"]
    jit["JIT Compiler Hot Code Optimizer"]
    native["Native Machine Code"]
    jvm["JVM Execution Engine"]
    os["Host Operating System"]
    hw["CPU Hardware"]
    
    editor -->|writes| src
    src -->|input| compiler
    compiler -->|produces| bytecode
    bytecode -->|loads| classloader
    classloader -->|validates| verifier
    verifier -->|passes| jvm
    jvm --> interpreter
    jvm --> jit
    interpreter --> native
    jit --> native
    native --> os
    os --> hw
```

## 4.2 Programming Language Generations Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    gen1["1GL Machine Language Binary 0s and 1s"]
    gen2["2GL Assembly Language MOV ADD SUB"]
    gen3["3GL High Level Java C C++ Python"]
    gen4["4GL Very High Level SQL MATLAB R"]
    gen5["5GL Logic Prolog Mercury AI Rules"]
    
    gen1 -->|evolved into| gen2
    gen2 -->|evolved into| gen3
    gen3 -->|evolved into| gen4
    gen4 -->|evolved into| gen5
    
    java["Java belongs to 3GL with 4GL traits declarative annotations"]
    gen3 --- java
```

## 4.3 Translation Models — Compiler vs Interpreter vs Java Hybrid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    compA["Pure Compiler Flow"]
    compB["Source Code"]
    compC["Entire Program Scanned"]
    compD["Machine Code Executable"]
    compE["CPU Runs Directly FAST not portable"]
    
    compA --> compB --> compC --> compD --> compE
    
    intA["Pure Interpreter Flow"]
    intB["Source Code"]
    intC["Read One Line"]
    intD["Translate and Execute"]
    intE["Repeat Until Done SLOW portable"]
    
    intA --> intB --> intC --> intD --> intE
    
    hybA["Java Hybrid Flow"]
    hybB["Source MyProgram java"]
    hybC["javac Compiler"]
    hybD["Bytecode MyProgram class"]
    hybE["JVM Interpreter plus JIT"]
    hybF["Native Code at Runtime"]
    hybG["CPU Executes FAST and PORTABLE"]
    
    hybA --> hybB --> hybC --> hybD --> hybE --> hybF --> hybG
```

## 4.4 Java Buzzwords — Cognitive Map

```mermaid
mindmap
  root((Java Buzzwords))
    Simple
      No pointers
      Garbage collection
    Object Oriented
      Class and Object
      Inheritance Polymorphism
    Platform Independent
      WORA principle
      Bytecode on any JVM
    Robust
      Strong typing
      Exception handling
    Secure
      Bytecode verifier
      Sandbox
    Multithreaded
      Thread class
      synchronized keyword
    Distributed
      RMI and Sockets
    Dynamic
      Reflection API
      Runtime class loading
    High Performance
      JIT compiler
    Portable
      Fixed primitive sizes
    Interpreted
      Hybrid model
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node IDs above are alphanumeric (e.g., `compA`, `hybG`) and contain no reserved keywords. Labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting to ensure clean Mermaid v10 rendering on the KTU digital notes portal.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`** Define a programming language. List any **two differences** between a high-level language and a low-level language.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A programming language is a formal notation system comprising syntax, semantics, and rules used to express algorithms in a form executable by a computer.

| Aspect | High-Level Language | Low-Level Language |
|---|---|---|
| **Abstraction** | Hardware-independent | Hardware-specific |
| **Portability** | Highly portable | Not portable |
| **Readability** | English-like, easy to debug | Mnemonics/binary, hard to read |
| **Translation** | Compiler/Interpreter | Assembler / Direct execution |
| **Example** | Java, Python, C | Assembly, Machine Code |

*(Any two valid differences = 3 marks: 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for table)*

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`** What is **bytecode** in Java? Why is it called **platform-independent**?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Bytecode** is the intermediate, low-level, **non-executable-by-CPU** instruction set produced by the Java compiler (`javac`) after compiling a `.java` source file. It is stored in `.class` files and consists of instructions for the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** rather than for any physical processor.

**Why Platform-Independent:** Because the bytecode is **the same for all operating systems** — the same `MyProgram.class` runs on Windows, Linux, or Mac without recompilation. The **JVM** is the only platform-specific component that translates this common bytecode into the host machine's native instructions. This implements the **WORA (Write Once, Run Anywhere)** principle.

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks explanation of platform independence)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — Programming Paradigms

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**(a)** Explain the **object-oriented programming paradigm** in detail. Compare it with the **procedural programming paradigm** using a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **major generations of programming languages** (1GL to 5GL) with one example language for each generation. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — OOP vs Procedural Paradigm

**Definition (1 Mark):** Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) organises software as a collection of **objects** that bundle **data (attributes)** and **behaviour (methods)**, emphasising concepts like **encapsulation, inheritance, polymorphism, and abstraction**.

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Procedural | Object-Oriented |
|---|---|---|
| Basic Unit | Function / Procedure | Class / Object |
| Data Handling | Global, exposed | Encapsulated inside class |
| Reusability | Function libraries | Inheritance & polymorphism |
| Extensibility | Hard for large projects | Easier via inheritance |
| Real-world mapping | Weak (functions first) | Strong (objects first) |

**Example (3 Marks):**

*Procedural (C-style):*
```c
struct Student { char name[30]; int age; };
void setAge(struct Student *s, int a) { s->age = a; }
int getAge(struct Student *s) { return s->age; }
```
Functions and data are **separate entities**; the programmer must manually pass the structure to each function.

*Object-Oriented (Java):*
```java
class Student {
    private String name;
    private int age;
    public void setAge(int a) { this.age = a; }
    public int getAge() { return this.age; }
}
```
Here, **data (`age`) and behaviour (`setAge`, `getAge`) are bound together** inside one `Student` class. External code cannot access `age` directly — it must use the public methods. This is **encapsulation**, the cornerstone of OOP.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Generations of Languages

**1GL — Machine Language (1.5 Marks):**
Binary `0/1` instructions directly executed by the CPU. Hardware-specific, extremely fast, but unreadable.
*Example:* `10110000 01100001` (x86 `MOV AL, 61h`).

**2GL — Assembly Language (1.5 Marks):**
Mnemonic representation of machine code. Translated by an **assembler**. Still hardware-specific but more readable.
*Example:* `ADD R1, R2, R3` (ARM).

**3GL — High-Level Procedural & OOP (2 Marks):**
English-like syntax, hardware-independent. Translated by compilers/interpreters. Supports structured programming.
*Examples:* **Java, C, C++, Python, FORTRAN**.

**4GL — Very High-Level Declarative (1 Mark):**
Focus on **what** to compute, not **how**. Domain-specific.
*Examples:* **SQL** (`SELECT * FROM Students WHERE age > 18`).

**5GL — Logic / Constraint-Based (1 Mark):**
Programs are expressed as **logical statements and constraints**, solved by inference engines. Used in AI.
*Example:* **Prolog** — `ancestor(X, Y) :- parent(X, Y).`

---

### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — Java Translation Model

**`[KTU University Exam - June 2023]`**

**(a)** With a **neat block diagram**, explain the **Java compilation and execution process**. How does Java achieve **platform independence**? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** List and explain **any 7 of the 11 Java buzzwords** with one-line justifications. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Java Compilation & Execution

**Diagram (3 Marks):** The student should reproduce the **5-stage pipeline** from Section 4.1 (Source → javac → Bytecode → ClassLoader → Verifier → JVM Execution Engine → Native Code → CPU).

**Step-by-Step Explanation (3 Marks):**

1. **Source Code Creation** → `MyProgram.java` is written by the programmer in any text editor or IDE.
2. **Compilation** → The `javac` compiler translates the source into **bytecode** stored in `MyProgram.class`. Bytecode is a set of instructions for the abstract JVM, not for any real CPU.
3. **Class Loading** → At runtime, the JVM's **ClassLoader Subsystem** reads the `.class` file into memory.
4. **Bytecode Verification** → The **verifier** checks for stack overflow, type mismatches, illegal access, and invalid opcodes — this is Java's **security firewall**.
5. **Execution** → The JVM's **Execution Engine** either **interprets** the bytecode line-by-line or uses the **JIT (Just-In-Time) compiler** to convert hot paths into **native machine code** for the host OS.

**Platform Independence Mechanism (1 Mark):**
The **bytecode is the same** on every machine. The **JVM is the only platform-specific component**. So a `MyProgram.class` compiled on Windows runs unchanged on Linux or Mac, provided each platform has a JVM installed. This is the **WORA (Write Once, Run Anywhere)** principle — the foundation of Java's cross-platform dominance in enterprise and Android development.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Seven Java Buzzwords

| # | Buzzword | Explanation (1 Mark Each) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Simple** | Eliminates complex features of C++ (pointers, multiple inheritance, operator overloading) and adds automatic garbage collection. |
| 2 | **Object-Oriented** | Everything in Java is an object (except primitives), built around classes, inheritance, polymorphism, and encapsulation. |
| 3 | **Platform Independent** | Compiled bytecode runs identically on any OS with a JVM — implements WORA. |
| 4 | **Robust** | Strong compile-time and run-time type checking, exception handling, and no manual memory management eliminate entire classes of bugs. |
| 5 | **Secure** | Bytecode verifier + SecurityManager + sandboxed applets (historically) prevent unauthorised memory access and virus propagation. |
| 6 | **Multithreaded** | Built-in `Thread` class and `synchronized` keyword enable concurrent execution of multiple tasks within a single program. |
| 7 | **Dynamic** | Classes are loaded on demand at runtime, supporting reflection, RMI, and runtime type information. |

*(Any 7 chosen = 7 marks; writing all 11 fetches bonus appreciation from the examiner.)*

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Pitfalls on This Topic:**
> 1. **Never write "Java is purely compiled" or "Java is purely interpreted."** This is the **#1 board-exam trap** and costs 2-3 marks instantly. Always state "Java uses a **hybrid two-stage model**: compiled to bytecode by `javac`, then interpreted/JIT-compiled by the JVM at runtime."
> 2. **Confusing JVM with JRE/JDK.** KTU examiners specifically test:
>    - **JVM** = Runtime engine that executes bytecode.
>    - **JRE** = JVM + standard libraries (for end-users).
>    - **JDK** = JRE + development tools like `javac` (for developers).
> 3. **Forgetting the "Why"** — don't just list buzzwords. Each buzzword must be tied to a **practical benefit** (e.g., "Multithreaded → allows responsive GUIs and parallel server request handling").
> 4. **Missing the diagram** in 14-mark Java execution questions. A neat block diagram of the **Source → Compiler → Bytecode → JVM → Native** pipeline is **mandatory** for full marks.
> 5. **Confusing `int` size in Java vs C.** Java's `int` is **always 32 bits** (JLS guarantee). C's `int` size depends on the platform. This is a frequent 3-mark sub-question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams:**

- **Programming Language =** Formal notation with **syntax + semantics** for instructing computers.
- **5 Generations:** 1GL (Machine) → 2GL (Assembly) → 3GL (High-Level, *Java belongs here*) → 4GL (Declarative/SQL) → 5GL (Logic/Prolog).
- **Java's Hybrid Model:** `Source (.java) → javac → Bytecode (.class) → JVM (Interpreter + JIT) → Native Machine Code`. **Never call it "purely compiled" or "purely interpreted."**
- **WORA Principle:** "Write Once, Run Anywhere" — achieved by making the **bytecode universal** and the **JVM platform-specific**.
- **11 Java Buzzwords:** Simple, Object-Oriented, Distributed, Robust, Secure, Architecture-Neutral, Portable, High-Performance, Multithreaded, Dynamic, Interpreted.
- **`int` is always 32 bits in Java** across all platforms (JLS §4.2).
- **JVM ≠ JRE ≠ JDK** — JVM executes bytecode, JRE = JVM + libraries, JDK = JRE + dev tools.
- **Bytecode Verifier** is Java's security checkpoint — validates type safety and stack integrity before execution.
- **JIT (Just-In-Time Compiler)** converts frequently executed bytecode ("hot spots") into native code at runtime, giving Java near-C++ performance.
- **OOP core principles:** Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Abstraction — must be defined with one-line examples.
- **Common pitfall answer for 14-mark questions:** Always pair *theoretical explanation + diagram + example code* for full marks.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Overview of Programming Languages**
> *Aligned with KTU 2024 Scheme | OEITT611 Object-Oriented Programming in Java*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Setting Up the Development Environment (IDE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT611 - OBJECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING IN JAVA
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Setting Up the Development Environment (IDE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Integrated Development Environment (IDE):** A software application that consolidates the essential tools required for software development — including a **source code editor**, **compiler/interpreter**, **debugger**, and **build automation tools** — into a single graphical user interface, thereby streamlining the software development lifecycle.

In the context of **Java programming under the KTU 2024 Scheme**, the development environment is a **three-tier layered stack** consisting of the **JDK (Java Development Kit)**, the **JRE (Java Runtime Environment)**, and the **JVM (Java Virtual Machine)**. The IDE (such as **IntelliJ IDEA**, **Eclipse**, or **NetBeans**) sits on top of the JDK, providing a productive interface for writing, compiling, running, and debugging Java programs.

## 1.2 Core Components of the Java Development Environment

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU syllabus requires students to understand **each tier** of the Java environment independently before relying on an IDE.

| Tier | Component | Full Form | Primary Role |
|------|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| 1 (Top) | **JDK** | Java Development Kit | Used by *developers* to write and compile code |
| 2 (Middle) | **JRE** | Java Runtime Environment | Used by *end-users* to run compiled `.class` files |
| 3 (Bottom) | **JVM** | Java Virtual Machine | Executes **bytecode** and abstracts the OS/hardware |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Workshop" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine building a car:**
> - **JDK** = The *engineer's complete toolshed* (hammers, wrenches, drill press, design software). Without it, the engineer cannot *design* the car.
> - **JRE** = The *showroom* where customers come to drive the already-built car. They don't need the engineering tools.
> - **JVM** = The *engine* inside the car — the part that actually makes it move. It is the *executor* of all the engineering work.
> - **IDE** = The *engineer's workbench with a magnifying lamp, blueprint holder, and computer* — making the engineer faster, more accurate, and more comfortable.

This analogy directly mirrors the **WORA (Write Once, Run Anywhere)** philosophy of Java: the engineer (developer) designs the car in any workshop (OS), and the engine (JVM) ensures the car runs on any road (platform).

## 1.4 Why an IDE is Mandatory in Modern KTU Labs

> [!WARNING]
> KTU lab examinations (as per the **2024 Scheme regulations**) often require students to demonstrate proficiency in **at least one IDE** (commonly Eclipse or IntelliJ). Students relying solely on `notepad` + `javac` will lose marks in viva-voce and practical record evaluation.

An IDE provides:
- **Syntax Highlighting** — Color-coded keywords, literals, and identifiers
- **Intelligent Code Completion** (a.k.a. IntelliSense) — Reduces typing errors
- **Real-time Error Detection** — Underlines errors *before* compilation
- **Integrated Debugger** — Step-through execution with breakpoints
- **Refactoring Tools** — Safe rename, extract method, inline variable
- **Project Management** — Handles classpath, libraries (`.jar` files), and dependencies

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 The JDK–JRE–JVM Relationship (Layered Architecture)

The Java development environment follows a strict **containment hierarchy**:

$$\text{JDK} \supset \text{JRE} \supset \text{JVM}$$

**What does $\supset$ mean here?** It means *"contains"*. The JDK *contains* the JRE, and the JRE *contains* the JVM. Installing the JDK automatically gives you the JRE and JVM; installing only the JRE gives you the JVM but **not** the compiler (`javac`).

### 2.1.1 Inside the JDK

The **JDK (Java Development Kit)** ships with the following critical tools:

- **`javac`** — The Java compiler that converts `.java` source files into `.class` bytecode files
- **`java`** — The launcher that starts the JVM and loads your application
- **`javadoc`** — Generates HTML documentation from Java source comments
- **`jar`** — Bundles multiple `.class` files and resources into a single `.jar` archive
- **`jdb`** — The command-line Java debugger
- **`jshell`** — The interactive REPL (Read-Eval-Print Loop) introduced in **Java 9**

### 2.1.2 Inside the JRE

The **JRE (Java Runtime Environment)** provides the libraries and supporting files needed to *run* Java programs:

- **Class Libraries** (`rt.jar` / `jrt-fs.jar`) — The standard library (e.g., `java.lang`, `java.util`)
- **Property & Configuration Files** — `deployment.properties`, `java.security`
- **Supporting Native Libraries** (`.dll` on Windows, `.so` on Linux)

### 2.1.3 Inside the JVM

The **JVM (Java Virtual Machine)** is the abstract computing machine that:

1. **Loads** bytecode (`.class` files) via the ClassLoader subsystem
2. **Verifies** bytecode for safety (Bytecode Verifier)
3. **Executes** bytecode via the Execution Engine (Interpreter + JIT Compiler)
4. **Manages memory** through the Garbage Collector (Heap, Stack, Method Area)

> [!NOTE]
> **Platform Independence Mechanism:** Java source code → `javac` compiler → **Bytecode (`.class`)** → JVM (platform-specific) → Native OS execution. The bytecode is the *common intermediate language*; the JVM is what differs per platform.

## 2.2 The Two Critical Environment Variables

For the KTU lab exam, you **must** know how to configure these on your system:

| Variable | Purpose | Typical Value (Windows) |
|----------|---------|--------------------------|
| `JAVA_HOME` | Points to the JDK installation root | `C:\Program Files\Java\jdk-21` |
| `PATH` | Append the JDK `bin/` directory so OS finds `javac` and `java` globally | `C:\Program Files\Java\jdk-21\bin` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Lab Mistake:** Setting `JAVA_HOME` but forgetting to add `%JAVA_HOME%\bin` to `PATH`. Result: `javac` works *only* from the JDK `bin` folder, not from any directory. This loses **2-3 marks** in viva.

## 2.3 Comparative Study of Popular Java IDEs (KTU 2024 Recommended)

| Feature | **IntelliJ IDEA** (Community) | **Eclipse** (Adoptium) | **NetBeans** (Apache) | **VS Code** + Extension |
|---------|-------------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|--------------------------|
| Vendor | JetBrains | Eclipse Foundation | Apache Foundation | Microsoft |
| License | Free (Community Edition) | Free & Open Source | Free & Open Source | Free & Open Source |
| Smart Completion | Excellent (Industry-leading) | Good | Good | Basic |
| Built-in Debugger | Yes (Advanced) | Yes | Yes | Yes (via extension) |
| Git Integration | Built-in | EGit Plugin | Built-in | Built-in |
| KTU Lab Recommendation | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ |
| Startup Speed | Moderate | Slow | Moderate | Fast |
| Memory Footprint | High | Very High | Moderate | Low |

## 2.4 The Java Program Lifecycle (In-Depth)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a **favourite KTU question** — worth **3 to 7 marks** when asked in Part A or Part B.

The complete lifecycle of a Java program involves **three distinct stages**:

### Stage 1 — Source Code Creation
The developer writes a plain-text file ending in `.java` (e.g., `HelloWorld.java`). This file is human-readable.

### Stage 2 — Compilation
The command `javac HelloWorld.java` invokes the compiler, which:
- Performs **lexical analysis** (tokenization)
- Performs **syntax analysis** (parsing into an Abstract Syntax Tree)
- Performs **semantic analysis** (type checking, scope resolution)
- Generates **bytecode** in the file `HelloWorld.class`

### Stage 3 — Execution
The command `java HelloWorld` (note: *no* `.class` extension) launches the JVM, which:
- Loads `HelloWorld.class` via the **ClassLoader**
- Verifies the bytecode
- Uses the **JIT (Just-In-Time) compiler** to convert hot bytecode paths into native machine code
- Executes the `main` method

$$T_{\text{total}} = T_{\text{compile}} + T_{\text{load}} + T_{\text{verify}} + T_{\text{execute}}$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{compile}}$ = Time taken by `javac` (typically **sub-second** for small programs)
- $T_{\text{load}}$ = ClassLoader subsystem time
- $T_{\text{verify}}$ = Bytecode Verifier time
- $T_{\text{execute}}$ = JVM execution engine time

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The layered Java environment is the foundation of:
- **Enterprise Applications** — Banking, ERP systems (run on dedicated JREs)
- **Android Development** — Java/Kotlin runs on the **ART (Android Runtime)** — a JVM derivative
- **Big Data** — Apache Hadoop, Apache Spark run on the JVM
- **Embedded & IoT** — Java ME (Micro Edition) and **GraalVM Native Image**

# 3. Step-by-Step Setup Procedure & Analytical Walkthrough

## 3.1 Verifying an Existing Java Installation (Step-by-Step)

This is the **first step** every KTU student should perform in the lab before writing any code.

### Step 1 — Open a Terminal / Command Prompt
- **Windows:** Press `Win + R`, type `cmd`, press Enter
- **Linux/macOS:** Open the Terminal application

### Step 2 — Check the Compiler Version
Type the following command:

```bash
javac --version
```

**Expected Output (example):**
```text
javac 21.0.2
```

If you see `'javac' is not recognized as an internal or external command`, your **JDK is not installed** or your **PATH variable is misconfigured**.

### Step 3 — Check the Runtime Version
Type the following command:

```bash
java --version
```

**Expected Output (example):**
```text
openjdk 21.0.2 2024-01-16
OpenJDK Runtime Environment (build 21.0.2+9)
OpenJDK 64-Bit Server VM (build 21.0.2+9, mixed mode, sharing)
```

### Step 4 — Check the Location of the JVM
Type the following command:

```bash
where java
```
*(Use `which java` on Linux/macOS)*

**Expected Output:**
```text
C:\Program Files\Java\jdk-21\bin\java.exe
```

This confirms the OS is resolving `java` from the JDK installation, not an embedded JRE.

## 3.2 Installing the JDK (Detailed Procedure)

### Step 3.2.1 — Download the JDK
- Navigate to the **Adoptium (Eclipse Temurin)** or **Oracle JDK** download page
- Select **JDK 17 (LTS)** or **JDK 21 (LTS)** — both are KTU-compatible
- Choose the **x64** installer for your operating system

### Step 3.2.2 — Run the Installer
- Double-click the downloaded `.exe` (Windows) or `.dmg` (macOS)
- Accept the license agreement
- Note the **installation directory** (default: `C:\Program Files\Java\jdk-21`)
- Click **Next** → **Install** → **Finish**

### Step 3.2.3 — Configure the Environment Variables (Windows)

**Step A:** Open **System Properties** → **Advanced** → **Environment Variables**

**Step B:** Under **System Variables**, click **New**:
- Variable name: `JAVA_HOME`
- Variable value: `C:\Program Files\Java\jdk-21`

**Step C:** Find the `Path` variable in **System Variables**, click **Edit**, then **New**:
- Add entry: `%JAVA_HOME%\bin`

**Step D:** Click **OK** on all three dialog boxes to save

**Step E:** **Open a NEW terminal window** (existing terminals will not pick up the new variables) and re-run the verification commands in **Step 3.1**.

## 3.3 Writing, Compiling, and Running Your First Java Program (Manual Method)

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **mandatory** method every KTU student must master before moving to an IDE. Viva questions frequently test this.

### Step 3.3.1 — Create the Source File
Open any text editor (Notepad, nano, gedit) and type:

```java
public class HelloWorld {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Hello, KTU 2024!");
    }
}
```

**Critical Rule:** The filename **MUST exactly match** the `public class` name, including case. `HelloWorld.java` ≠ `helloworld.java`.

Save the file to a dedicated folder, e.g., `C:\KTU\JavaLab\Module1\`.

### Step 3.3.2 — Navigate to the Folder
```bash
cd C:\KTU\JavaLab\Module1
```

### Step 3.3.3 — Compile
```bash
javac HelloWorld.java
```

**Outcome on Success:** No output. A new file `HelloWorld.class` appears in the same directory.

**Outcome on Failure:** Error messages like `error: class, interface, or enum expected`. Read them carefully — they show the **line number** and **column** of the error.

### Step 3.3.4 — Run
```bash
java HelloWorld
```

**Expected Output:**
```text
Hello, KTU 2024!
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Typing `java HelloWorld.class` (with the extension) results in `Error: Could not find or load main class HelloWorld.class`. The `java` command expects the **class name**, not the file name.

## 3.4 Setting Up the IDE (IntelliJ IDEA — KTU Recommended)

### Step 3.4.1 — Download & Install IntelliJ IDEA Community Edition
- Visit the JetBrains website
- Download the **Community Edition** (free, open-source)
- Run the installer with default options

### Step 3.4.2 — Create a New Project
1. Launch IntelliJ IDEA
2. Click **New Project**
3. Set **Name**: `KTU_Java_Module1`
4. Set **Location**: `C:\KTU\JavaProjects\`
5. Set **Language**: `Java`
6. Set **Build System**: `IntelliJ` (default) or `Maven` (for advanced courses)
7. Set **JDK**: Click the dropdown → **Add JDK** → Select `C:\Program Files\Java\jdk-21`
8. Click **Create**

### Step 3.4.3 — Create a New Java Class
1. In the **Project** panel (left), right-click `src` → **New** → **Java Class**
2. Name it `HelloWorld`
3. Type the code from **Step 3.3.1**

### Step 3.4.4 — Run the Program
- Right-click anywhere in the editor → **Run 'HelloWorld.main()'**
- Or use the keyboard shortcut: `Shift + F10` (Windows) / `Ctrl + R` (macOS)
- View the output in the **Run** panel at the bottom

## 3.5 Setting Up the IDE (Eclipse — Alternative for KTU Labs)

1. Download **Eclipse IDE for Java Developers** from the Eclipse Foundation
2. Extract and run `eclipse.exe`
3. When prompted, set the **Workspace** folder: `C:\KTU\JavaWorkspace`
4. Go to **Window** → **Preferences** → **Java** → **Installed JREs** → **Add** → Select your JDK
5. **File** → **New** → **Java Project** → Name: `KTU_Java_Module1` → **Finish**
6. Right-click `src` → **New** → **Class** → Name: `HelloWorld` → Check `public static void main(String[] args)` → **Finish**
7. Press `Ctrl + F11` to run

## 3.6 Debugging Configuration (Essential for KTU Lab)

### 3.6.1 — Setting a Breakpoint
- In IntelliJ or Eclipse, click in the **gutter** (left margin) next to a line number
- A **red dot** appears — this is a breakpoint
- When you run in **Debug mode**, execution **pauses** at this line

### 3.6.2 — Debug Controls
- **Step Over (F8)** — Execute current line, move to next
- **Step Into (F7)** — Enter the called method
- **Step Out (Shift+F8)** — Exit the current method
- **Resume (F9)** — Continue execution until next breakpoint
- **Stop (Ctrl+F2)** — Terminate the debug session

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Mastering the debugger is worth **3-5 marks** in KTU lab viva. Examiners often ask: *"What is a breakpoint? How do you step through code?"*

## 3.7 Troubleshooting Quick-Reference Table

| Symptom | Root Cause | Fix |
|---------|-----------|-----|
| `'javac' is not recognized` | JDK not in `PATH` | Add `%JAVA_HOME%\bin` to `PATH` |
| `class has no main method` | Missing `public static void main(String[] args)` | Add the exact signature |
| `Error: Could not find or load main class` | Wrong directory or `classpath` issue | Run from folder containing `.class` file |
| `class X is public, must be declared in file named X.java` | Filename ≠ class name | Rename file to match class name |
| `illegal character: \u00bb` | Curly quotes from a Word/website paste | Re-type with plain `"` quotes |
| `java.lang.UnsupportedClassVersionError` | Source compiled with newer JDK, run with older JRE | Use matching JDK/JRE versions |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The JDK / JRE / JVM Containment Hierarchy

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the strict **layered containment** of the Java environment. Every higher tier *includes* everything below it.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph tier1["TIER 1 DEVELOPER LAYER"]
        jdkBox["JDK Java Development Kit\njavac, java, jar, javadoc, jshell, jdb"]
    end
    subgraph tier2["TIER 2 RUNTIME LAYER"]
        jreBox["JRE Java Runtime Environment\nClass Libraries, rt.jar, deployment.properties"]
    end
    subgraph tier3["TIER 3 EXECUTION LAYER"]
        jvmBox["JVM Java Virtual Machine\nClassLoader, Bytecode Verifier, JIT Compiler, GC"]
    end
    subgraph ide["OPTIONAL PRODUCTIVITY LAYER"]
        ideBox["IDE IntelliJ IDEA Eclipse NetBeans VS Code\nEditor, Debugger, Refactoring, Project Manager"]
    end
    jdkBox -->|contains| jreBox
    jreBox -->|contains| jvmBox
    ideBox -.->|calls| jdkBox
    jvmBox -->|executes| bytecode["BYTECODE HelloWorld.class"]
    bytecode -->|input to| jvmBox
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Arrows (`-->`) indicate direct *containment* or *flow*. Dashed arrows (`-.->`) indicate *optional calls* — the IDE invokes the JDK but is *not* part of the JDK.

## 4.2 Java Program Compilation and Execution Flow

This is the **single most important diagram** for KTU Module 1. Memorize its blocks in order.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    src["SOURCE FILE HelloWorld.java\nHuman readable text"]
    compiler["JAVAC COMPILER\njavac HelloWorld.java"]
    bytecode["BYTECODE FILE HelloWorld.class\nPlatform independent"]
    classloader["CLASS LOADER\nLoads .class into memory"]
    verifier["BYTECODE VERIFIER\nValidates safety and integrity"]
    exec["EXECUTION ENGINE\nInterpreter + JIT Compiler"]
    native["NATIVE OS OUTPUT\nTerminal shows Hello KTU 2024"]
    src -->|input| compiler
    compiler -->|produces| bytecode
    bytecode -->|loaded by| classloader
    classloader -->|passed to| verifier
    verifier -->|verified bytecode| exec
    exec -->|machine code| native
```

## 4.3 IDE Internal Architecture Block Diagram

For students who may be asked *"What are the components of an IDE?"* in viva, here is the conceptual block diagram:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ideCore["IDE CORE ARCHITECTURE"]
        editor["SOURCE CODE EDITOR\nSyntax highlighting, autocomplete, code folding"]
        compilerIde["BUILDER COMPILER INTEGRATION\nInvokes javac in background"]
        debugger["DEBUGGER\nBreakpoints, step controls, variable inspection"]
        refactor["REFACTORING ENGINE\nRename, extract method, inline"]
        vcs["VERSION CONTROL\nGit integration, diff viewer, commit history"]
        runner["TEST RUNNER\nJUnit integration, test reports"]
    end
    editor <-->|continuous feedback| compilerIde
    compilerIde -->|errors| editor
    editor -->|controlled execution| debugger
    debugger -->|runtime data| editor
    editor -->|refactor request| refactor
    refactor -->|updated source| editor
    editor -->|commits| vcs
    editor -->|runs JUnit| runner
    runner -->|test results| editor
```

## 4.4 Environment Variable Configuration Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    install["INSTALL JDK\nto C:Program FilesJavajdk-21"]
    javaHome["SET JAVA_HOME\nC:Program FilesJavajdk-21"]
    pathEdit["EDIT PATH\nAdd %JAVA_HOME%bin"]
    newTerm["OPEN NEW TERMINAL\nto refresh variables"]

    javaHome --> pathEdit
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam Pattern:** Part A consists of 5 questions of 3 marks each. The questions below are the **two most likely** variations for this topic.

---

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]`

**(3 Marks) Differentiate between JDK, JRE, and JVM. Which one is mandatory for a developer to install?**

**Model Answer (Step-by-Step):**

| Aspect | JDK | JRE | JVM |
|--------|-----|-----|-----|
| Full Form | Java Development Kit | Java Runtime Environment | Java Virtual Machine |
| Purpose | Develop & compile Java code | Run compiled Java programs | Execute bytecode |
| Contains | JRE + dev tools (`javac`, `jar`) | JVM + class libraries | Just the execution engine |
| Who needs it? | Developers | End-users | Embedded in JRE |
| Platform-dependent? | No (installer is platform-specific, but tools are not) | No | **Yes** (separate JVM for Windows, Linux, macOS) |

**Conclusion:** A developer **must install the JDK**, because it includes both the JRE (needed to run programs) and the compiler `javac` (needed to create `.class` files). Installing only the JRE allows running programs but not compiling them.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper 2]`

**(3 Marks) What is an IDE? List any four features of IntelliJ IDEA that enhance Java development productivity.**

**Model Answer:**

An **IDE (Integrated Development Environment)** is a software application that combines a code editor, compiler, debugger, and other development tools into a single unified interface.

**Four Key Features of IntelliJ IDEA:**

1. **Smart Code Completion** — Context-aware suggestions that complete variable names, method calls, and even entire code blocks (`Ctrl + Space`).
2. **On-the-fly Code Analysis** — Detects errors, unused declarations, and performance issues *before* compilation (red/yellow underlines).
3. **Powerful Refactoring** — Allows safe rename of classes, methods, and variables across the entire project (`Shift + F6`).
4. **Built-in Version Control** — Native Git integration showing diffs, branches, and history directly in the IDE.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B (full internal choice).

---

### ❓ Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Main Question Pattern]`

**(14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the architecture of the Java Development Environment with a neat diagram. Differentiate between the roles of `javac` and `java` commands.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a step-by-step procedure to install JDK 17 on a Windows system. How do you configure the `PATH` and `JAVA_HOME` environment variables? Why are both required?

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question A (a)

**Java Development Environment Architecture (7 Marks):**

The Java Development Environment follows a **three-tier architecture**:

**Tier 1 — JDK (Java Development Kit):**
- The topmost layer containing development tools
- Includes `javac` (compiler), `java` (launcher), `jar` (archiver), `javadoc` (doc generator), `jdb` (debugger), `jshell` (REPL)
- Used by developers to write, compile, and package Java code

**Tier 2 — JRE (Java Runtime Environment):**
- Contains the standard class libraries (`java.lang.*`, `java.util.*`, etc.)
- Contains the supporting files required to run a Java application
- Used by end-users who only want to *run* pre-compiled programs

**Tier 3 — JVM (Java Virtual Machine):**
- The actual execution engine
- Has its own stack, heap, and garbage collector
- Is **platform-dependent** — a different JVM exists for Windows, Linux, macOS

**Containment Relationship:**

$$\text{JDK} \supset \text{JRE} \supset \text{JVM}$$

**Diagram (To be drawn in exam — reproduce this):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph a1["DEVELOPER LAYER"]
        aJdk["JDK\njavac, java, jar, jshell, jdb"]
    end
    subgraph a2["RUNTIME LAYER"]
        aJre["JRE\nClass Libraries, rt.jar, Properties"]
    end
    subgraph a3["EXECUTION LAYER"]
        aJvm["JVM\nClassLoader, Verifier, JIT, GC"]
    end
    aJdk -->|includes| aJre
    aJre -->|includes| aJvm
    aJvm -->|executes| aByte["BYTECODE .class files"]
```

**Difference between `javac` and `java`:**

| Command | Tool | Input | Output | Phase |
|---------|------|-------|--------|-------|
| `javac HelloWorld.java` | Java Compiler | Source `.java` file | Bytecode `.class` file | Compile-time |
| `java HelloWorld` | JVM Launcher | Class name (no extension) | Program execution | Run-time |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write `java HelloWorld.class` and get `ClassNotFoundException`. The `java` command takes the **class name**, not the file name. Examiners specifically check this — losing **1-2 marks**.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question A (b)

**Step-by-Step JDK 17 Installation on Windows (7 Marks):**

**Step 1: Download the Installer**
- Visit the official Adoptium (Eclipse Temurin) website or Oracle JDK download page
- Select **JDK 17 (LTS)**, **x64 Installer** for Windows
- Save the `.exe` file to your Downloads folder

**Step 2: Run the Installer**
- Double-click the downloaded `.exe`
- Click **Next** on the welcome screen
- Note the default installation path: `C:\Program Files\Eclipse Adoptium\jdk-17.0.10.7-hotspot\`
- Click **Next** → **Install** (provide admin credentials if prompted) → **Finish**

**Step 3: Configure `JAVA_HOME`**
- Right-click **This PC** → **Properties** → **Advanced system settings**
- Click **Environment Variables**
- Under **System variables**, click **New**:
  - **Variable name:** `JAVA_HOME`
  - **Variable value:** `C:\Program Files\Eclipse Adoptium\jdk-17.0.10.7-hotspot\`
- Click **OK**

**Step 4: Configure `PATH`**
- In the same **Environment Variables** window, find `Path` in System variables
- Click **Edit** → **New**
- Type: `%JAVA_HOME%\bin`
- Click **OK** → **OK** → **OK**

**Step 5: Verify the Installation**
- Open a **NEW** Command Prompt (existing windows do not see the new variables)
- Type: `javac --version` → Should display `javac 17.0.10`
- Type: `java --version` → Should display `openjdk 17.0.10`

**Why Both `PATH` and `JAVA_HOME` are Required:**

- **`JAVA_HOME`** is used by **Java-aware applications** (Maven, Gradle, Tomcat, Eclipse, IntelliJ) to locate the JDK root. It is a *convention* not a *system requirement*.
- **`PATH`** is used by the **Operating System** to find executables like `javac` and `java` from any directory in the terminal.

If you only set `PATH` (hard-coding the full path), then any third-party tool that looks for `JAVA_HOME` will fail. If you only set `JAVA_HOME`, your terminal still cannot find `javac` from arbitrary folders. **Both are required for a complete, robust setup.**

---

### ❓ Question B `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Alternative Pattern]`

**(14 Marks)**

**(a)** What is an Integrated Development Environment (IDE)? Compare any three popular Java IDEs (IntelliJ IDEA, Eclipse, NetBeans) based on at least six parameters.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the complete lifecycle of a Java program — from writing the source code to seeing the output — with a neat block diagram. Explain the role of the JIT compiler.

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question B (a)

**Definition (1 Mark):**

An **IDE (Integrated Development Environment)** is a software suite that combines a source code editor, build automation tools (compiler/interpreter), and a debugger into a single application. It dramatically improves developer productivity by eliminating the need to switch between separate tools.

**Comparative Table of Three Java IDEs (6 Marks):**

| Parameter | **IntelliJ IDEA (Community)** | **Eclipse** | **NetBeans** |
|-----------|-------------------------------|-------------|--------------|
| Vendor | JetBrains | Eclipse Foundation | Apache Foundation |
| License | Free (Community Edition) | Free & Open Source | Free & Open Source |
| Smart Completion | Best-in-class, AI-assisted | Good (with plugins) | Good |
| Memory Footprint | High (4 GB+ recommended) | Very High (2-4 GB) | Moderate (1-2 GB) |
| Startup Time | Moderate (~10 sec) | Slow (~20-30 sec) | Moderate (~15 sec) |
| Build System | IntelliJ / Maven / Gradle | Eclipse / Maven | Ant / Maven / Gradle |
| Git Integration | Built-in, excellent | EGit plugin | Built-in |
| KTU Recommendation | Best for beginners & industry | Best for legacy projects | Best for quick prototypes |

**Conclusion:** For KTU 2024 lab examinations, **IntelliJ IDEA Community Edition** is the most recommended choice due to its intelligent code completion, modern UI, and industry relevance. **Eclipse** remains a strong alternative, especially for students continuing to advanced Java/J2EE courses.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for Question B (b)

**Java Program Lifecycle (7 Marks):**

**Phase 1 — Source Code Creation**
- The developer writes a `.java` file (e.g., `HelloWorld.java`) using an editor or IDE
- This file is **human-readable** and contains valid Java syntax

**Phase 2 — Compilation (`javac` phase)**
- The command `javac HelloWorld.java` is invoked
- The compiler performs:
  1. **Lexical Analysis** — Breaks source into tokens (keywords, identifiers, operators)
  2. **Syntax Analysis** — Builds an Abstract Syntax Tree (AST)
  3. **Semantic Analysis** — Type checking, scope resolution
  4. **Code Generation** — Produces `HelloWorld.class` containing **bytecode**

**Phase 3 — Loading (`java` phase, ClassLoader subsystem)**
- The command `java HelloWorld` launches the JVM
- The **Bootstrap ClassLoader** loads core Java APIs
- The **Platform ClassLoader** loads extension libraries
- The **Application ClassLoader** loads `HelloWorld.class` from the current directory

**Phase 4 — Verification (Bytecode Verifier)**
- Checks for:
  - Valid bytecode structure
  - No illegal data type conversions
  - No stack overflow / underflow
  - No illegal access to private methods

**Phase 5 — Execution (Execution Engine)**
- The **Interpreter** reads bytecode line-by-line and executes it
- The **JIT (Just-In-Time) Compiler** identifies "hot" methods (frequently executed code) and compiles them into **native machine code** for the underlying CPU
- The compiled native code is cached, so subsequent calls are **much faster**

**Role of the JIT Compiler (2 Marks):**
The JIT bridges the performance gap between interpreted bytecode and compiled native code. Without JIT, Java would be significantly slower than C++. With JIT, Java achieves near-native performance for long-running applications (servers, daemons), while retaining full platform independence.

**Block Diagram (To be drawn in exam):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    b1["SOURCE HelloWorld.java"] -->|javac| b2["BYTECODE HelloWorld.class"]
    b2 -->|ClassLoader| b3["MEMORY Method Area Heap"]
    b3 -->|Verifier| b4["VERIFIED BYTECODE"]
    b4 -->|Interpreter| b5["SLOW FIRST RUN"]
    b5 -->|JIT compiles hot code| b6["NATIVE MACHINE CODE"]
    b6 -->|Fast subsequent runs| b7["OUTPUT TO CONSOLE"]
```

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Areas where students consistently lose marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the `static` keyword in `main`** — `public void main(String[] args)` is *not* a valid entry point. Causes `Main method not found` error. (Lose **2 marks**)
> 2. **Wrong filename** — `public class HelloWorld` must be saved as `HelloWorld.java`, not `hello.java`. Compiler will reject it. (Lose **2 marks**)
> 3. **Running `java HelloWorld.class`** — The `java` command takes the *class name*, not the file name. (Lose **1-2 marks**)
> 4. **Confusing `Path` and `PATH` (Windows vs Java)** — `PATH` is the OS environment variable. The Java classpath is set with `-cp` or `CLASSPATH` (an entirely different concept introduced later).
> 5. **Drawing the JDK–JRE–JVM diagram incorrectly** — Many students place JRE and JVM *side-by-side* or draw JVM as a subset of JDK but JRE as separate. The correct relationship is **strict containment**: JDK $\supset$ JRE $\supset$ JVM. (Lose **2-3 marks**)
> 6. **Not opening a new terminal after setting environment variables** — Demonstrated in labs; examiners may deduct **1 mark** in viva.

---

## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The Java environment has **three tiers**: **JDK $\supset$ JRE $\supset$ JVM**
- ✅ **JDK** is for developers; **JRE** is for end-users; **JVM** is the actual executor
- ✅ `javac` compiles `.java` → `.class`; `java` runs the `.class` file
- ✅ The `main` method signature is **exactly**: `public static void main(String[] args)`
- ✅ The `public class` name **must match the filename** (case-sensitive)
- ✅ Two environment variables are essential: **`JAVA_HOME`** and **`PATH`** (with `%JAVA_HOME%\bin`)
- ✅ Popular IDEs: **IntelliJ IDEA**, **Eclipse**, **NetBeans**, **VS Code**
- ✅ IDE features: syntax highlighting, code completion, debugging, refactoring, version control
- ✅ The Java lifecycle: **Source → Compile → Load → Verify → Execute**
- ✅ The **JIT compiler** converts hot bytecode into native code for performance
- ✅ The **WORA principle** (Write Once, Run Anywhere) is achieved via bytecode + platform-specific JVM
- ✅ **Always open a new terminal** after changing environment variables
- ✅ Debugger shortcuts: **F8** (Step Over), **F7** (Step Into), **F9** (Resume), **Shift+F8** (Step Out)
- ✅ Java 17 and Java 21 are both **LTS (Long-Term Support)** versions and acceptable for KTU 2024 labs
- ✅ `java` command takes the **class name**; **never** include the `.class` extension

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Writing and Running a Simple Java Program
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT611 - OBJECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING IN JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Writing and Running a Simple Java Program

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Java program** is a structured set of instructions written in the Java programming language that gets compiled into **bytecode** (an intermediate, platform-independent representation) and executed by the **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)**. The JVM, in turn, runs on top of a host operating system, enabling the famous *"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)* paradigm.

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus, a *simple Java program* refers to a **single-file, single-class, console-based application** that:
1. Contains one `public class` declaration.
2. Includes a `main()` method as the program entry point.
3. Uses standard output (`System.out.println()`) to display results.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 emphasizes the *mechanics* of writing, compiling, and running Java. You are not yet expected to master OOP concepts here — only the *toolchain lifecycle* and the *anatomy of a minimal Java source file*.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🧠

Think of a **Java program** like a **recipe for a dish**:

| Cooking Concept | Java Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Recipe written on paper | `.java` source file (human-readable) |
| Recipe translated to chef's shorthand | `.class` bytecode (compiled form) |
| The chef who actually cooks | **JVM** (Java Virtual Machine) |
| The kitchen (stove, oven, utensils) | Operating System + Hardware |
| A standardized recipe format | Java Language Specification |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why so many layers?** If you write a recipe in English, an Indian chef and a French chef can both follow it — *provided they both understand English*. Similarly, Java bytecode is the "universal language" that any JVM (Windows JVM, Linux JVM, Mac JVM) understands. This is what makes Java **platform-independent**.

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold-Defined for KTU Recall)

- **Source Code** → The human-readable `.java` file written by the programmer.
- **Compiler (`javac`)** → Translates source code into bytecode.
- **Bytecode** → A platform-neutral intermediate code stored in `.class` files.
- **JVM (Java Virtual Machine)** → The runtime engine that interprets/executes bytecode.
- **JRE (Java Runtime Environment)** → JVM + standard libraries (needed to *run* programs).
- **JDK (Java Development Kit)** → JRE + compiler + debugging tools (needed to *develop* programs).
- **Class** → The fundamental building block (blueprint) in Java.
- **Main Method** → The mandatory entry point where program execution begins.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU Viva:** *JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM*. The **JDK contains** the JRE, which in turn **contains** the JVM. Think of it as **boxes inside boxes** — a Russian doll analogy.

---

## 1.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Java is the backbone of:
- **Android mobile applications** (Kotlin/Java).
- **Enterprise backend systems** (banking, e-commerce — Spring Boot).
- **Embedded systems & smart cards** (Java Card).
- **Big Data frameworks** (Hadoop, Kafka, Elasticsearch).
- **Scientific computing** (NASA WorldWind, JMOL for molecular visualization).

Understanding how to write and run a simple program is the **gateway skill** to all of these domains.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Simple Java Program — Stepwise Breakdown

A minimal Java program has **four mandatory structural elements**. Let us dissect each one:

### 🔹 Step 1: The Class Declaration
```java
public class HelloWorld {
```
- Every Java program lives inside a **class**.
- The keyword `public` means the class is accessible from anywhere.
- The class name **must exactly match** the filename → file is `HelloWorld.java`.
- The opening brace `{` begins the class body.

### 🔹 Step 2: The `main()` Method (Entry Point)
```java
public static void main(String[] args) {
```
- **`public`** → JVM can call it from outside the class.
- **`static`** → It can be invoked *without* creating an object of the class.
- **`void`** → It returns nothing.
- **`main`** → Reserved name; JVM looks for *exactly* this signature.
- **`String[] args`** → Accepts command-line arguments as a string array.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Insight:** A common mistake is writing `void main()` without `static` or `String[] args`. The program **will compile** but **will not run** — JVM throws `MainMethodNotFoundException` (or in modern JDKs, the program just exits with an error).

### 🔹 Step 3: The Statement
```java
    System.out.println("Hello, World!");
```
- `System` → A built-in class in `java.lang` package.
- `out` → A static object of type `PrintStream` representing standard output (console).
- `println()` → A method that prints the argument followed by a newline.

### 🔹 Step 4: Closing Braces
```java
    } // end of main
}     // end of class
```
- Every opening brace `{` must have a matching closing brace `}`.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Reference Sheet (Cheat Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize this table — it answers 70% of Module 1 viva/practical questions.

| **Element** | **Mandatory?** | **Purpose** | **Common Mistake (Marks Lost)** |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| `public class ClassName` | ✅ Yes | Blueprint declaration | Filename mismatch |
| `class` keyword | ✅ Yes | Defines a class | Forgetting the keyword |
| `public static void main(String[] args)` | ✅ Yes | Entry point signature | Missing `static` or `String[] args` |
| `System.out.println()` | ❌ No (for compilation) | Output to console | Using lowercase `system` (error) |
| `import` statement | ❌ No (for simple program) | Import external packages | Unnecessary imports |
| `package` statement | ❌ No (defaults to unnamed) | Logical grouping | Wrong package folder structure |
| Semicolons `;` | ✅ Yes | Statement terminator | Missing at line end |
| Braces `{}` | ✅ Yes | Block delimiters | Mismatched pairs |

---

## 2.3 The Java Program Lifecycle (Compile → Run)

```
┌─────────────────┐    javac     ┌──────────────┐    java     ┌────────────┐
│  HelloWorld.java│ ───────────▶ │ HelloWorld   │ ──────────▶ │   Output   │
│  (Source code)  │              │ .class       │             │  on screen │
└─────────────────┘              │  (Bytecode)  │             └────────────┘
                                 └──────────────┘                  ▲
                                                                  │
                                                              JVM executes
```

### Two Key Tools:
1. **`javac` (Java Compiler)** → Source `.java` → Bytecode `.class`
2. **`java` (Java Launcher)** → Loads `.class` into JVM and starts execution

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Practical Exam Tip:** The examiner often asks: *"What is the difference between `javac` and `java`?"* Answer: `javac` is the *compiler* (creates `.class`), `java` is the *launcher* (starts JVM to execute the `.class`).

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of the Lifecycle

The compile-then-run lifecycle is used in:
- **CI/CD pipelines** (Jenkins, GitHub Actions compile Java before deployment).
- **Microservices architecture** (each service compiled separately).
- **Android APK generation** (Java/Kotlin → DEX bytecode for Dalvik/ART VM).
- **Embedded JVMs** (smart cards, IoT devices use the same lifecycle but with stripped JVMs).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Walkthrough & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Complete "Hello, World!" Program — Annotated

```java
// File: HelloWorld.java
// Purpose: Demonstrates the structure of a minimal Java program

public class HelloWorld {                          // ← Line 1: Class declaration

    public static void main(String[] args) {       // ← Line 2: Main method signature
        System.out.println("Hello, World!");       // ← Line 3: Output statement
    }                                             // ← Line 4: End of main

}                                                  // ← Line 5: End of class
```

### Exhaustive Line-by-Line Explanation

| **Line** | **Code** | **What Happens (Logical Conversion)** |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | `// File: HelloWorld.java` | Single-line comment, ignored by compiler. |
| 2 | `public class HelloWorld {` | Declares a publicly accessible class named `HelloWorld`. Opens class body. |
| 3 | `public static void main(String[] args) {` | Declares the JVM entry point. `static` allows invocation without objects. `void` means no return value. `String[] args` accepts command-line arguments. |
| 4 | `System.out.println("Hello, World!");` | Accesses the static `out` field of the `System` class, calls its `println()` method with the string literal, and terminates with a newline. |
| 5 | `}` | Closes the `main` method body. |
| 6 | `}` | Closes the `HelloWorld` class body. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Strict rule:** Java is **case-sensitive**. `System` and `system` are two different identifiers. Forgetting this loses you marks in the KTU practical exam.

---

## 3.2 Compilation & Execution — Exhaustive Terminal Walkthrough

### Step-by-step on a Linux/Windows/Mac terminal:

**Step 1:** Open a text editor (Notepad, VS Code, Sublime, vim) and save the file as:
```
HelloWorld.java
```

**Step 2:** Open a terminal/command prompt and navigate to the folder containing the file.

**Step 3:** Compile the program:
```bash
javac HelloWorld.java
```
- This produces a new file: `HelloWorld.class` (the bytecode).
- If there are syntax errors, the compiler prints error messages with line numbers.

**Step 4:** Run the program:
```bash
java HelloWorld
```
- **Important:** Do **NOT** type `java HelloWorld.class` — just the class name.
- The JVM loads the bytecode, verifies it, and invokes `main()`.

**Step 5:** Observe the output:
```
Hello, World!
```

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Command-Line Argument Version

A more advanced variant accepts **command-line arguments** via `args[]`:

```java
public class GreetUser {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.println("Hello, " + args[0] + "!");
    }
}
```

### Exhaustive Logic Flow:

$$ \text{Output} = \text{"Hello, "} \; \Vert \; args[0] \; \Vert \; \text{"!"} $$

Where:
- $\text{args[0]}$ is the first command-line argument (a `String`).
- The `+` operator performs **string concatenation** when one operand is a `String`.
- The `||` symbol here represents the textual concatenation operation (NOT mathematical OR).

### Execution:
```bash
javac GreetUser.java
java GreetUser Kerala
```
**Output:**
```
Hello, Kerala!
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** If you forget to pass an argument, `args[0]` does not exist, and the program crashes with `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`. KTU examiners love to test this edge case.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Common Errors & Fixes

| **Error Message** | **Root Cause** | **Fix** |
|:---|:---|:---|
| `class, interface, or enum expected` | Mismatched braces | Count `{` and `}` — they must match. |
| `cannot find symbol` | Typo in identifier (e.g., `prntln`) | Check spelling against Java API. |
| `incompatible types` | Assigning wrong data type | Ensure types match (e.g., `int` to `int`). |
| `public class X must be defined in a file named X.java` | Filename ≠ class name | Rename file to match `public class` name. |
| `Error: Could not find or load main class` | Wrong class name at runtime OR `classpath` issue | Use exact class name, no `.class` extension. |
| `Exception in thread "main" java.lang.NoSuchMethodError: main` | Missing/wrong `main` signature | Ensure `public static void main(String[] args)`. |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📕 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Java Program Execution Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Programmer writes source code in HelloWorld.java] --> B[Save file with .java extension]
    B --> C[Open terminal and run javac HelloWorld.java]
    C --> D{Syntax correct?}
    D -- No --> E[Compiler prints errors with line numbers]
    E --> A
    D -- Yes --> F[Bytecode generated in HelloWorld.class]
    F --> G[Run command: java HelloWorld]
    G --> H[ClassLoader loads .class into JVM memory]
    H --> I[Bytecode Verifier validates code]
    I --> J[JVM invokes main method]
    J --> K[System.out.println executes]
    K --> L[Output: Hello, World! appears on console]
    L --> M[main returns and JVM shuts down]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Follow the arrows from `A` (source writing) to `M` (program termination). The decision diamond `D` represents the compiler's syntax check — a loop-back occurs on errors.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Anatomy of a Java Source File

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SRC[HelloWorld.java Source File]
        direction TB
        P1[Optional: package declaration] --> P2[Optional: import statements]
        P2 --> P3[Required: public class ClassName]
        P3 --> P4[Required: public static void main String args]
        P4 --> P5[Required: System.out.println statement]
        P5 --> P6[Required: closing braces for main and class]
    end
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — JDK / JRE / JVM Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    JDK[JDK - Java Development Kit] --> JRE[JRE - Java Runtime Environment]
    JRE --> JVM[JVM - Java Virtual Machine]
    JDK --> COMP[javac compiler]
    JDK --> DEBUG[jdb debugger]
    JRE --> LIB[rt.jar / Standard Libraries]
    JVM --> EXEC[Interpreter + JIT Compiler]
```

**Interpretation of the hierarchy:**

$$ \text{JDK} = \text{JRE} \cup \{\text{javac}, \text{jdb}, \text{jar}, \text{javadoc}, \ldots\} $$

$$ \text{JRE} = \text{JVM} \cup \{\text{standard libraries}, \text{classloader}, \ldots\} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Question:** *"I want to only run a Java application on my machine. Do I need JDK or JRE?"* → **JRE is sufficient.** You only need the full JDK if you intend to *develop* (compile) Java code.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📒 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Explain the role of the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) in achieving platform independence. List two other components of the JDK.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Java Virtual Machine (JVM)** is the runtime engine that executes Java bytecode. It acts as an **abstract layer** between the compiled program and the underlying operating system. When a `.java` file is compiled using `javac`, the output is a `.class` file containing **bytecode** — a platform-neutral instruction set. The JVM interprets this bytecode and translates it into native machine instructions specific to the host OS (Windows, Linux, macOS). Hence, the same bytecode can run on any platform that has a compatible JVM installed, achieving Java's **"Write Once, Run Anywhere" (WORA)** property.

Two other components of the JDK are:
1. **`javac`** — The Java compiler that converts source code into bytecode.
2. **`jdb`** — The Java Debugger used to step through and troubleshoot programs.

*(Examiner's note: Mentioning WORA explicitly earns a full 3 marks. Generic answers like "JVM runs the program" score only 1 mark.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**Differentiate between `javac` and `java` commands. What happens if you type `java HelloWorld.class` instead of `java HelloWorld`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | `javac` | `java` |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Type | Java compiler | Java application launcher |
| Input | `HelloWorld.java` (source) | `HelloWorld` (class name) |
| Output | `HelloWorld.class` (bytecode) | Program execution output |
| When used | Development time | Runtime |
| Invokes | Compiler internals | JVM |

If you type `java HelloWorld.class`, the launcher will search for a class named **`HelloWorld.class`** (literally, including the dot), fail to find it, and throw:
```
Error: Could not find or load main class HelloWorld.class
```
The correct usage is `java HelloWorld` (no extension), because `java` automatically appends `.class` when looking for bytecode.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE pattern**, Part B questions have an **internal choice**. Attempt **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Write a Java program that accepts **two numbers** as command-line arguments and prints their **sum, difference, and product**. Explain the **structure** of the program by labeling every component (class, main method, statements). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **complete lifecycle** of a Java program from source code creation to execution. Include the role of `javac`, JVM, classloader, and bytecode verifier. Use a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for (a):

```java
public class Calculator {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int a = Integer.parseInt(args[0]);
        int b = Integer.parseInt(args[1]);
        
        int sum = a + b;
        int diff = a - b;
        int prod = a * b;
        
        System.out.println("Sum = " + sum);
        System.out.println("Difference = " + diff);
        System.out.println("Product = " + prod);
    }
}
```

**Component Labeling (Step-by-step):**

1. **`public class Calculator`** — Declares the class. Filename must be `Calculator.java`.
2. **`public static void main(String[] args)`** — Entry point signature.
   - `public` → accessible to JVM.
   - `static` → callable without object instantiation.
   - `void` → returns no value.
   - `String[] args` → array to hold command-line arguments.
3. **`Integer.parseInt(args[0])`** — Converts the first string argument (e.g., `"10"`) to an integer (`10`).
4. **Arithmetic operations** — `+`, `-`, `*` performed on integers.
5. **`System.out.println(...)`** — Prints results to standard output.
6. **Closing braces** `}` — Two braces close `main` and `Calculator` respectively.

**Execution:**
```bash
javac Calculator.java
java Calculator 15 7
```
**Output:**
```
Sum = 22
Difference = 8
Product = 105
```

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for (b):

The Java program lifecycle has **four major stages**:

**Stage 1 — Source Code Creation:**
The programmer writes `Calculator.java` using a text editor. The file is plain ASCII/UTF-8 text containing Java syntax.

**Stage 2 — Compilation:**
The command `javac Calculator.java` invokes the Java compiler. The compiler:
- Performs **lexical analysis** (tokenizing the source).
- Performs **syntax analysis** (building a parse tree).
- Performs **semantic analysis** (type checking, scope resolution).
- Generates **bytecode** → `Calculator.class`.

**Stage 3 — Class Loading:**
When you run `java Calculator`, the **ClassLoader subsystem** locates the `.class` file, reads its bytecode, and loads it into JVM memory (specifically, the *Method Area* in JVM's runtime data areas).

**Stage 4 — Bytecode Verification & Execution:**
