The **Bytecode Verifier** checks the code for illegal operations (e.g., stack overflow, type mismatches). Then the **Execution Engine** (Interpreter + JIT compiler) translates bytecode into native machine code line-by-line (interpreted) or compiles hot paths to native code (JIT — Just-In-Time compilation).

**Stage 5 — Output & Termination:**
The `println` statements produce console output. When `main()` returns, the JVM shuts down.

**Diagram (already covered in Section 4.1):**
The flowchart shows: `Source → javac → Bytecode → ClassLoader → Verifier → Interpreter/JIT → Output`.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **differences between JDK, JRE, and JVM** using a neat diagram. Why does a developer need JDK while an end-user only needs JRE? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that accepts a **name and age** as command-line arguments and prints:
> `Hello <name>, you will be <age+10> years old in 10 years.`

Explain what happens if no arguments are passed. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for (a):

**Definitions:**

- **JVM (Java Virtual Machine):** An abstract computing machine (runtime engine) that executes Java bytecode. It is platform-dependent (different JVMs for Windows, Linux, Mac) but the bytecode it runs is platform-independent.
- **JRE (Java Runtime Environment):** Provides the libraries, JVM, and other components needed to *run* Java applications. It does **not** contain development tools like compilers/debuggers.
- **JDK (Java Development Kit):** A superset of JRE. It includes the JRE plus development tools like `javac`, `jdb`, `jar`, `javadoc`, etc.

**Diagram (Refer to Section 4.3 — JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM):**

$$ \text{JDK} = \text{JRE} \cup \{\text{Development Tools}\} $$

$$ \text{JRE} = \text{JVM} \cup \{\text{Standard Libraries, ClassLoader}\} $$

**Why developer needs JDK, end-user needs JRE:**
- A **developer** must *compile* and *debug* code → requires `javac`, `jdb` → needs **JDK**.
- An **end-user** only wants to *run* pre-compiled applications (`.class` / `.jar` files) → only needs **JRE**.

**Real-world analogy:** A *chef* needs raw ingredients + stove + recipes (JDK). A *diner* only needs the cooked dish served on a plate (JRE).

---

#### 📝 Model Solution for (b):

```java
public class AgePredictor {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        if (args.length < 2) {
            System.out.println("Error: Please provide both name and age as arguments.");
            return;
        }
        
        String name = args[0];
        int age = Integer.parseInt(args[1]);
        int futureAge = age + 10;
        
        System.out.println("Hello " + name + ", you will be " + futureAge + " years old in 10 years.");
    }
}
```

**Execution:**
```bash
javac AgePredictor.java
java AgePredictor Anu 20
```
**Output:**
```
Hello Anu, you will be 30 years old in 10 years.
```

**What happens if no arguments are passed?**

- `args.length` equals `0`.
- The `if` condition `args.length < 2` is **true**.
- The error message prints, and `return;` exits the `main` method early, preventing a crash.

**If we had skipped the check:**
- `args[0]` access → `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`.
- `Integer.parseInt(args[1])` → same exception.
- The JVM prints a stack trace and terminates abnormally.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - ❌ **Losing 2 marks:** Writing `void main()` without `static` or `String[] args`. The signature must be **exactly** `public static void main(String[] args)`.
> - ❌ **Losing 1 mark:** Mismatch between class name (`HelloWorld`) and filename (`hello.java`). Java is case-sensitive and strict.
> - ❌ **Losing 1 mark:** Forgetting the `;` at the end of `println` statement — compilation error.
> - ❌ **Losing 2 marks:** Writing `java HelloWorld.class` in execution steps. Never include the `.class` extension in the `java` command.
> - ❌ **Losing 1 mark:** Using `print` instead of `println` and not mentioning what the difference is when asked.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🚀

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and pin to your study wall!**

- ✅ A Java program **must** be inside a `class`.
- ✅ The `public class` name **must match** the filename (case-sensitive).
- ✅ `main()` signature is **fixed**: `public static void main(String[] args)`.
- ✅ Compilation uses **`javac filename.java`** → produces `.class`.
- ✅ Execution uses **`java ClassName`** → launches JVM (no `.class` extension).
- ✅ Java is **case-sensitive**: `System` ≠ `system`.
- ✅ Every statement ends with **`;`**.
- ✅ Braces `{}` must be **balanced**.
- ✅ `System.out.println()` prints to **console**; `print()` does not add newline.
- ✅ `String[] args` holds **command-line arguments** as strings.
- ✅ `Integer.parseInt()` converts a **String → int**.
- ✅ `+` operator on Strings performs **concatenation**.
- ✅ **JDK ⊃ JRE ⊃ JVM** (Russian doll hierarchy).
- ✅ **WORA** = Write Once, Run Anywhere (achieved via bytecode + JVM).
- ✅ Java uses **two-stage execution**: compile (javac) → run (java → JVM).
- ✅ **Common error:** `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` when accessing non-existent `args[i]`.
- ✅ **Common error:** `Could not find or load main class` when class name is mistyped.
- ✅ Always handle **missing command-line arguments** with `args.length` check.

> 🎯 **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** For Module 1 questions, always draw the **JDK/JRE/JVM diagram** and the **lifecycle flowchart** if a diagram is requested — it almost guarantees full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Java: Variables, Data Types, and Operators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT611 - OBJECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING IN JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Java: Variables, Data Types, and Operators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Variables — Definition

A **variable** in Java is a named memory location that holds a value of a specific data type, declared using a strict type-binding rule. Every variable must be declared with a data type *before* it can be used, and the compiler enforces that the variable can only store values compatible with that declared type.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A Java variable is a *strongly-typed* identifier that refers to a memory location allocated on the **Stack** (for primitives) or the **Heap** (for references), constrained to hold only values of its declared type. Java is *statically and strongly typed* — once declared, the type of a variable cannot change at runtime.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Think of a variable as a **labelled, size-fixed box in a warehouse**.
- The **label** on the box = the variable's name (e.g., `age`).
- The **size/shape** of the box = the data type (e.g., `int`, `double`) — it determines what fits inside.
- The **contents** of the box = the value stored (e.g., `25`).
- The **warehouse clerk (compiler)** checks the label and refuses to put a square peg (a `String`) into a round hole (an `int`).

### 1.2 Data Types — Definition

A **data type** defines the *kind* of value a variable can hold, the *size* of memory required, the *range* of values possible, and the *operations* permitted on those values. Java's type system is divided into two main categories:

1. **Primitive Data Types** — 8 predefined types built into the language.
2. **Reference (Non-Primitive) Data Types** — types that refer to objects (e.g., `String`, arrays, classes).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Java's **8 primitive types** are *not* objects — they hold their value directly in memory. The standard metric used in KTU questions for range is: $\text{Range} = -2^{(n-1)} \text{ to } 2^{(n-1)} - 1$ for signed types of `n` bits.

### 1.3 Operators — Definition

An **operator** is a special symbol that performs a specific operation on one, two, or three operands and returns a result. Java provides a rich set of operators grouped into categories: arithmetic, relational, logical, bitwise, assignment, ternary, and `instanceof`.

**Intuitive Analogy:**
Operators are like **verbs in a sentence**. The operands are the *nouns* (data), and the operator is the *action* (add, compare, assign). For example, in `a + b`, the `+` is the operator, and `a`, `b` are operands.

---

### 1.4 Classification of Java Data Types (at a glance)

| Category | Types | Storage |
|---|---|---|
| **Integer** | `byte`, `short`, `int`, `long` | Stack (direct) |
| **Floating-point** | `float`, `double` | Stack (direct) |
| **Character** | `char` | Stack (direct) |
| **Boolean** | `boolean` | Stack (direct) |
| **Reference** | `String`, Arrays, Classes, Interfaces | Heap (reference on stack) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 — Module 1):** Students must master the **size, range, and default values** of all 8 primitive types, the **rules of type promotion** in expressions, **widening vs narrowing casting**, and the **precedence and associativity** of all operator categories.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 8 Primitive Data Types — Detailed Analysis

Java guarantees the **size** of each primitive type across all platforms (platform independence), which is critical for KTU questions.

#### **A. Integer Types (Signed, 2's Complement)**

| Type | Size (bits) | Size (bytes) | Min Value | Max Value | Default |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `byte` | 8 | 1 | $-128$ | $127$ | `0` |
| `short` | 16 | 2 | $-32{,}768$ | $32{,}767$ | `0` |
| `int` | 32 | 4 | $-2^{31}$ | $2^{31}-1$ | `0` |
| `long` | 64 | 8 | $-2^{63}$ | $2^{63}-1$ | `0L` |

**Range Formula (Signed 2's Complement):**

$$\text{Range} = [-2^{(n-1)},\ \ 2^{(n-1)} - 1]$$

For `int` (n = 32):

$$\text{Range}_{\text{int}} = [-2^{31},\ \ 2^{31} - 1] = [-2{,}147{,}483{,}648,\ \ 2{,}147{,}483{,}647]$$

#### **B. Floating-Point Types (IEEE 754 Standard)**

| Type | Size (bits) | Precision | Default | Suffix |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `float` | 32 | ~6–7 decimal digits | `0.0f` | `f` or `F` |
| `double` | 64 | ~15 decimal digits | `0.0d` | `d` or `D` (optional) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The IEEE 754 standard is the global benchmark. The **standard metric** for `float` precision is **24 bits of mantissa (~7 significant digits)** and for `double` it is **53 bits of mantissa (~15–17 significant digits)**. Always use `double` for scientific/engineering calculations unless memory is a constraint.

#### **C. Character and Boolean**

- **`char`**: 16-bit, **unsigned**, uses the **Unicode** character set (range `\u0000` to `\uFFFF`, i.e., 0 to 65,535).
- **`boolean`**: size is *not precisely defined* by the JVM spec (typically 1 bit logically), can only hold `true` or `false`.

### 2.2 Variables — Declaration, Initialization, and Scope

**Three forms of variable usage in Java:**

1. **Declaration:** `int age;`  → Reserves memory.
2. **Initialization:** `age = 25;`  → Assigns a value.
3. **Combined:** `int age = 25;`  → Both in one statement.

**Scope of Variables (KTU High-Yield Topic):**

| Scope Type | Declaration Location | Lifetime | Visibility |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Local** | Inside a method/block | Until block ends | Within the block |
| **Instance** | Inside class, outside method | Until object exists | Throughout the class (non-static) |
| **Static (Class)** | Inside class with `static` | Until program ends | Throughout the class (shared) |
| **Parameter** | In method signature | During method call | Inside the method |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** Local variables **do not have default values** in Java — they must be initialized before use, or the compiler throws a "variable might not have been initialized" error. Instance and static variables *do* have defaults (e.g., `0`, `0.0`, `false`, `null`).

### 2.3 Type Casting — Widening vs Narrowing

#### **A. Widening (Implicit / Automatic) Conversion**
Happens when a smaller type is assigned to a larger type — *no data loss*, performed automatically by the compiler.

$$\text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}$$

**Conversion Rule:**

$$\text{If } \text{size}(T_1) < \text{size}(T_2) \Rightarrow \text{Widening is automatic}$$

#### **B. Narrowing (Explicit) Conversion**
Required when a larger type is assigned to a smaller type — *possible data loss*, requires a cast operator.

$$\text{double} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{byte}$$

**Syntax:** `targetType variable = (targetType) value;`

#### **C. Type Promotion in Expressions**
When evaluating a binary expression, Java automatically promotes the operands to a common type using these rules:

**Promotion Rule Chain (KTU Hot Question):**

1. If either operand is `double` → the other is promoted to `double`.
2. Else, if either is `float` → the other is promoted to `float`.
3. Else, if either is `long` → the other is promoted to `long`.
4. **Otherwise, both are promoted to `int`** (even if they are `byte` or `short`).

### 2.4 Operators — Categories and Precedence

#### **KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet — Operators and Precedence**

| Precedence | Operator | Description | Associativity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 (highest) | `()` `[]` `.` | Parentheses, array, member | Left-to-Right |
| 2 | `++` `--` `+` `-` `~` `!` | Unary (post then pre) | Right-to-Left |
| 3 | `*` `/` `%` | Multiplicative | Left-to-Right |
| 4 | `+` `-` | Additive | Left-to-Right |
| 5 | `<<` `>>` `>>>` | Shift | Left-to-Right |
| 6 | `<` `<=` `>` `>=` `instanceof` | Relational | Left-to-Right |
| 7 | `==` `!=` | Equality | Left-to-Right |
| 8 | `&` | Bitwise AND | Left-to-Right |
| 9 | `^` | Bitwise XOR | Left-to-Right |
| 10 | $\vert$ | Bitwise OR | Left-to-Right |
| 11 | `&&` | Logical AND | Left-to-Right |
| 12 | $\vert\vert$ | Logical OR | Left-to-Right |
| 13 | `?:` | Ternary | Right-to-Left |
| 14 | `=` `+=` `-=` `*=` `/=` `%=` etc. | Assignment | Right-to-Left |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Table:** In row 10 and 12 above, the pipe symbol $\vert$ is used in **LaTeX math mode** for the bitwise/logical OR operator, which is why the table renders correctly. Inside markdown table cells, **never** use a raw `|` for operators — always wrap in `$...$` math mode.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Systems (IoT):** `byte` and `short` are used in memory-constrained microcontrollers (e.g., Arduino-Java integrations).
- **Scientific Computing:** `double` is mandatory for physics simulations, signal processing, and CAD tools due to its 15-digit precision.
- **Database Systems:** `long` is used for timestamps (Unix epoch in milliseconds).
- **Network Programming:** `byte` arrays handle raw packet data.

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of data type directly impacts **memory footprint** and **numerical precision** — two engineering trade-offs that KTU questions love to test.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Range Calculation for `int` Data Type

Java's `int` type uses **32 bits** with **2's complement** signed representation. The high-order bit is the sign bit (0 for positive, 1 for negative).

**Step 1:** Identify the number of bits.

$$n = 32$$

**Step 2:** The sign bit occupies 1 bit, leaving $n - 1 = 31$ bits for magnitude.

$$n - 1 = 31$$

**Step 3:** Maximum positive value is when all 31 magnitude bits are 1.

$$\text{Max}_{\text{int}} = 2^{(n-1)} - 1 = 2^{31} - 1$$

**Step 4:** Evaluate numerically.

$$2^{31} = 2{,}147{,}483{,}248$$

**Step 5:** Subtract 1 to get the actual maximum.

$$2^{31} - 1 = 2{,}147{,}483{,}247$$

**Step 6:** Minimum value is the most negative number, represented as $2^{(n-1)}$ in 2's complement (sign bit = 1, all magnitude bits = 0).

$$\text{Min}_{\text{int}} = -2^{(n-1)} = -2^{31} = -2{,}147{,}483{,}648$$

**Step 7:** Final range.

$$\text{Range}_{\text{int}} = [-2^{31},\ \ 2^{31} - 1] = [-2{,}147{,}483{,}648,\ \ 2{,}147{,}483{,}647]$$

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Evaluation of a Type-Promotion Expression

**Problem:** Evaluate the following Java expression and explain each promotion step:

```java
byte a = 10;
byte b = 20;
byte c = (byte)(a * b);
```

**Step 1:** Variables `a` and `b` are declared as `byte`.

$$a = 10,\ \ b = 20$$

**Step 2:** According to Java's type promotion rules, in any binary arithmetic operation, **both operands smaller than `int` are promoted to `int`** *before* the operation.

$$\text{byte } a \xrightarrow{\text{promotion}} \text{int } a' = 10$$

$$\text{byte } b \xrightarrow{\text{promotion}} \text{int } b' = 20$$

**Step 3:** Perform the multiplication in `int` arithmetic.

$$a' \times b' = 10 \times 20 = 200$$

**Step 4:** The result `200` is an `int`. Assigning it back to `byte` requires **narrowing casting** (explicit cast).

$$\text{byte } c = (\text{byte})\, 200$$

**Step 5:** `200` fits within `byte` range $[-128, 127]$ — wait, **200 > 127**, so truncation occurs. The cast wraps around using modulo $2^8 = 256$.

$$200 \bmod 256 = 200$$

Since `byte` is signed, `200` is interpreted as $200 - 256 = -56$.

**Step 6:** Final value of `c`.

$$c = -56$$

**Conclusion:** This is a classic KTU trap — students forget that `a * b` is computed as `int`, not `byte`, leading to unexpected overflow.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Operator Behavior on Different Data Types

| Operator | Operands Allowed | Result Type | Special Note |
|---|---|---|---|
| `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%` | Numeric | Promoted type | Integer division truncates |
| `==`, `!=`, `<`, `>` | Numeric, `char`, `boolean` (only `==`/`!=`) | `boolean` | No mixing of types without promotion |
| `&&`, `$\vert\vert$`, `!` | `boolean` only | `boolean` | Short-circuit evaluation |
| `&`, `$\vert$`, `^` (bitwise) | Integer types, `boolean` | Same as operand | Non-short-circuit logical when used with `boolean` |
| `<<`, `>>`, `>>>` | Integer types | Same as left operand | `>>>` is unsigned right shift |
| `instanceof` | Object reference vs class | `boolean` | Checks runtime type |

**Derivative Example — Short-Circuit Evaluation:**

```java
int x = 5;
boolean result = (x > 10) && (++x > 5);
```

- `(x > 10)` evaluates to `false`.
- Because `&&` is short-circuit, the right side `(++x > 5)` is **never evaluated**.
- `x` remains **5**, not 6. This is a frequent KTU question.

---

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: Integer Overflow Detection

**Problem:** Predict the output of `byte b = (byte) 130;`

**Step 1:** Literal value is `130` (default `int`).

$$v = 130$$

**Step 2:** `byte` range is $[-128, 127]$. Since $130 > 127$, overflow occurs.

$$130 - 128 = 2 \text{ positions beyond max}$$

**Step 3:** General formula for signed overflow wrap-around:

$$v_{\text{wrapped}} = v - 2^n \quad \text{where } n = 8 \text{ for byte}$$

$$v_{\text{wrapped}} = 130 - 2^8 = 130 - 256 = -126$$

**Step 4:** Final stored value.

$$b = -126$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Java Data Type Hierarchy (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A0["JAVA DATA TYPES"] --> B0["PRIMITIVE TYPES 8 IN NUMBER"]
    A0 --> C0["REFERENCE TYPES NON PRIMITIVE"]
    B0 --> B1["byte 8 bit"]
    B0 --> B2["short 16 bit"]
    B0 --> B3["int 32 bit"]
    B0 --> B4["long 64 bit"]
    B0 --> B5["float 32 bit IEEE 754"]
    B0 --> B6["double 64 bit IEEE 754"]
    B0 --> B7["char 16 bit Unicode"]
    B0 --> B8["boolean true false"]
    C0 --> C1["String"]
    C0 --> C2["Arrays"]
    C0 --> C3["Classes"]
    C0 --> C4["Interfaces"]
    C0 --> C5["Enumerations"]
    C3 --> D1["User Defined"]
    C4 --> D1
    C1 --> D2["Predefined in java lang"]
```

### 4.2 Type Casting Flow Diagram (Widening vs Narrowing)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1["byte"] -->|implicit widening| A2["short"]
    A2 -->|implicit widening| A3["int"]
    A3 -->|implicit widening| A4["long"]
    A4 -->|implicit widening| A5["float"]
    A5 -->|implicit widening| A6["double"]
    A6 -->|explicit narrowing| A5
    A5 -->|explicit narrowing| A4
    A4 -->|explicit narrowing| A3
    A3 -->|explicit narrowing| A2
    A2 -->|explicit narrowing| A1
    style A1 fill:#e1f5ff
    style A6 fill:#ffe1e1
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Expression Evaluation Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["INPUT Operands and Operator"] --> stepB["LEXICAL ANALYSIS Tokenize"]
    stepB --> stepC["PRECEDENCE CHECK Apply priority"]
    stepC --> stepD{"OPERAND TYPES"}
    stepD -->|Both int or smaller| stepE["PROMOTE TO INT"]
    stepD -->|Any long| stepF["PROMOTE TO LONG"]
    stepD -->|Any float| stepG["PROMOTE TO FLOAT"]
    stepD -->|Any double| stepH["PROMOTE TO DOUBLE"]
    stepE --> stepI["EXECUTE OPERATION"]
    stepF --> stepI
    stepG --> stepI
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ["RESULT TYPE Determined by promotion"]
    stepJ --> stepK["NARROWING CAST If assigned to smaller type"]
    stepK --> stepL["FINAL VALUE Stored or Returned"]
```

### 4.4 Variable Scope Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    classScope["CLASS SCOPE STATIC AND INSTANCE VARIABLES"]
    methodScope["METHOD SCOPE LOCAL VARIABLES AND PARAMETERS"]
    blockScope["BLOCK SCOPE INSIDE LOOPS IF ELSE"]
    classScope --> methodScope
    methodScope --> blockScope
    step1["static int count 0"] -.-> classScope
    step2["int instanceVar 0"] -.-> classScope
    step3["int param PARAM"] -.-> methodScope
    step4["int localVar 0"] -.-> methodScope
    step5["int loopVar i 0"] -.-> blockScope
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Mermaid labels have been kept as raw uppercase alphanumeric text without bold/italic markers to ensure clean rendering in all viewers.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Explain the difference between primitive and reference data types in Java with two examples for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Primitive Data Types | Reference Data Types |
|---|---|---|
| **Storage** | Store actual value directly in memory (stack) | Store reference/address to object (heap) |
| **Size** | Fixed (1 to 8 bytes depending on type) | Variable, depends on object |
| **Default Value** | Has defaults (e.g., `0`, `0.0f`, `false`) | `null` |
| **Examples** | `int x = 10;` `char ch = 'A';` | `String s = "KTU";` `int[] arr = {1,2,3};` |
| **Operations** | Cannot be `null` | Can be `null` |

**Mark Distribution:** Definition (1 mark) + Comparison Table (1 mark) + Examples (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*What is type promotion in Java? Illustrate with an example involving `byte` and `int`.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Type promotion is Java's automatic conversion of operands to a common type before evaluating a binary expression. If either operand is `double`, the other is promoted to `double`; if either is `float`, promotion to `float`; if either is `long`, promotion to `long`; otherwise both are promoted to `int`.

**Example (1.5 marks):**

```java
byte a = 10;
byte b = 20;
int result = a * b;  // a and b promoted to int before multiplication
```

Here, although `a` and `b` are `byte`, Java promotes both to `int` and the result of `a * b` is `int`. The value of `result` is `200`.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2023]**

**(a)** Explain the eight primitive data types in Java with their sizes and ranges. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Java defines 8 primitive data types that are built into the language. They are platform-independent in size, ensuring portability.

**Integer Types (2 marks):**

| Type | Size | Range |
|---|---|---|
| `byte` | 8 bits | $-128$ to $127$ |
| `short` | 16 bits | $-32{,}768$ to $32{,}767$ |
| `int` | 32 bits | $-2^{31}$ to $2^{31} - 1$ |
| `long` | 64 bits | $-2^{63}$ to $2^{63} - 1$ |

Example: `long population = 8_000_000_000L;` (Note the `L` suffix.)

**Floating-Point Types (1.5 marks):**

| Type | Size | Range (approx) | Precision |
|---|---|---|---|
| `float` | 32 bits | $\pm 3.4 \times 10^{38}$ | 7 digits |
| `double` | 64 bits | $\pm 1.8 \times 10^{308}$ | 15–17 digits |

Example: `double pi = 3.141592653589793;`

**Character Type (1 mark):** `char` is 16-bit unsigned, holding a single Unicode character.

Example: `char grade = 'A';` or `char unicodeA = '\u0041';`

**Boolean Type (1.5 marks):** `boolean` can hold only `true` or `false`. Used for control flow.

Example: `boolean isPass = true;`

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often confuse the size of `boolean` and `char`. Remember — `char` is **16-bit unsigned Unicode**, while `boolean` has **JVM-implementation-dependent** size (logical size is 1 bit).

---

**(b)** Explain the various categories of operators in Java with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Arithmetic Operators (1.5 marks):** `+`, `-`, `*`, `/`, `%`
```java
int a = 10, b = 3;
int q = a / b;  // 3 (integer division)
int r = a % b;  // 1 (modulus)
```

**2. Relational Operators (1 mark):** `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`, `==`, `!=`
Return `boolean` values. Example: `a > b` returns `false`.

**3. Logical Operators (1 mark):** `&&`, `$||$`, `!` — operate on `boolean` operands with short-circuit evaluation.

**4. Bitwise Operators (1 mark):** `&`, `$|$`, `^`, `~`, `<<`, `>>`, `>>>`
Operate on individual bits of integers.

**5. Assignment Operators (0.5 mark):** `=`, `+=`, `-=`, `*=`, `/=`, `%=`
Example: `a += 5;` is equivalent to `a = a + 5;`

**6. Unary Operators (0.5 mark):** `++`, `--`, `+`, `-`, `!`, `~`
Prefix vs postfix: `++a` increments then uses; `a++` uses then increments.

**7. Ternary Operator (0.5 mark):** `condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse`
Example: `int min = (a < b) ? a : b;`

**8. `instanceof` Operator (0.5 mark):** Checks if an object is an instance of a class.
Example: `if (obj instanceof String) { ... }`

**9. Operator Precedence Summary Table (0.5 mark):**

| Precedence (High to Low) | Operators |
|---|---|
| Unary | `++`, `--`, `!`, `~` |
| Arithmetic | `*`, `/`, `%`, `+`, `-` |
| Shift | `<<`, `>>`, `>>>` |
| Relational | `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=`, `instanceof` |
| Equality | `==`, `!=` |
| Bitwise | `&`, `^`, $\vert$ |
| Logical | `&&`, $\vert\vert$ |
| Ternary | `?:` |
| Assignment | `=`, `+=`, etc. |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students lose marks by mixing up `&` (bitwise, no short-circuit) and `&&` (logical, short-circuit) when used with `boolean` operands. Both are valid, but only `&&` short-circuits.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Explain variables in Java: declaration, initialization, types of variables, and their scope with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition (1 mark):** A variable is a named memory location that holds a value of a declared data type.

**Declaration and Initialization (1.5 marks):**
```java
int count;        // declaration
count = 10;       // initialization
int total = 100;  // combined
```

**Types of Variables (3.5 marks):**

| Type | Example | Scope | Lifetime |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Local** | Declared inside a method | Method/block | Until block ends |
| **Instance** | Declared in class, no `static` | Class-wide (object) | Until object GC'd |
| **Static** | Declared with `static` | Class-wide (shared) | Until program ends |
| **Parameter** | In method signature | Inside method | During method call |

```java
class Student {
    static int count = 0;          // static variable
    String name;                   // instance variable

    Student(String n) {
        name = n;                  // local assignment to instance
        count++;
    }

    void display() {
        int temp = 5;              // local variable
        System.out.println(name + " " + count + " " + temp);
    }
}
```

**Scope Rules (1 mark):**
- Local variables are visible only within the block where declared.
- Instance variables are accessible by all non-static methods.
- Static variables are shared across all objects.
- A local variable shadows an instance variable with the same name; use `this.variable` to disambiguate.

---

**(b)** Explain type casting (widening and narrowing) and type promotion in Java with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Widening (Implicit) Casting (2 marks):**
Automatically converts a smaller type to a larger type. No data loss occurs.

$$\text{byte} \rightarrow \text{short} \rightarrow \text{int} \rightarrow \text{long} \rightarrow \text{float} \rightarrow \text{double}$$

```java
int x = 100;
double d = x;     // automatic: int widened to double
System.out.println(d);  // 100.0
```

**2. Narrowing (Explicit) Casting (2 marks):**
Requires explicit cast operator; may cause data loss.

```java
double d = 9.78;
int x = (int) d;  // explicit: double narrowed to int
System.out.println(x);  // 9 (fractional part lost)
```

**3. Type Promotion in Expressions (3 marks):**

Java promotes operands in binary operations using the chain:
- Any `double` → promote to `double`
- Else any `float` → promote to `float`
- Else any `long` → promote to `long`
- Otherwise → both promote to `int`

**Example 1:**
```java
byte a = 10, b = 20;
int result = a * b;   // a, b promoted to int; result = 200
```

**Example 2:**
```java
int i = 5;
double d = 2.5;
double result = i + d;  // i promoted to double; result = 7.5
```

**Example 3:**
```java
long l = 1000L;
int i = 5;
long result = l + i;   // i promoted to long; result = 1005L
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** A very common mistake is writing `byte c = a * b;` *without* an explicit cast. This causes a **compilation error** because the result of `a * b` is `int`, and narrowing back to `byte` requires `(byte)`. Even if values fit, Java's strict typing demands the cast.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Marks-Losing Mistakes in this Topic:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `L` or `f` suffix:** Writing `long x = 10000000000;` causes a compilation error (integer too large for int). Must write `10000000000L`.
> 2. **Mixing `=` (assignment) and `==` (comparison):** A classic KTU trap.
> 3. **Assuming `boolean` size is 1 bit in memory:** It's JVM-dependent; the spec only mandates two values: `true` and `false`.
> 4. **Forgetting type promotion in `byte` arithmetic:** `byte a = 10, b = 20; byte c = a * b;` fails to compile.
> 5. **Confusing `>>` (arithmetic shift, preserves sign) with `>>>` (logical shift, fills with zeros):** Critical for negative numbers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Bullets — Read Before Exam:**

- **8 Primitive Types** are `byte`, `short`, `int`, `long`, `float`, `double`, `char`, `boolean` — sizes: 8, 16, 32, 64, 32, 64, 16, JVM-defined bits respectively.
- **Default values**: numeric → `0`, `float` → `0.0f`, `double` → `0.0d`, `char` → `'\u0000'`, `boolean` → `false`, reference → `null`.
- **Range formula** for signed `n`-bit integer: $[-2^{(n-1)},\ 2^{(n-1)} - 1]$.
- **`char` is unsigned 16-bit Unicode** (0 to 65,535), *not* ASCII.
- **Integer literals** default to `int`; add `L` for `long`. **Floating-point literals** default to `double`; add `f` for `float`.
- **Widening is automatic** (smaller → larger); **narrowing requires explicit cast** (larger → smaller).
- **Type promotion rule**: in expressions, the largest operand type wins; otherwise `int` wins (even for `byte`/`short`).
- **Operators' precedence** (high → low): Unary → Arithmetic → Shift → Relational → Equality → Bitwise → Logical → Ternary → Assignment.
- **`++a` vs `a++`**: prefix increments first, postfix increments after use.
- **`&&` and `||` short-circuit**; `&` and `|` (with booleans) do **not** short-circuit.
- **`>>>` is unsigned right shift** (fills with zeros); `>>` is signed (preserves sign bit).
- **`instanceof`** returns `true` if the object is an instance of the specified class or its subclass.
- **Local variables** must be initialized before use; **instance/static variables** have default values.
- **Variable scope hierarchy**: Block < Method < Class; static variables are shared across all instances of a class.
- **`final` keyword** makes a variable a *constant* — value cannot be changed after initialization (conventionally named in `UPPER_SNAKE_CASE`).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Structures (Conditional Statements, Loops)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT611 - OBJECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING IN JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Control Structures (Conditional Statements, Loops)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the paradigm of **Structured Programming**, **Control Structures** are the fundamental constructs that dictate the **flow of execution** within a program. According to the **Böhm-Jacopini Theorem (1966)**, any computable function can be expressed using exactly three control structures: **Sequence**, **Selection**, and **Iteration**. In Java, these are implemented as statements that govern which block of code executes, under what conditions, and how many times.

Java classifies control structures into three primary categories:

1. **Selection (Decision) Statements** — `if`, `if-else`, `if-else-if` ladder, nested `if`, `switch-case`, and the **ternary operator** (`? :`).
2. **Iteration (Looping) Statements** — `for`, `while`, `do-while`, and the **enhanced for-each** loop (introduced in Java 5).
3. **Jump Statements** — `break`, `continue`, `return`, and labelled jumps (used to transfer control unconditionally).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 1 mandates explicit coverage of **conditional branching (`if-else`, `switch`)**, **pre-test vs post-test loops (`while`, `do-while`)**, and the **`for` loop family (including the enhanced `for-each`)**. Jump statements (`break`, `continue`) are examinable only in the context of loops and `switch`.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are **driving a car from your college to home**:

- **Sequence** is driving straight — every instruction runs one after another, in order.
- **Selection (if-else)** is reaching a **fork in the road**: *"If the traffic light is red, stop; else, go."* The path you take depends on a *condition*.
- **Switch** is like a **multi-lane highway exit** — you pick exactly one lane based on your destination number (1, 2, 3, …).
- **While loop** is like a **revolving door that checks first** — *"While the door is not full, keep pushing."* The body may **never execute** if the condition is initially false.
- **Do-while loop** is a **door that swings open first, then checks** — *"Open the door, then check if the latch is engaged."* The body executes **at least once**, no matter what.
- **For loop** is a **counted staircase** — *"For step = 1 to 10, climb one stair."* You know the exact starting point, ending point, and step size in advance.
- **Enhanced for-each** is a **delivery boy scanning a row of packages** — he picks up *each* package one-by-one without caring about the index.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction to Memorize:** `while` is a **pre-test (entry-controlled)** loop; `do-while` is a **post-test (exit-controlled)** loop. The compiler enforces this difference in Java syntax (note the **trailing semicolon** after `while(condition);` in a `do-while`).

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology (KTU Board Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Entry-controlled loop** | Condition is tested *before* the body executes (`for`, `while`). |
| **Exit-controlled loop** | Condition is tested *after* the body executes (`do-while`). |
| **Iteration** | One complete execution of the loop body. |
| **Counter variable** | Variable that tracks loop progress (commonly `i`, `j`, `k`). |
| **Infinite loop** | Loop whose termination condition is never met — a logical/runtime hazard. |
| **Fall-through** | Behaviour in `switch` where execution "drops" into the next `case` if `break` is omitted. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Selection Statements — Structural Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 Simple `if` Statement

**Why:** To execute a block of code *only* when a specific condition evaluates to `true`.

**How:** The condition inside parentheses `()` must yield a `boolean` result. In Java, the condition **cannot** be an integer (unlike C/C++).

```
if (condition) {
    // executes ONLY if condition is true
}
```

#### 2.1.2 `if-else` Statement

**Why:** Provides a binary decision path — execute *this* block OR *that* block, never both.

```
if (condition) {
    // true-branch
} else {
    // false-branch
}
```

#### 2.1.3 `if-else-if` Ladder

**Why:** To test multiple conditions in sequence. The first matching condition's block executes; remaining blocks are skipped.

```
if (condition1) {
    // block 1
} else if (condition2) {
    // block 2
} else {
    // default block
}
```

#### 2.1.4 Nested `if`

**Why:** To embed decision logic *within* another decision. Indentation must be rigorous to avoid the **dangling-else** problem.

#### 2.1.5 `switch-case` Statement

**Why:** A cleaner, more efficient alternative to long `if-else-if` ladders when a single variable is compared against **multiple discrete constant values**.

**KTU High-Yield Rules:**

1. The expression inside `switch(...)` must evaluate to `char`, `byte`, `short`, `int`, **`String`** (Java 7+), or an **enum**.
2. Each `case` label must be a **compile-time constant** (literal or `final` variable).
3. **No duplicate `case` values** are allowed.
4. `break` terminates the switch; without it, **fall-through** occurs.
5. `default` is optional but recommended — handles unmatched values.

#### 2.1.6 Ternary Operator `?:`

**Why:** A compact one-line replacement for simple `if-else`. **Syntax:** `result = condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse;`

---

### 2.2 Iteration Statements — Structural Breakdown

#### 2.2.1 The `for` Loop (Counted / Definite Loop)

**Why:** Best when the number of iterations is **known in advance**.

```
for (initialization; condition; update) {
    // body
}
```

Execution order: **initialization → condition check → body → update → condition check → body → …**

#### 2.2.2 The `while` Loop (Pre-test / Entry-controlled)

**Why:** Best when the number of iterations is **unknown** and depends on runtime conditions.

```
while (condition) {
    // body — must contain logic that eventually falsifies the condition
}
```

#### 2.2.3 The `do-while` Loop (Post-test / Exit-controlled)

**Why:** Guarantees **at least one execution** of the body — useful for menus, input validation, and game rounds.

```
do {
    // body
} while (condition);   // NOTE the mandatory semicolon
```

#### 2.2.4 Enhanced `for-each` Loop

**Why:** Simplifies iteration over **arrays** and **collections** (objects implementing `Iterable`). Cannot modify the collection structure during traversal.

```
for (Type variable : collection/array) {
    // body
}
```

---

### 2.3 Jump Statements (Supporting Role)

| Statement | Function |
|---|---|
| `break` | Terminates the **innermost** loop or `switch`. |
| `continue` | Skips the **current iteration** and jumps to the next. |
| `return` | Exits the entire method. |
| **Labelled `break`/`continue`** | Transfers control out of an *outer* nested loop. |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Logical Truth Table

#### 2.4.1 Logical Operator Truth Table

| A | B | A && B (AND) | A \|\| B (OR) | !A (NOT) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| false | false | false | false | true |
| false | true | false | true | true |
| true | false | false | true | false |
| true | true | true | true | false |

> [!NOTE]
> Java uses **short-circuit evaluation**: `&&` skips the right operand if the left is `false`; `||` skips the right if the left is `true`. This matters when the right operand has a **side effect** (e.g., method call, division).

#### 2.4.2 Loop Complexity Reference

| Loop Type | Ideal Use | Min. Executions | Termination Trigger |
|---|---|---|---|
| `for` | Fixed count | 0 | Counter exceeds bound |
| `while` | Sentinel-driven | 0 | Condition becomes false |
| `do-while` | Menu / validation | **1** | Condition becomes false |
| `for-each` | Read-only traversal | 0 | All elements consumed |

#### 2.4.3 Operator Precedence (Relational & Logical — High-Yield Subset)

1. `!` (unary NOT) — highest
2. `*`, `/`, `%`
3. `+`, `-`
4. `<`, `<=`, `>`, `>=` (relational)
5. `==`, `!=` (equality)
6. `&&` (logical AND)
7. `||` (logical OR) — lowest

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** `==` compares **references** for objects and **values** for primitives. For `String` comparison, use `.equals()`. This is a **favourite KTU trick question** in code-tracing problems.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Control Systems:** `if-else` drives threshold-based decisions in embedded firmware (e.g., *"if temperature > 90°C, cut power"*).
- **Data Processing Pipelines:** `for-each` iterates over sensor arrays in IoT analytics.
- **Menu-driven Applications:** `do-while` powers ATM and kiosk interfaces that must display options at least once.
- **Compilers & Parsers:** `switch` statements are optimized by `tableswitch`/`lookupswitch` JVM bytecodes for high-performance dispatch.
- **Numerical Computing:** Loop constructs implement matrix multiplication, Taylor series, and iterative solvers (Newton-Raphson, etc.).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Tracing Logic: `for` Loop Iteration Count

**Problem:** Determine the number of times the body of the following loop executes.

```java
for (int i = 10; i <= 20; i += 2) {
    System.out.println(i);
}
```

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Let } i_0 &= 10 \quad \text{(initial value)} \\
i_n &= i_{n-1} + 2 \quad \text{(arithmetic progression with } d=2) \\
\text{Termination: } i_n &\le 20 \\
10 + 2n &\le 20 \\
2n &\le 10 \\
n &\le 5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical Trace Table:**

| Iteration | $i$ value | Condition $i \le 20$ | Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | true | Print 10, then $i \leftarrow 12$ |
| 2 | 12 | true | Print 12, then $i \leftarrow 14$ |
| 3 | 14 | true | Print 14, then $i \leftarrow 16$ |
| 4 | 16 | true | Print 16, then $i \leftarrow 18$ |
| 5 | 18 | true | Print 18, then $i \leftarrow 20$ |
| 6 | 20 | true | Print 20, then $i \leftarrow 22$ |
| 7 | 22 | **false** | **Exit loop** |

**Result:** Body executes **6 times**, printing `10 12 14 16 18 20` on separate lines.

**Generalized Formula for `for` Loop Count:**

$$
N = \left\lfloor \frac{\text{upper} - \text{lower}}{\text{step}} \right\rfloor + 1
$$

(Apply only when the progression is monotonic and the loop terminates normally.)

---

### 3.2 Tracing Logic: Nested Loop Complexity

**Problem:** How many times does the inner `System.out.println` execute?

```java
for (int i = 1; i <= 3; i++) {
    for (int j = 1; j <= 4; j++) {
        System.out.println(i + "," + j);
    }
}
```

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{\text{outer}} &= 3 \\
N_{\text{inner}} &= 4 \\
N_{\text{total}} &= N_{\text{outer}} \times N_{\text{inner}} \\
&= 3 \times 4 = 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trace Matrix:**

| | j=1 | j=2 | j=3 | j=4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **i=1** | (1,1) | (1,2) | (1,3) | (1,4) |
| **i=2** | (2,1) | (2,2) | (2,3) | (2,4) |
| **i=3** | (3,1) | (3,2) | (3,3) | (3,4) |

**Total inner-statement executions = 12**.

---

### 3.3 Boolean Expression Simplification (De Morgan's Laws)

**Problem:** Simplify the condition `!(x > 0 && y < 10)`.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\neg(A \land B) &\equiv (\neg A) \lor (\neg B) \quad \text{(De Morgan's Law)} \\
\text{Let } A &\equiv (x > 0), \quad B \equiv (y < 10) \\
\neg A &\equiv (x \le 0) \\
\neg B &\equiv (y \ge 10) \\
\therefore \quad \neg(A \land B) &\equiv (x \le 0) \;\|\|\; (y \ge 10)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **De Morgan's Laws** are frequently tested in KTU papers to simplify negated compound conditions. Memorize both forms:
> 1. $\neg(A \land B) \equiv \neg A \lor \neg B$
> 2. $\neg(A \lor B) \equiv \neg A \land \neg B$

---

### 3.4 Output Trace: `switch` Fall-through Demonstration

**Code:**

```java
int x = 2;
switch (x) {
    case 1: System.out.print("One ");
    case 2: System.out.print("Two ");
    case 3: System.out.print("Three ");
    default: System.out.print("Default");
}
```

**Step-by-Step Trace:**

1. `x = 2` → matches `case 2`.
2. `break` is **absent** → falls through to `case 3` → falls through to `default`.
3. **Output:** `Two Three Default`

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners specifically test **fall-through behaviour** in `switch`. Omitting `break` is **not a syntax error** in Java — it is a logical one.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Control Structure

| Scenario | Recommended Construct | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Check if a number is even/odd | `if-else` | Binary decision |
| Grade calculation (A/B/C/D/F) | `if-else-if` ladder or `switch` | Multiple mutually exclusive branches |
| Print numbers 1 to 100 | `for` loop | Known iteration count |
| Read user input until "quit" is typed | `while` loop | Sentinel value, unknown count |
| Display a menu at least once | `do-while` | Post-test requirement |
| Iterate over all marks in a class | `for-each` | Clean read-only traversal |
| Skip processing of negative numbers | `if` + `continue` | Filter pattern |
| Stop a search upon finding an element | `if` + `break` | Early exit pattern |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart: `if-else` Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start]) --> inputA[/Read condition/]
    inputA --> decisionA{Is condition true?}
    decisionA -- Yes --> branchA1[Execute true-block]
    branchA1 --> joinA
    decisionA -- No --> branchA2[Execute false-block]
    branchA2 --> joinA
    joinA([End])
```

### 4.2 Flowchart: `if-else-if` Ladder

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start]) --> condB1{Condition 1?}
    condB1 -- Yes --> blockB1[Block 1]
    condB1 -- No --> condB2{Condition 2?}
    condB2 -- Yes --> blockB2[Block 2]
    condB2 -- No --> condB3{Condition 3?}
    condB3 -- Yes --> blockB3[Block 3]
    condB3 -- No --> defB[Default Block]
    blockB1 --> endB([End])
    blockB2 --> endB
    blockB3 --> endB
    defB --> endB
```

### 4.3 Flowchart: `switch-case` Dispatch

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startC([Start]) --> readC[/Read variable/]
    readC --> caseC1{case 1?}
    caseC1 -- Yes --> bodyC1[Block 1]
    caseC1 -- No --> caseC2{case 2?}
    caseC2 -- Yes --> bodyC2[Block 2]
    caseC2 -- No --> caseC3{case 3?}
    caseC3 -- Yes --> bodyC3[Block 3]
    caseC3 -- No --> defC[default Block]
    bodyC1 --> brkC[break]
    bodyC2 --> brkC
    bodyC3 --> brkC
    defC --> endC([End])
    brkC --> endC
```

### 4.4 Flowchart: `for` Loop (Pre-test)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startD([Start]) --> initD[Initialization: i = start]
    initD --> testD{i ≤ end?}
    testD -- Yes --> bodyD[Execute loop body]
    bodyD --> updateD[Update: i = i + step]
    updateD --> testD
    testD -- No --> endD([End])
```

### 4.5 Flowchart: `while` vs `do-while` (Pre-test vs Post-test)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph WHILE_Pre_Test
        sW([Start]) --> cW{Condition?}
        cW -- Yes --> bW[Body]
        bW --> cW
        cW -- No --> eW([End])
    end
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph DOWHILE_Post_Test
        sD([Start]) --> bD[Body executes first]
        bD --> cD{Condition?}
        cD -- Yes --> bD
        cD -- No --> eD([End])
    end
```

### 4.6 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Nested Loop with `break` & `continue`

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    entryP([Outer Loop start i=1..3]) --> innerEntry[Enter inner loop j=1..5]
    innerEntry --> checkEven{j is even?}
    checkEven -- Yes --> skipP[continue skip this iteration]
    skipP --> checkEven
    checkEven -- No --> printP[Print i and j]
    printP --> checkLimit{j greater than 4?}
    checkLimit -- Yes --> exitInner[break exit inner loop]
    checkLimit -- No --> incJ[j++]
    incJ --> checkEven
    exitInner --> incI[i++]
    incI --> entryP
    entryP -- i greater than 3 --> exitP([End])
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The inner loop prints only odd values of `j` (because `continue` skips even ones). When `j > 4`, the `break` exits the inner loop, but the **outer loop continues** with the next `i`. This mimics real-world packet-filtering and search algorithms.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**  
Differentiate between entry-controlled and exit-controlled loops in Java. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Entry-controlled loop:** The test condition is evaluated *before* the body executes. If the condition is initially `false`, the body may execute **zero times**. Examples: `for` loop, `while` loop. *(1 mark)*
- **Exit-controlled loop:** The test condition is evaluated *after* the body executes. The body executes **at least once**, regardless of the condition. Example: `do-while` loop. *(1 mark)*
- **Key syntactic difference:** A `do-while` loop ends with a **semicolon** after the `while(condition);` — this is a common point of confusion. *(1 mark)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**  
Explain the concept of *fall-through* in a Java `switch` statement. How can it be prevented?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Fall-through** is the behaviour in which, when a matching `case` is found and no `break` (or `return`) statement is present, execution **continues into the subsequent `case(s)`** until a `break` or the end of the `switch` is reached. *(1.5 marks)*
- **Prevention:** Insert a `break;` statement at the end of every `case` block where fall-through is not intended. *(1 mark)*
- Java also supports **explicit fall-through** using arrow syntax (`case 1 -> ...`) introduced as a **standard feature in Java 14+** — although traditional `case :` syntax remains dominant in KTU syllabi. *(0.5 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions offer an **internal choice** — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Write a Java program to read an integer `N` from the user and determine whether it is a **prime number** using a `for` loop. Display appropriate output. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class PrimeCheck {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter a positive integer: ");
        int n = sc.nextInt();
        boolean isPrime = true;

        if (n <= 1) {
            isPrime = false;
        } else {
            for (int i = 2; i <= n / 2; i++) {
                if (n % i == 0) {
                    isPrime = false;
                    break;   // early exit
                }
            }
        }

        if (isPrime)
            System.out.println(n + " is a Prime number.");
        else
            System.out.println(n + " is NOT a Prime number.");
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Mark Allocation:**

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Class structure, `main` signature, `Scanner` import | 1 |
| Correct input reading | 1 |
| Prime logic with `for` loop (loop bound $i \le n/2$) | 3 |
| `break` for early termination | 1 |
| Correct output formatting | 1 |

---

**(b)** Explain the **enhanced `for-each` loop** in Java. Write a program to find the **sum and average of marks stored in an array** using the `for-each` loop. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Explanation (3 marks):**
- Introduced in **Java 5 (J2SE 1.5)**, the enhanced `for` loop (also called `for-each`) provides a **concise, index-free** way to traverse arrays and collections.
- **Syntax:** `for (Type variable : arrayOrCollection) { ... }`
- **Limitations:** *(i)* The loop variable is **read-only** — modifying it does not affect the original array. *(ii)* It is **not suitable** when you need the index. *(iii)* Cannot traverse backwards.

**Program (4 marks):**

```java
public class MarksStats {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int[] marks = {78, 85, 92, 67, 88, 95, 73};
        int sum = 0;

        for (int m : marks) {
            sum += m;
        }

        double average = (double) sum / marks.length;
        System.out.println("Total marks: " + sum);
        System.out.println("Average: " + average);
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
Total marks: 578
Average: 82.57142857142857
```

**Mark Allocation for (b):**

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Correct explanation of `for-each` syntax and purpose | 2 |
| Listing at least two limitations | 1 |
| Correct program logic (sum & average) | 3 |
| Output trace | 1 |

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the **`if-else-if` ladder** with a suitable Java program that accepts a **percentage** and prints the corresponding **grade** as per the following rules: **(7 marks)**

| Percentage | Grade |
|---|---|
| $\ge 90$ | A+ |
| $80 \le p < 90$ | A |
| $70 \le p < 80$ | B+ |
| $60 \le p < 70$ | B |
| $50 \le p < 60$ | C |
| $< 50$ | F |

**Model Solution:**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class GradeCalculator {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter percentage: ");
        double p = sc.nextDouble();
        String grade;

        if (p >= 90) {
            grade = "A+";
        } else if (p >= 80) {
            grade = "A";
        } else if (p >= 70) {
            grade = "B+";
        } else if (p >= 60) {
            grade = "B";
        } else if (p >= 50) {
            grade = "C";
        } else {
            grade = "F";
        }

        System.out.println("Grade: " + grade);
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Why ladder works efficiently here:** Conditions are tested **top-down**; the first match wins, so we don't need to write `p < 90 && p >= 80` explicitly — the previous `else if` already guarantees the upper bound.

**Mark Allocation:**

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Correct conditional boundaries (all 6 ranges) | 3 |
| Proper use of `else if` chain | 2 |
| Variable declaration and output | 1 |
| Code quality & indentation | 1 |

---

**(b)** Compare the `while` and `do-while` loops in Java. Write a program that uses a **`do-while` loop to implement a menu-driven calculator** (Addition, Subtraction, Exit) that continues until the user chooses to exit. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Feature | `while` | `do-while` |
|---|---|---|
| Type | Entry-controlled (pre-test) | Exit-controlled (post-test) |
| Minimum executions | 0 | 1 |
| Semicolon after condition | Not required | **Mandatory** |
| Use case | Unknown iteration count, may not run | Menu, validation, must run at least once |

**Program (5 marks):**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class MenuCalculator {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        int choice;
        do {
            System.out.println("\n--- MENU ---");
            System.out.println("1. Addition");
            System.out.println("2. Subtraction");
            System.out.println("3. Exit");
            System.out.print("Enter choice: ");
            choice = sc.nextInt();

            if (choice == 1 || choice == 2) {
                System.out.print("Enter two numbers: ");
                double a = sc.nextDouble();
                double b = sc.nextDouble();
                if (choice == 1)
                    System.out.println("Sum = " + (a + b));
                else
                    System.out.println("Difference = " + (a - b));
            } else if (choice != 3) {
                System.out.println("Invalid choice. Try again.");
            }
        } while (choice != 3);

        System.out.println("Exiting calculator. Goodbye!");
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Sample Run:**

```
--- MENU ---
1. Addition
2. Subtraction
3. Exit
Enter choice: 1
Enter two numbers: 10 5
Sum = 15.0

--- MENU ---
1. Addition
2. Subtraction
3. Exit
Enter choice: 3
Exiting calculator. Goodbye!
```

**Mark Allocation for (b):**

| Component | Marks |
|---|---|
| Comparison table with 4 distinct features | 2 |
| Correct `do-while` structure with terminating semicolon | 1 |
| Menu display logic | 1 |
| Arithmetic operations and exit handling | 1 |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students LOSE marks in Control Structure questions:**
>
> 1. **Missing semicolon after `do-while` condition** → Compilation error. (Loses 1–2 marks immediately.)
> 2. **Using `=` instead of `==`** in `if` conditions. Java compilers reject this for `boolean`, but students waste time debugging.
> 3. **Off-by-one errors** in `for` loops: writing `i < n` when `i <= n` is required (or vice versa). Always do a **dry trace** for `n = 0` and `n = 1` boundary cases.
> 4. **Forgetting `break` in `switch`** — the program compiles, but output is wrong, costing all logic marks.
> 5. **Integer overflow** in loop counters when computing `i++` near `Integer.MAX_VALUE`. Use `long` for safety in numerical programs.
> 6. **Infinite loops** — forgetting to update the counter inside a `while` loop is the #1 reason programs hang during viva.
> 7. **Comparing `String` with `==` instead of `.equals()`** — works in some cases due to string interning, fails in others; examiners LOVE this trap.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Control Structures in Java**

- ✅ Java has **three categories** of control structures: **Selection**, **Iteration**, **Jump**.
- ✅ `if`, `if-else`, `if-else-if` ladder, nested `if` — all conditions must yield a `boolean` (no integer-as-boolean like C/C++).
- ✅ `switch` accepts `byte`, `short`, `char`, `int`, **`String`**, and `enum` — but **NOT** `long`, `float`, or `double`.
- ✅ Every `case` in `switch` must be a **compile-time constant**; duplicates are illegal.
- ✅ **Fall-through** in `switch` occurs when `break` is omitted — it is a *feature* exploited deliberately in some advanced patterns.
- ✅ `for` loop is best for **counted iterations**; syntax: `for(init; condition; update)`.
- ✅ `while` is **pre-test** (entry-controlled); body may never execute.
- ✅ `do-while` is **post-test** (exit-controlled); body executes **at least once**; **semicolon is mandatory** after the condition.
- ✅ Enhanced `for-each` (Java 5+) simplifies array/collection traversal but is **read-only** and gives **no index access**.
- ✅ `break` exits the **innermost** loop/switch; `continue` skips to the **next iteration**.
- ✅ **Logical operators:** `&&` (AND), `||` (OR), `!` (NOT). Java uses **short-circuit evaluation**.
- ✅ **Operator precedence** (high to low): `!` → arithmetic → relational → equality (`==`, `!=`) → `&&` → `||`.
- ✅ **De Morgan's Laws:** $\neg(A \land B) \equiv \neg A \lor \neg B$ and $\neg(A \lor B) \equiv \neg A \land \neg B$.
- ✅ **General loop count formula:** $N = \left\lfloor \dfrac{\text{upper} - \text{lower}}{\text{step}} \right\rfloor + 1$.
- ✅ For nested loops, total iterations = product of individual iteration counts.
- ✅ **Golden rule:** Always do a **dry trace** with boundary values (0, 1, negative, maximum) before submitting code in the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input/Output Basics (Reading Console Input, Writing Console Output)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT611 - OBJECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING IN JAVA
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Input/Output Basics (Reading Console Input, Writing Console Output)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Console Input/Output (I/O)** in Java refers to the mechanism by which a program exchanges data with the **standard input stream** (typically the keyboard) and the **standard output stream** (typically the monitor) through three predefined streams: `System.in`, `System.out`, and `System.err`, which are automatically available to every Java application without requiring explicit instantiation.

The three standard streams in Java are:

1. **`System.out`** — The standard output stream used to send results to the console.
2. **`System.in`** — The standard input stream used to read data entered by the user.
3. **`System.err`** — The standard error stream used to output error messages.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Post Office Counter**
> Think of your Java program as a person standing behind a post office counter.
> - **`System.out`** is the **window through which the clerk hands out letters (output)** to the customer.
> - **`System.in`** is the **letter slot through which customers drop in their requests (input)**.
> - **`Scanner` / `BufferedReader`** are the **tools the clerk uses to read the letters** — `Scanner` is like a magnifying glass that can read numbers, words, and even whole paragraphs, while `BufferedReader` is a faster conveyor belt that reads a whole line at once.

### 1.3 Key Characteristics

- Java does **not** provide direct console I/O like C's `scanf()` or `printf()`. Instead, it uses stream classes from `java.io` and `java.util` packages.
- `System.out` is a **public static final** reference of type `PrintStream`.
- `System.in` is a **public static final** reference of type `InputStream`.
- All console operations in Java are **byte-oriented** at the lowest level, but wrappers like `Scanner` convert them to higher-level types (int, double, String).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Writing Console Output — `System.out`

`System.out` provides three primary methods for writing to the console:

| Method | Description | Output Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `System.out.print()` | Prints the argument as a string. | **Does NOT** move the cursor to the next line. |
| `System.out.println()` | Prints the argument and terminates the line. | **Moves** the cursor to the next line. |
| `System.out.printf()` | Prints using a **format string** with format specifiers. | Does NOT move to next line (unless `\n` is in format). |
| `System.out.format()` | Identical to `printf()`. | Same as `printf()`. |

#### 2.1.1 The `printf()` Format Specifiers (High-Yield for KTU)

`printf()` uses **format specifiers** that begin with `%` and end with a **conversion character**.

$$ \text{Format Specifier} = \% \ \ [\text{flag}] \ \ [\text{width}] \ [.precision] \ \ \text{conversion} $$

| Specifier | Data Type | Example | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `%d` | Decimal integer | `printf("%d", 25)` | `25` |
| `%f` | Floating-point | `printf("%.2f", 3.14159)` | `3.14` |
| `%s` | String | `printf("%s", "KTU")` | `KTU` |
| `%c` | Character | `printf("%c", 'A')` | `A` |
| `%n` | Platform-independent newline | `printf("Hello%nWorld")` | `Hello` on one line, `World` on next |
| `%b` | Boolean | `printf("%b", true)` | `true` |
| `%x` / `%o` | Hexadecimal / Octal integer | `printf("%x", 255)` | `ff` |
| `%%` | Literal percent sign | `printf("50%%")` | `50%` |

#### 2.1.2 Common Format Flags

- `-` : Left-justify within the given width.
- `+` : Always show sign for numeric values.
- `0` : Pad with leading zeros.
- `,` : Use grouping separator (e.g., `1,000,000`).

> [!NOTE]
> **`%n` vs `\n`**: Always prefer `%n` inside `printf()` because it is **platform-independent** (produces `\r\n` on Windows and `\n` on Linux/Mac). Using `\n` may produce double-spacing on Windows.

### 2.2 Reading Console Input — Three Approaches

Java provides **three primary ways** to read from `System.in`:

#### 2.2.1 Approach 1: `Scanner` Class (Most Common — KTU Favorite)

Located in `java.util` package. The `Scanner` class is the **easiest and most examiner-preferred** method in KTU exams because it provides direct methods to parse primitive types and strings.

**Step-by-step usage logic:**
1. **Import** the package: `import java.util.Scanner;`
2. **Create** a Scanner object bound to `System.in`.
3. **Check** availability using `hasNext()` methods to avoid `InputMismatchException`.
4. **Read** values using `nextXxx()` methods.
5. **Close** the scanner to release resources.

#### 2.2.2 Approach 2: `BufferedReader` Class

Located in `java.io` package. It is **faster** than `Scanner` for large inputs and works with `InputStreamReader` to convert bytes to characters.

#### 2.2.3 Approach 3: `Console` Class (JDK 1.6+)

Located in `java.io` package. It is used for **secure password input** because it does not echo characters to the screen. However, it **does not work in IDEs** like Eclipse/IntelliJ (only in command-line terminals).

### 2.3 KTU Cheat Sheet — Scanner Class Methods

| Method | Returns | Reads |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `next()` | `String` | A single token (up to whitespace) |
| `nextLine()` | `String` | An entire line (until Enter) |
| `nextInt()` | `int` | An integer |
| `nextLong()` | `long` | A long integer |
| `nextFloat()` | `float` | A float |
| `nextDouble()` | `double` | A double |
| `nextBoolean()` | `boolean` | A boolean |
| `next().charAt(0)` | `char` | A single character (workaround) |
| `hasNextInt()` | `boolean` | Tests if next token is an int |
| `hasNextLine()` | `boolean` | Tests if another line exists |
| `close()` | `void` | Closes the scanner |

### 2.4 KTU Cheat Sheet — BufferedReader + InputStreamReader

| Method | Returns | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `readLine()` | `String` | Reads one line as a String |
| `read()` | `int` | Reads one character (returns -1 at EOF) |
| `close()` | `void` | Closes the stream |

> [!WARNING]
> **`Scanner.nextLine()` Trap**: Calling `nextInt()` followed by `nextLine()` causes `nextLine()` to read the **leftover newline character** from the previous `nextInt()` call, producing an **empty string**. Always consume the leftover newline with an extra `sc.nextLine()` call.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Scanner** is used in academic projects, command-line utilities, and beginner applications.
- **BufferedReader** is used in **competitive programming** (e.g., CodeChef, HackerRank) and in enterprise batch processing.
- **Console** class is used in **authentication systems**, **password validators**, and **secure shell tools**.
- `printf`-style formatting is the basis of **logging frameworks** (Log4j, SLF4J) used in production-grade Java EE applications.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Code Walkthroughs & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Walkthrough 1 — Basic `println` Output

```java
public class OutputDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        System.out.print("Hello ");
        System.out.print("World");
        System.out.println("!");
        System.out.println("Welcome to KTU Java");
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step analysis:**

| Step | Statement | Action | Cursor Position After |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | `print("Hello ")` | Writes "Hello " | Right after the space |
| 2 | `print("World")` | Appends "World" | Right after "World" |
| 3 | `println("!")` | Writes "!" then newline | Beginning of new line |
| 4 | `println("Welcome to KTU Java")` | Writes the string + newline | Beginning of new line |

**Output produced:**
```
Hello World!
Welcome to KTU Java
```

### 3.2 Walkthrough 2 — `printf` Format Specifier Breakdown

```java
int rollNo = 42;
String name = "Anand";
float marks = 87.4567f;

System.out.printf("Roll:%d, Name:%-10s, Marks:%.2f%n", rollNo, name, marks);
```

**Detailed analysis of the format string `"Roll:%d, Name:%-10s, Marks:%.2f%n"`:**

| Substring | Type | Meaning | Maps to |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `Roll:` | Literal | Printed as-is | — |
| `%d` | Integer specifier | Prints integer | `rollNo = 42` |
| `, Name:` | Literal | Printed as-is | — |
| `%-10s` | String specifier | Left-justified, width 10 | `name = "Anand"` |
| `, Marks:` | Literal | Printed as-is | — |
| `%.2f` | Float specifier | 2 digits after decimal | `marks = 87.4567` |
| `%n` | Newline | Platform-independent newline | — |

**Substitution evaluation:**

$$ \text{Output} = \text{"Roll:42, Name:Anand"}+(\text{4 spaces to fill width 10})+\text{", Marks:87.46"} $$

$$\text{Final Output} = \text{"Roll:42, Name:Anand    , Marks:87.46"}$$

### 3.3 Walkthrough 3 — `Scanner` Reading Mixed Inputs

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class StudentInput {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

        System.out.print("Enter roll number: ");
        int roll = sc.nextInt();

        System.out.print("Enter name: ");
        sc.nextLine();              // Consume leftover newline
        String name = sc.nextLine();

        System.out.print("Enter CGPA: ");
        double cgpa = sc.nextDouble();

        System.out.printf("Hello %s (Roll %d), your CGPA is %.2f%n", name, roll, cgpa);
        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Sample Input:**
```
Enter roll number: 101
Enter name: Rahul Krishnan
Enter CGPA: 8.76
```

**Sample Output:**
```
Hello Rahul Krishnan (Roll 101), your CGPA is 8.76
```

**Why is `sc.nextLine()` needed before reading the name?**

$$ \text{Buffer after nextInt()} = \text{"101\n"} $$

$$ \text{nextLine() reads the leftover } "\n" \rightarrow \text{empty string consumed} $$

$$ \text{Next nextLine() reads "Rahul Krishnan" correctly} $$

### 3.4 Walkthrough 4 — `BufferedReader` Method

```java
import java.io.*;

public class BRDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
        BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(new InputStreamReader(System.in));

        System.out.print("Enter your age: ");
        int age = Integer.parseInt(br.readLine());

        System.out.print("Enter your salary: ");
        double sal = Double.parseDouble(br.readLine());

        System.out.println("Age: " + age + ", Salary: " + sal);
        br.close();
    }
}
```

**Analytical comparison — why `parseInt` is mandatory:**

`br.readLine()` returns a `String` because the underlying `InputStreamReader` decodes raw **bytes** into **characters**. Since Java does not know whether the user typed a number or a word, it returns the safest type (`String`). The `Integer.parseInt()` wrapper method converts this string into a primitive `int` by parsing each character.

$$ \text{"25"} \xrightarrow{\text{parseInt}} \boxed{25}_{\text{int}} $$

$$ \text{"25"} \xrightarrow{\text{parseDouble}} \boxed{25.0}_{\text{double}} $$

### 3.5 Walkthrough 5 — `Console` Class for Password

```java
import java.io.Console;

public class PasswordDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Console c = System.console();
        if (c == null) {
            System.err.println("Console not available. Run from command prompt.");
            return;
        }
        char[] pass = c.readPassword("Enter password: ");
        System.out.println("Password length: " + pass.length);
    }
}
```

**Key points:**
- `readPassword()` returns a `char[]` (not `String`) so the password can be **overwritten in memory** after use, reducing security risk.
- `System.console()` returns `null` when invoked from an IDE — this is why the null-check is essential.

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Three Input Methods

| Property | `Scanner` | `BufferedReader` | `Console` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Package | `java.util` | `java.io` | `java.io` |
| Speed | Slow (parses input) | **Fast** (buffered) | Fast |
| Reads primitives | **Yes (direct)** | No (needs `parseXxx`) | Limited |
| Thread-safe | No | **Yes** | **Yes** |
| Works in IDE | **Yes** | **Yes** | No (command-line only) |
| KTU Exam Frequency | **Very High** | Medium | Low |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flowchart — How Console Input is Processed

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Starts] --> B[Import java.util.Scanner]
    B --> C[Create Scanner object sc equals new Scanner System.in]
    C --> D[Display prompt using System.out.print]
    D --> E[Call nextXxx method e.g. nextInt]
    E --> F{Is input a valid type?}
    F -->|Yes| G[Store value in variable]
    F -->|No| H[Throw InputMismatchException]
    G --> I[Process the data]
    I --> J{More inputs needed?}
    J -->|Yes| D
    J -->|No| K[Call sc.close]
    H --> L[Handle exception with try catch]
    L --> D
    K --> M[Program Ends]
```

### 4.2 Diagram — Java I/O Class Hierarchy (Console I/O Relevant)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[java.io.InputStream] --> B[System.in static field]
    A --> C[FilterInputStream]
    C --> D[BufferedInputStream]
    B --> E[InputStreamReader]
    E --> F[FileReader]
    E --> G[BufferedReader]
    G --> H[LineNumberReader]

    I[java.io.PrintStream] --> J[System.out static field]
    I --> K[System.err static field]

    L[java.util.Scanner] --> M[Uses System.in internally]
    N[java.io.Console] --> O[Used for secure password input]
```

### 4.3 Process Diagram — `printf` Formatting Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Format String e.g. Hello s you are d years old] --> B[Java scans for percent symbols]
    B --> C[Match each specifier to an argument]
    C --> D[Apply width and precision flags]
    D --> E[Convert argument to string representation]
    E --> F[Concatenate literals and formatted values]
    F --> G[Write final string to System.out]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — BufferedReader Wrapping Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Keyboard raw bytes e.g. 72 101 108 108 111] --> B[System.in InputStream]
    B --> C[InputStreamReader converts bytes to chars]
    C --> D[BufferedReader buffers entire line]
    D --> E[readLine returns String]
    E --> F[Integer.parseInt converts to int]
    F --> G[Primitive int value stored in variable]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**Differentiate between `print()`, `println()`, and `printf()` methods in Java with one example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Method | Description | Example | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `print()` | Prints text without moving to a new line. | `System.out.print("Hi");` | `Hi` (cursor stays on same line) |
| `println()` | Prints text and moves the cursor to the next line. | `System.out.println("Hi");` | `Hi` followed by newline |
| `printf()` | Prints using formatted output with `%` specifiers. | `System.out.printf("Value=%d", 10);` | `Value=10` |

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`**
**Explain the role of the `Scanner` class. Write a Java statement to read a double value from the console.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The `Scanner` class, defined in `java.util` package, is used to **read formatted input** from `System.in` (the keyboard). It provides methods like `nextInt()`, `nextDouble()`, `nextLine()`, etc., that automatically parse tokens into the appropriate data type.

```java
import java.util.Scanner;
Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
double price = sc.nextDouble();
```

---

### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

## **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]`

### (a) Explain the `Scanner` class methods for reading different data types from the console. Write a complete Java program that reads a student's roll number (int), name (String), and CGPA (float) and displays them using `printf()`. **(7 marks)**

### (b) Explain the `printf()` format specifiers `%d`, `%s`, `%f`, `%n`, and `%.2f` with examples. What is the difference between `%n` and `\n`? **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A (a)

**Scanner Class Methods:**

| Method | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- |
| `nextInt()` | Reads an integer |
| `nextFloat()` | Reads a float |
| `nextDouble()` | Reads a double |
| `next()` | Reads a single word (token) |
| `nextLine()` | Reads an entire line |
| `hasNextInt()` | Checks if next token is an int |

**Complete Java Program:**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class StudentInfo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);

        System.out.print("Enter Roll Number: ");
        int roll = sc.nextInt();

        System.out.print("Enter Name: ");
        sc.nextLine();                      // Consume leftover newline
        String name = sc.nextLine();

        System.out.print("Enter CGPA: ");
        float cgpa = sc.nextFloat();

        System.out.printf("Roll: %d%n", roll);
        System.out.printf("Name: %s%n", name);
        System.out.printf("CGPA: %.2f%n", cgpa);

        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Sample Output (for input 101, Aswathi, 8.95):**
```
Roll: 101
Name: Aswathi
CGPA: 8.95
```

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Q.A (b)

**Format Specifier Explanations:**

| Specifier | Data Type | Example Statement | Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `%d` | Decimal integer | `printf("%d", 42)` | `42` |
| `%s` | String | `printf("%s", "KTU")` | `KTU` |
| `%f` | Floating-point | `printf("%f", 3.14)` | `3.140000` |
| `%.2f` | Float with 2 decimals | `printf("%.2f", 3.14159)` | `3.14` |
| `%n` | Platform-independent newline | `printf("A%nB")` | `A` (newline) `B` |

**Difference between `%n` and `\n`:**

`\n` is a **literal newline character** (LF on Unix, CR+LF on Windows) that may cause double line breaks on Windows. `%n` is interpreted by the JVM and produces the **correct newline sequence for the host operating system**, making the program **portable**.

$$ \%n \rightarrow \begin{cases} \backslash n & \text{on Linux/Mac} \\ \backslash r \backslash n & \text{on Windows} \end{cases} $$

---

## **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified]`

### (a) Compare `Scanner` and `BufferedReader` for console input in Java. Write a Java program that uses `BufferedReader` to read two integers and display their sum. **(7 marks)**

### (b) Explain the `Console` class in Java. Write a program to demonstrate password input using `Console.readPassword()`. Why does it return `char[]` instead of `String`? **(7 marks)**

---

### ✅ Model Solution — Q.B (a)

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | `Scanner` | `BufferedReader` |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Package | `java.util` | `java.io` |
| Parsing | Automatic (`nextInt()`) | Manual (`parseInt()`) |
| Speed | Slower | **Faster** (buffered) |
| Thread-safe | No | **Yes** |
| Whitespace handling | Splits on whitespace | Reads full line |

**Java Program using `BufferedReader`:**

```java
import java.io.*;

public class SumDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) throws IOException {
```
