**(b)** Write a Java program to demonstrate method overriding. Explain how runtime polymorphism is achieved in your program. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Concept:** Method overloading allows multiple methods in the **same class** to share the **same name** but differ in the **parameter list** (number, type, or order of arguments). The compiler identifies the correct method at **compile time**, hence it is called **static polymorphism**.

**Ways of Achieving Method Overloading:**

1. **Different Number of Parameters:**
```java
class MathOp {
    int add(int a, int b)         { return a + b; }
    int add(int a, int b, int c)  { return a + b + c; }
}
```

2. **Different Types of Parameters:**
```java
class MathOp {
    int add(int a, int b)         { return a + b; }
    double add(double a, double b){ return a + b; }
}
```

3. **Different Order of Parameters:**
```java
class Display {
    void show(int a, String b) { System.out.println(a + " " + b); }
    void show(String a, int b) { System.out.println(a + " " + b); }
}
```

**Key Rule:** Return type **alone** cannot differentiate overloaded methods.

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Java Program Demonstrating Method Overriding:**
```java
class Shape {
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic shape");
    }
    double area() {
        return 0.0;
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    double radius;
    Circle(double r) { this.radius = r; }

    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Circle");
    }

    @Override
    double area() {
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }
}

class Square extends Shape {
    double side;
    Square(double s) { this.side = s; }

    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Square");
    }

    @Override
    double area() {
        return side * side;
    }
}

public class ShapeDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s;

        s = new Circle(5.0);
        s.draw();
        System.out.println("Area: " + s.area());

        s = new Square(4.0);
        s.draw();
        System.out.println("Area: " + s.area());
    }
}
```

**Explanation of Runtime Polymorphism:**

In the program above, the reference `s` is of type `Shape` (parent), but it holds **different child objects** at different times (`Circle` then `Square`). When `s.draw()` or `s.area()` is called, the **compiler cannot determine** at compile time which version of the method will execute, because the object type is known only during execution.

The **JVM**, at runtime, inspects the **actual object** referenced by `s`, consults the **virtual method table (vtable)**, and dispatches the call to the **most-specific (overridden) method**. This is **dynamic method dispatch** — the heart of runtime polymorphism.

**Output:**
```
Drawing a Circle
Area: 78.53981633974483
Drawing a Square
Area: 16.0
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Differentiate between method overloading and method overriding in Java. Create a Java class `Employee` with overloaded constructors and a method `calculateSalary()` that is overridden in two subclasses `PermanentEmployee` and `ContractEmployee`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is dynamic method dispatch? Explain with a diagram how the JVM resolves overridden method calls at runtime. **(7 marks)**

#### **Solution to (a):**

**Difference Table:**

| **Aspect** | **Method Overloading** | **Method Overriding** |
|---|---|---|
| Class Scope | Within the same class | Between parent and child classes |
| Signature | Must differ | Must be identical |
| Polymorphism Type | Compile-time | Run-time |
| Inheritance | Not required | Required |
| Access Modifiers | No restriction | Cannot be more restrictive |
| `@Override` Annotation | Not used | Recommended |

**Java Program:**
```java
class Employee {
    String name;
    int id;

    // Constructor 1: No args
    Employee() {
        this.name = "Unknown";
        this.id = 0;
    }

    // Constructor 2: With name
    Employee(String name) {
        this.name = name;
        this.id = 0;
    }

    // Constructor 3: With name and id
    Employee(String name, int id) {
        this.name = name;
        this.id = id;
    }

    double calculateSalary() {
        return 0.0;
    }
}

class PermanentEmployee extends Employee {
    double basicPay;

    PermanentEmployee(String name, int id, double basicPay) {
        super(name, id);
        this.basicPay = basicPay;
    }

    @Override
    double calculateSalary() {
        return basicPay + (basicPay * 0.40) + (basicPay * 0.15);
    }
}

class ContractEmployee extends Employee {
    double hourlyRate;
    int hours;

    ContractEmployee(String name, int id, double hourlyRate, int hours) {
        super(name, id);
        this.hourlyRate = hourlyRate;
        this.hours = hours;
    }

    @Override
    double calculateSalary() {
        return hourlyRate * hours;
    }
}

public class PayrollDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Employee e1 = new PermanentEmployee("Arun", 101, 50000);
        Employee e2 = new ContractEmployee("Meera", 102, 500, 160);

        System.out.println("Arun's Salary: " + e1.calculateSalary());
        System.out.println("Meera's Salary: " + e2.calculateSalary());
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Arun's Salary: 77500.0
Meera's Salary: 80000.0
```

The `Employee` class demonstrates **constructor overloading** (3 versions), and `calculateSalary()` is **overridden** in both subclasses to exhibit **runtime polymorphism**.

#### **Solution to (b):**

**Dynamic Method Dispatch** is the mechanism by which a call to an **overridden method** is resolved **at runtime** based on the **actual object type** being referenced, not the declared reference type.

**JVM Resolution Process:**

```
[1] Code: Shape s = new Circle();
[2] Call: s.draw();
        |
        v
[3] Compiler: emits invokevirtual with symbolic ref to Shape.draw()
        |
        v
[4] Runtime: JVM extracts object reference from local variable 's'
        |
        v
[5] JVM: Inspects object's class (Circle) in heap
        |
        v
[6] JVM: Looks up 'draw' in Circle's vtable -> FOUND
        |
        v
[7] JVM: Invokes Circle.draw() directly (skips Shape.draw())
```

**ASCII Diagram — Method Table Lookup:**

```
Object Header (Heap)
+---------------------------+
| Class Pointer: Circle     |
+---------------------------+
| Fields: radius = 5.0      |
+---------------------------+

Circle's vtable
+---------------------------+
| Slot 0: Object             |
| Slot 1: Shape.draw() ------+---> Circle.draw()  (overridden!)
| Slot 2: Shape.area() ------+---> Circle.area()  (overridden!)
+---------------------------+

JVM follows the vtable pointer and dispatches to the child version.
```

This **late binding** ensures that the correct overridden method is executed, even if the reference type is the parent class.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing Overloading with Overriding:** Writing "Overloading happens between parent and child classes" costs **2 marks** instantly. Overloading = same class, different parameters.
> 2. **Return Type Confusion:** Writing that "return type can be different in overriding" is **wrong** (except covariant return from Java 5+). Examiners deduct 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting `@Override` Annotation:** While not a compile error, the **absence of `@Override`** in a 7-mark program is considered **bad practice** and may cost 0.5–1 mark for "code quality".
> 4. **Static Method Misconception:** Writing "static methods can be overridden" is a **common KTU trap**. The correct term is **method hiding**, not overriding.
> 5. **Missing `super` in Output:** When asked to demonstrate `super.start()` calling parent, students often forget to show **both** outputs (parent's and child's).
> 6. **Confusing Binding Phases:** Always explicitly mention **early binding (compile-time)** vs **late binding (run-time)** — this 2-mark differentiator is the most-tested KTU concept.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Polymorphism** = "One interface, many implementations."
- ✅ **Method Overloading** = Same class, **same method name**, **different parameters**, resolved at **compile time** (static binding).
- ✅ **Method Overriding** = Parent-child inheritance, **same signature**, resolved at **runtime** (dynamic binding via vtable).
- ✅ Overloading can differ by: **number**, **type**, or **order** of parameters.
- ✅ Overriding requires: **same name, same parameters, same (or covariant) return type**.
- ✅ Overriding **cannot reduce** access visibility (`public` → `private` is illegal).
- ✅ `final`, `static`, and `private` methods **cannot be overridden** (they can be hidden or not inherited).
- ✅ The `@Override` annotation provides **compile-time safety** against signature mismatches.
- ✅ Use `super.methodName()` to invoke the **parent's overridden method** from the child class.
- ✅ **Constructors can be overloaded** but **never overridden**.
- ✅ JVM uses **`invokevirtual`** instruction for overridden methods and **vtable lookup** for dynamic dispatch.
- ✅ **Run-time polymorphism is the foundation** of frameworks like Spring, JavaFX, and the Java Collection API.
- ✅ **KTU 2024 exam tip:** Always pair the **definition** with the **mechanism (binding phase)** — a 3-mark question expects both.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dynamic Method Dispatch
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT611 - OBJECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING IN JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dynamic Method Dispatch

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Dynamic Method Dispatch (DMD)** is the mechanism by which a call to an **overridden** instance method is resolved at **runtime** (not at compile time) based on the **actual runtime type of the object** being referred to, rather than the declared type of the reference variable. It is the direct implementation of **Runtime Polymorphism** in Java and is fundamentally tied to the concept of **late binding**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, Dynamic Method Dispatch is treated as the *practical machinery* that powers polymorphic behavior. The Java Virtual Machine (JVM) — specifically the **invokevirtual** bytecode instruction — is responsible for performing this runtime lookup inside the **method table (vtable)** of the actual object's class.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Universal Remote Control"

Imagine a **TV Remote Control** with a single button labelled **"Power"**.

- The *remote* is your **reference variable** of type `TV`.
- You can point the remote at a `SonyTV`, an `LGTV`, or a `MiTV`. The remote doesn't know or care which brand is on the other end.
- When you press **"Power"**, what actually happens is determined by the **actual TV** sitting in front of you (the *object*).

If the `SonyTV` has a *smart power-on sequence* (LED glow → logo → channel), the remote triggers that. If it's a vintage `CRT TV`, it simply clicks on. The **same button press** produces **different results** based on the *actual receiving device*.

This is exactly Dynamic Method Dispatch: **one interface (reference), many implementations (objects), with the JVM deciding the real action at the moment of the call**.

### 1.3 The Three Pillars of DMD

> [!NOTE]
> For Dynamic Method Dispatch to be valid in Java, **all three** of these must hold simultaneously:
>
> 1. **Inheritance** must exist (a parent–child class relationship).
> 2. **Method overriding** must have occurred (same signature in subclass).
> 3. **Upcasting** must be in effect (parent reference → child object).

### 1.4 Why DMD Matters — KTU 2024 Engineering Perspective

In real-world software engineering (think: a payment gateway supporting `CreditCard`, `UPI`, `NetBanking`), you want to write a **single piece of processing logic** that works seamlessly across dozens of payment types added in the future. DMD gives you that **Open/Closed Principle** — code *open to extension, closed to modification*.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Step-by-Step Mechanics of DMD

Let us deconstruct what happens internally when the JVM encounters a polymorphic call:

- **Step 1 — Compile Time Check:** The Java compiler verifies that the *declared type* of the reference contains the called method. If not, **compilation fails** (e.g., `cannot find symbol`).
- **Step 2 — Bytecode Generation:** The compiler emits an `invokevirtual` instruction (NOT `invokespecial` or `invokestatic`). This instruction is the marker that says *"defer this decision to runtime"*.
- **Step 3 — Runtime Object Identification:** When the line executes, the JVM inspects the **heap object** the reference points to and identifies its **concrete class**.
- **Step 4 — Vtable Lookup:** The JVM consults the **method table (vtable)** of the concrete class. Each class in Java has a vtable mapping method signatures to actual code addresses.
- **Step 5 — Method Invocation:** The JVM invokes the method found in the vtable — which is the **overriding** version in the subclass.
- **Step 6 — Stack Frame Push:** A new stack frame is created and the method executes. Crucially, *only the method is dispatched dynamically; the access modifiers, return type, and exception specifications are statically bound.*

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Rule

| Rule | Reason (The "Why") |
|---|---|
| Only **overridden** methods participate | Overloading is resolved at compile time (static binding) since the *signature itself* is different — no ambiguity exists. |
| **Static** methods are excluded | Static methods belong to the *class*, not the *object*. They are bound at compile time based on the reference type. |
| **Private** methods are excluded | Private methods are not inherited, hence cannot be overridden, hence cannot be polymorphically dispatched. |
| **Final** methods are excluded | The `final` keyword forbids overriding, eliminating the very ambiguity DMD resolves. |
| **Constructors** are excluded | Constructors are not members that can be inherited/overridden in the polymorphic sense. |
| **Fields (instance variables)** are excluded | Fields in Java are **never** virtual. They are always resolved by reference type — a classic KTU trap! |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write `super.speak()` and believe DMD still applies. It does **not** — `super` is a *compile-time* reference to the parent, and the call is bound statically. Similarly, accessing a field via a parent reference always returns the *parent's* field value, regardless of the child's value.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield "Formula" Sheet (Rules Cheat Sheet)

| # | Condition | DMD Applies? | Binding Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Overridden **instance** method, parent ref, child object | **Yes** | Late (Runtime) |
| 2 | Overloaded method (different signature) | No | Early (Compile-time) |
| 3 | Static method called via parent reference | No | Early (Compile-time) |
| 4 | Final method | No | Early (Compile-time) |
| 5 | Private method | No | Early (Compile-time) |
| 6 | Instance variable access | No | Early (Compile-time) |
| 7 | Constructor invocation | No | Early (Compile-time) |
| 8 | `super.method()` call | No | Early (Compile-time) |
| 9 | `this.method()` inside a method | Conditional | Runtime if overridden, else compile-time |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

DMD is the backbone of virtually every Java framework:

- **Java Collections Framework:** `List myList = new ArrayList<>();` — the *reference* is `List`, but adding elements may invoke `ArrayList`-specific overridden behavior.
- **JDBC:** `Connection con = DriverManager.getConnection(url);` — the returned `Connection` is actually an implementation-specific object (e.g., Oracle, MySQL) whose overridden methods execute at runtime.
- **Spring Framework:** Dependency injection returns parent-interface references holding child beans; all `@Override`d methods dispatch dynamically.
- **GUI Event Handling:** A single `ActionListener` reference can point to anonymous inner classes, each executing a unique action.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Trace Analysis & Analytical Matrix

Since Java Dynamic Method Dispatch is a *behavioral* concept, we replace algebraic derivations with **exhaustive code traces** — the KTU-equivalent analytical method for OO programming.

### 3.1 Canonical DMD Program

```java
class Shape {
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a generic Shape");
    }
    double area() {
        return 0.0;
    }
}

class Circle extends Shape {
    double radius = 5.0;
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Circle");
    }
    @Override
    double area() {
        return 3.14159 * radius * radius;
    }
}

class Rectangle extends Shape {
    double length = 4.0, width = 6.0;
    @Override
    void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Rectangle");
    }
    @Override
    double area() {
        return length * width;
    }
}

public class DispatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s;        // Step A: Reference declared of type Shape

        s = new Circle();       // Step B: Upcasting (implicit)
        s.draw();               // Step C: DMD in action
        System.out.println(s.area());

        s = new Rectangle();    // Step D: Re-assignment
        s.draw();               // Step E: DMD resolves to Rectangle
        System.out.println(s.area());

        s = new Shape();        // Step F: Pure parent object
        s.draw();               // Step G: No override possible
    }
}
```

### 3.2 Exhaustive Line-by-Line Trace Table

| Execution Line | Reference `s` Type | Object's Actual Class | Method Called | DMD Resolves To | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `Shape s;` | `Shape` | `null` (uninitialized) | — | — | — |
| `s = new Circle();` | `Shape` | `Circle` | — | — | — |
| `s.draw();` | `Shape` | `Circle` | `draw()` | **`Circle.draw()`** | `Drawing a Circle` |
| `s.area();` | `Shape` | `Circle` | `area()` | **`Circle.area()`** | `78.53975` |
| `s = new Rectangle();` | `Shape` | `Rectangle` | — | — | — |
| `s.draw();` | `Shape` | `Rectangle` | `draw()` | **`Rectangle.draw()`** | `Drawing a Rectangle` |
| `s.area();` | `Shape` | `Rectangle` | `area()` | **`Rectangle.area()`** | `24.0` |
| `s = new Shape();` | `Shape` | `Shape` | — | — | — |
| `s.draw();` | `Shape` | `Shape` | `draw()` | **`Shape.draw()`** | `Drawing a generic Shape` |

### 3.3 The "Why" Behind the Trace — Detailed Reasoning

**Line-by-line logical justification:**

- At the *declaration* `Shape s;`, the compiler allocates space on the stack for a reference of type `Shape`. The reference can *only* legally point to a `Shape` object or any subclass thereof.
- The assignment `s = new Circle();` involves **implicit upcasting**. The Circle object is constructed on the heap, and the reference `s` stores its memory address. The *static type* is `Shape`; the *dynamic type* is `Circle`.
- When `s.draw();` is encountered, the compiler emits an `invokevirtual` instruction. At runtime, the JVM extracts the dynamic type (`Circle`) from the object header, fetches the `Circle` vtable entry for `draw()`, and dispatches to that method. The **static type** `Shape` is *never consulted* for method selection — only for access legality checks.
- `s.area();` follows the same pattern. The numeric return value is calculated using `Circle.radius = 5.0`, yielding $$3.14159 \times 5.0 \times 5.0 = 78.53975$$.
- The re-assignment `s = new Rectangle();` simply overwrites the reference. The *original* Circle object becomes eligible for garbage collection (if no other references exist).
- The final reassignment to `new Shape()` produces the base class output, proving that DMD always *ascends* to the most specific overriding implementation — and falls back to the parent's own method when no override exists.

### 3.4 The "Trap" Variation — Field Access via Parent Reference

```java
class Parent {
    int value = 10;
    void show() { System.out.println("Parent.show, value=" + value); }
}
class Child extends Parent {
    int value = 20;        // HIDES the parent's value (does NOT override)
    @Override
    void show() { System.out.println("Child.show, value=" + value); }
}

public class FieldTrap {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Parent p = new Child();
        System.out.println(p.value);   // ??
        p.show();                       // ??
    }
}
```

**Analytical Trace:**

| Statement | Output | Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| `p.value` | `10` | **Fields are NOT virtual.** Java binds field access to the *static type* `Parent`, not the runtime object `Child`. This is the most-tested KTU trap. |
| `p.show()` | `Child.show, value=20` | Methods ARE virtual. DMD routes to `Child.show()`. Inside, `value` refers to `Child.value = 20` (Java's scoping rule, not DMD). |

### 3.5 Multilevel DMD Trace

```java
class A { void m() { System.out.println("A.m"); } }
class B extends A { @Override void m() { System.out.println("B.m"); } }
class C extends B { @Override void m() { System.out.println("C.m"); } }

public class MultiLevel {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        A ref = new C();   // A → B → C
        ref.m();           // ??
    }
}
```

**Trace:**

The JVM walks the inheritance chain **upward** starting from the object's actual class `C`:
- Does `C` define `m()`? **Yes** → dispatch to `C.m()`. The chain search stops here.

**Output:** `C.m`

If `C` had *not* overridden `m()`, the search would continue to `B`, then to `A`. This **bottom-up vtable walk** is the algorithm DMD uses.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 DMD Mechanism — JVM Internal Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Source Code: ref.draw] --> B[Java Compiler javac]
    B --> C[Bytecode: invokevirtual Shape.draw]
    C --> D{Program Execution Begins}
    D --> E[JVM Reads invokevirtual]
    E --> F[Extract Object Reference from Stack]
    F --> G[Read Object Header for Runtime Class ID]
    G --> H[Locate Vtable of Runtime Class]
    H --> I{Method Overridden in Subclass?}
    I --> J[Yes] --> K[Invoke Overriding Method]
    I --> L[No] --> M[Invoke Parent Method]
    K --> N[Push New Stack Frame and Execute]
    M --> N
    N --> O[Return to Caller]
```

### 4.2 DMD Decision Flowchart — Is Dynamic Binding Triggered?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Method Call Encountered] --> Q1{Is it an instance method?}
    Q1 --> N1[No: Static Binding] --> End1[Resolved at Compile Time]
    Q1 --> Y1[Yes] --> Q2{Is it private, static, or final?}
    Q2 --> Y2[Yes: Static Binding] --> End1
    Q2 --> N2[No] --> Q3{Is it overridden in the actual object class?}
    Q3 --> N3[No: Static Binding via parent class] --> End1
    Q3 --> Y3[Yes] --> Result[Dynamic Method Dispatch: Late Binding]
    Result --> Result2[Resolved at Runtime via Vtable]
```

### 4.3 Vtable Architecture Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VTable_A[Class A Vtable]
        M_A[m --> address of A.m]
    end
    subgraph VTable_B[Class B Vtable inherits from A]
        M_B[m --> address of B.m]
    end
    subgraph VTable_C[Class C Vtable inherits from B]
        M_C[m --> address of C.m]
    end
    Object_C["Object of type C"] --> VTable_C
    VTable_C -- if not found --> VTable_B
    VTable_B -- if not found --> VTable_A
```

### 4.4 Polymorphic Collection — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Client[Client Code: Shape arr] --> A1["arr[0] = new Circle"]
    Client --> A2["arr[1] = new Rectangle"]
    Client --> A3["arr[2] = new Triangle"]
    A1 --> Loop[For Each Shape in arr]
    A2 --> Loop
    A3 --> Loop
    Loop --> Call[s.draw and s.area]
    Call --> DMD{Dynamic Method Dispatch Engine}
    DMD --> R1[Circle Implementation]
    DMD --> R2[Rectangle Implementation]
    DMD --> R3[Triangle Implementation]
    R1 --> Out[Polymorphic Output Stream]
    R2 --> Out
    R3 --> Out
```

This block-level architecture illustrates the **Open/Closed Principle** in action: a single client loop (which never changes) dispatches work to many extensible shape implementations (which can be added without modifying the client).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Dynamic Method Dispatch. State any two conditions necessary for Dynamic Method Dispatch to occur in Java.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Dynamic Method Dispatch is the mechanism in Java by which a call to an overridden method is resolved at runtime, based on the actual type of the object referred to by a parent class reference, rather than the type of the reference variable. It is the implementation of runtime polymorphism and is achieved through late binding.
>
> **Conditions (1 mark — any two):**
> 1. There must be an inheritance relationship between two classes.
> 2. The subclass must override an instance method inherited from the parent.
> 3. The method must be invoked through a parent-class reference holding a child-class object (upcasting).

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Why don't static methods participate in Dynamic Method Dispatch? Can a `final` method be overridden to achieve polymorphism? Justify.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Static methods (2 marks):** Static methods belong to the *class* rather than to any individual *object*. They are loaded and bound at compile time based on the reference type. Since there is no per-object resolution needed, the JVM cannot — and does not need to — perform a vtable lookup. Hence, static methods are statically bound and cannot participate in DMD.
>
> **Final methods (1 mark):** No. The `final` modifier explicitly prevents method overriding. Since DMD fundamentally requires an overridden method to create ambiguity, a final method is bound at compile time and is excluded from the polymorphism mechanism.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of Dynamic Method Dispatch in Java with a suitable program. Discuss how it differs from static binding. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Concept (3 marks):**
Dynamic Method Dispatch (DMD) is Java's runtime mechanism for resolving calls to overridden instance methods. When a parent-class reference variable holds a child-class object, the compiler cannot decide at compile time which version of the overridden method to invoke, because the same reference could point to different child objects during the program's lifetime. The JVM defers this decision to runtime by inspecting the actual class of the object and consulting its vtable. This is also called **late binding**.

**Program (3 marks):**

```java
class Vehicle {
    void speed() { System.out.println("Vehicle: generic speed"); }
}
class Car extends Vehicle {
    @Override
    void speed() { System.out.println("Car: 120 km/h"); }
}
class Bike extends Vehicle {
    @Override
    void speed() { System.out.println("Bike: 80 km/h"); }
}
public class DMDTest {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Vehicle v;
        v = new Car(); v.speed();
        v = new Bike(); v.speed();
        v = new Vehicle(); v.speed();
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Car: 120 km/h
Bike: 80 km/h
Vehicle: generic speed
```

**Difference Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | Static Binding | Dynamic Method Dispatch |
|---|---|---|
| When resolved | Compile time | Runtime |
| Applies to | Static, private, final, overloaded methods | Overridden instance methods |
| Mechanism | Direct method address known | Vtable lookup at runtime |
| Flexibility | Lower | Higher (supports polymorphism) |

**(b)** Consider the following code. Predict the output and justify each line with the concept of Dynamic Method Dispatch. **(7 marks)**

```java
class Base {
    void display() { System.out.println("Base.display"); }
    static void staticShow() { System.out.println("Base.staticShow"); }
    int x = 100;
}
class Derived extends Base {
    @Override
    void display() { System.out.println("Derived.display, x=" + x); }
    static void staticShow() { System.out.println("Derived.staticShow"); }
    int x = 200;
}
public class Exam {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Base b = new Derived();
        b.display();
        b.staticShow();
        System.out.println("b.x = " + b.x);
    }
}
```

**Model Solution:**

**Output (2 marks):**
```
Derived.display, x=200
Base.staticShow
b.x = 100
```

**Justification (5 marks — distributed as below):**

- **`b.display()` → `Derived.display, x=200` (2 marks):** This is classic DMD. The method `display` is an *overridden instance* method. The JVM identifies the runtime type as `Derived`, locates `display` in the `Derived` vtable, and dispatches to `Derived.display()`. Inside that method, `x` refers to the *current object's* `x` field, which is `Derived.x = 200` due to Java's scoping rule (not DMD).

- **`b.staticShow()` → `Base.staticShow` (2 marks):** Static methods are bound at compile time. The compiler sees the *reference type* `Base` and binds the call to `Base.staticShow()`. The JVM does **not** perform a vtable lookup for static methods. This is a deliberate design choice to prevent method hiding from being treated as overriding.

- **`b.x` → `100` (1 mark):** Fields are **never virtual** in Java. The expression `b.x` is bound to the reference type `Base`, accessing `Base.x = 100`. Even though the object is a `Derived` with its own `x = 200`, the parent reference cannot see it. To access `200`, one would need a `Derived` reference or to invoke a method.

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Sample, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain how Dynamic Method Dispatch is implemented internally by the JVM using vtables. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

- **Vtable Concept (3 marks):** Each class loaded by the JVM is associated with a *method table* (vtable) — a runtime data structure that maps method signatures to actual code addresses. When a class is loaded, the JVM creates its vtable by copying the parent's vtable and overwriting entries for any methods it overrides.
- **Lookup Algorithm (2 marks):** When `invokevirtual` is executed, the JVM:
  1. Extracts the object reference from the operand stack.
  2. Reads the object's class pointer from the object header.
  3. Searches the vtable of that class for the method signature.
  4. If found, invokes the method at the recorded address; if not, walks up the inheritance chain.
- **Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.3 of these notes (the Vtable Architecture Diagram) for a clean visual representation.

**(b)** Write a Java program demonstrating Dynamic Method Dispatch using a three-level inheritance hierarchy: `Shape → Polygon → Triangle`. The `draw()` method should be overridden at each level. Show the output and explain the role of upcasting. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

```java
class Shape {
    void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing a generic Shape"); }
}
class Polygon extends Shape {
    @Override
    void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing a Polygon"); }
}
class Triangle extends Polygon {
    @Override
    void draw() { System.out.println("Drawing a Triangle with 3 sides"); }
}
public class Hierarchy {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s1 = new Triangle();   // two-level upcast
        Shape s2 = new Polygon();    // one-level upcast
        Shape s3 = new Shape();
        s1.draw();
        s2.draw();
        s3.draw();
    }
}
```

**Output (2 marks):**
```
Drawing a Triangle with 3 sides
Drawing a Polygon
Drawing a generic Shape
```

**Role of Upcasting (5 marks):** Upcasting is the *implicit* conversion of a subclass reference to a superclass reference — performed automatically by the Java compiler because it is type-safe (an `is-a` relationship). In this program, `new Triangle()` is upcast twice (Triangle → Polygon → Shape) and stored in a `Shape` reference. This is the *prerequisite* for DMD: the same reference type (`Shape`) can be re-pointed to any subclass object during execution. The JVM exploits this uniform reference to perform polymorphic dispatch — at each `draw()` call, it consults the actual object's vtable, walking from `Triangle`'s vtable up through `Polygon` to `Shape` if no override is found. This is why `s1.draw()` invokes `Triangle.draw()` and not `Polygon.draw()`, even though the static type is `Shape`.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks on DMD Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing overriding with overloading** — DMD applies ONLY to overriding. Writing about method overloading in a DMD answer will fetch partial credit at best.
> 2. **Forgetting that static methods are statically bound** — A common blunder is to claim DMD works for `static` methods because they "exist" in both classes. They do not participate in DMD.
> 3. **Claiming fields are virtual** — Writing `System.out.println(b.x)` and expecting `200` will cost you 1 full mark. Fields are resolved at compile time.
> 4. **Using `super` and expecting DMD** — `super.display()` is statically bound to the immediate parent's version. It is a common trap.
> 5. **Confusing DMD with method hiding** — When a subclass declares a static method with the same signature, it is *method hiding*, not overriding, and is statically bound.
> 6. **Omitting the `@Override` annotation** — While not a runtime requirement, KTU examiners often look for it as a sign of conceptual clarity. Mention it in answers when discussing method signatures.
> 7. **Failing to mention `invokevirtual` bytecode** — A 1-mark differentiator in KTU papers. Naming the JVM instruction shows deep understanding.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Dynamic Method Dispatch (DMD)** = Runtime resolution of overridden instance methods via the JVM's vtable.
- **Three prerequisites:** Inheritance + Method Overriding + Upcasting.
- **Trigger bytecode:** `invokevirtual` (NOT `invokespecial`, `invokestatic`, or `invokeinterface` exclusively — though interface calls also use `invokeinterface` which behaves polymorphically too).
- **Applies to:** Only overridden *instance* methods.
- **Does NOT apply to:** Static, private, final methods, fields, constructors, or `super` calls.
- **Fields are never virtual** in Java — a favorite KTU question.
- **Multilevel dispatch:** The vtable walk is *bottom-up*, starting from the actual object's class and ascending the inheritance chain.
- **Open/Closed Principle:** DMD enables software systems that are *open to extension* (new subclasses) but *closed to modification* (existing client code is unchanged).
- **Common Java APIs using DMD:** `ArrayList`, `HashMap`, `Connection` (JDBC), `ActionListener` (AWT/Swing), Spring beans.
- **Vtable creation timing:** At class loading time by the JVM, not at object instantiation time. Each class has *one* vtable shared by all its instances.
- **Always use `@Override` annotation** in exam programs to clearly signal that overriding is intentional — this earns you a goodwill mark from KTU examiners.
- **Mention `invokevirtual`** in long-answer questions for a competitive edge.
- **Trace carefully:** When the same reference is reassigned to different subclass objects, *DMD picks the most specific implementation* of the *current* pointed object on every call.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Abstract Classes and Interfaces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT611 - OBJECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING IN JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Abstract Classes and Interfaces

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Abstract Classes — Formal Definition

An **abstract class** in Java is a class declared with the `abstract` keyword that **cannot be instantiated directly** and may contain **abstract methods** (methods declared without a body) as well as **concrete methods** (fully implemented methods). It serves as an incomplete blueprint that subclasses must complete by providing implementations for all inherited abstract methods.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An abstract class is a *restricted class* that cannot be used to create objects; it must be **inherited** to be utilized. It is the ideal mechanism in Java to achieve **partial abstraction**.

### 1.2 Interfaces — Formal Definition

An **interface** in Java is a completely abstract reference type (since Java 8, also allowing `default` and `static` methods) that defines a **contract of methods** a class must implement. All methods in an interface are implicitly `public` and `abstract` (prior to Java 8). Variables declared in an interface are implicitly `public static final` (i.e., **constants**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** An interface represents a **100% abstraction** mechanism (pre-Java 8) and is the primary tool used to achieve **multiple inheritance** in Java, since Java classes cannot extend more than one class.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Abstract Class Analogy — "A Partially Built House":**
Imagine a housing blueprint. The walls, roof design, and floor plan are **finalized (concrete methods)**, but the *interior design* (kitchen layout, furniture) is **left for the buyer to decide (abstract methods)**. You cannot move into the "abstract house" directly — you must build a specific version (subclass) first.

**Interface Analogy — "A Job Role Contract":**
Think of a **"Software Engineer" job contract**. The contract lists the *tasks* a software engineer MUST do (e.g., `code()`, `test()`, `deploy()`) but specifies **no implementation** — how they do those tasks is up to the company that hires them. Any class that "signs" this contract (implements the interface) must define how those tasks are accomplished.

### 1.4 Key Mandatory Syntax Markers

- **Abstract class:** Declared using the keyword `abstract`
- **Abstract method:** Declared using the keyword `abstract` (no body, ends with `;`)
- **Interface:** Declared using the keyword `interface`
- **Implementation:** A class uses `extends` for abstract classes and `implements` for interfaces

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If a subclass **does not implement all abstract methods** inherited from its abstract parent, the subclass **must also be declared abstract**.

### 1.5 Why They Exist — Design Philosophy

Both abstract classes and interfaces support the OOP pillar of **Abstraction** (hiding implementation, showing only functionality). They enforce a **uniform contract** across diverse subclasses, making software systems modular, extensible, and maintainable — a core demand in KTU 2024 outcome-based assessment.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 Characteristics of an Abstract Class — Structured Logic

1. **Declaration:** Use the `abstract` keyword in the class header.
2. **Instantiation:** **Cannot** be instantiated using `new AbstractClass()`.
3. **Abstract methods:** Can have **zero or more** abstract methods (no body).
4. **Concrete methods:** Can have **fully implemented** methods with bodies.
5. **Constructors:** Abstract classes **can have constructors** (used by subclasses via `super()`).
6. **Static members:** Can contain static variables and static methods.
7. **Final methods:** Can contain `final` methods that cannot be overridden.
8. **Inheritance:** A concrete subclass *must* implement **all** abstract methods, else itself becomes abstract.
9. **Modifiers:** Abstract methods **cannot be** `private`, `static`, or `final`.

### 2.2 Characteristics of an Interface — Structured Logic

1. **Declaration:** Use the `interface` keyword (not `class`).
2. **Implicit modifiers:** Methods are implicitly `public abstract`; variables are implicitly `public static final`.
3. **Java 8 Enhancements:** Interfaces may contain `default` (with body) and `static` methods.
4. **Java 9 Enhancements:** Interfaces may contain `private` methods (helper logic).
5. **Instantiation:** **Cannot** be instantiated directly.
6. **Constructors:** **Interfaces do NOT have constructors**.
7. **Multiple inheritance:** A class can implement **multiple interfaces** (comma-separated).
8. **Inheritance between interfaces:** An interface can `extend` **multiple** other interfaces.
9. **Implementation:** A class uses `implements` keyword; must override all abstract methods.
10. **State:** Interfaces traditionally **cannot hold state** (only constants).

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Cheat Sheet

| Feature | Abstract Class | Interface |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Keyword | `abstract` | `interface` |
| Abstraction Level | **Partial (0–100%)** | **Full (100% pre-Java 8)** |
| Methods Allowed | Abstract + Concrete + Default | Abstract + (Default + Static since Java 8) |
| Variables | Instance + Static + Final | Only `public static final` constants |
| Access Modifiers | Any (private, protected, public) | Methods implicitly `public` |
| Constructor | **Yes** | **No** |
| `extends` / `implements` | Subclass uses `extends` | Class uses `implements` |
| Multiple Inheritance | **Not supported** | **Supported** |
| Speed | Slightly **faster** | Slightly **slower** (extra indirection) |
| Use Case | Shared code + abstract contract | Pure contract / capability definition |
| `final` Methods Allowed | Yes | **No** (interface methods can't be final) |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Abstract classes** are used in **framework design** (e.g., Spring Framework's `AbstractController`) to provide **default behavior** while forcing subclasses to define specific logic.
- **Interfaces** are the foundation of **API design**, dependency injection, callback mechanisms (`Runnable`, `Callable`), and event-driven programming (`ActionListener` in Java Swing).
- The **Strategy Design Pattern** in software engineering relies entirely on interface-based polymorphism.

### 2.5 Decision Tree — When to Use What?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip — Use Abstract Class when:** subclasses share a common code base AND require a constructor.
> **Use Interface when:** you need to define a capability/role unrelated to class hierarchy OR need multiple inheritance.

$$
\text{Decision} = \begin{cases} \text{Abstract Class}, & \text{if shared state} \ \lor\ \text{shared code exists} \\ \text{Interface}, & \text{if defining a capability or contract} \end{cases}
$$
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthroughs & Code Derivations

### 3.1 Walkthrough 1 — Abstract Class Implementation (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Declare the abstract class with one abstract and one concrete method.**

```java
abstract class Shape {
    String color;

    // Concrete method
    void setColor(String c) {
        this.color = c;
    }

    // Abstract method — NO body
    abstract double area();
}
```

**Step 2 — Provide concrete subclasses that extend the abstract class.**

```java
class Circle extends Shape {
    double radius;

    Circle(double r) { this.radius = r; }

    @Override
    double area() {
        return Math.PI * radius * radius;
    }
}

class Rectangle extends Shape {
    double length, width;

    Rectangle(double l, double w) {
        this.length = l;
        this.width  = w;
    }

    @Override
    double area() {
        return length * width;
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Polymorphic invocation via a reference of the abstract type.**

```java
public class Main {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Shape s;                      // Reference of abstract type
        s = new Circle(5.0);
        System.out.println("Circle area   = " + s.area());
        s = new Rectangle(4.0, 6.0);
        System.out.println("Rectangle area = " + s.area());
    }
}
```

**Logic Explanation of Each Step:**

- **Line-by-line intent:** Step 1 establishes a *contract* (`area()`) and a *shared feature* (`setColor`). Step 2 commits `Circle` and `Rectangle` to fulfill the contract. Step 3 exploits **dynamic polymorphism** — the same `s.area()` call invokes different methods at runtime based on the actual object type.

**Resulting Output:**

$$
\text{Circle area} = 78.5398\ldots, \quad \text{Rectangle area} = 24.0
$$

### 3.2 Walkthrough 2 — Interface Implementation (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Declare the interface.**

```java
interface Drawable {
    void draw();                       // implicitly public abstract
    static double PI_VALUE = 3.14159;  // implicitly public static final
}
```

**Step 2 — Implement the interface in a concrete class.**

```java
class Triangle implements Drawable {
    public void draw() {
        System.out.println("Drawing a Triangle");
    }
}
```

**Step 3 — Multiple interface implementation (resolving the diamond problem).**

```java
interface Printable { void print(); }
interface Showable  { void show();  }

class Document implements Printable, Showable {
    public void print() { System.out.println("Printing...");   }
    public void show()  { System.out.println("Showing...");    }
}
```

**Logic Explanation:**

- Interfaces in Java solve the **"Diamond Problem"** of multiple inheritance: if two interfaces declare the same default method, the implementing class **must override** it explicitly to resolve ambiguity.

### 3.3 Walkthrough 3 — Abstract Class Implementing an Interface

Java allows an abstract class to **partially implement** an interface and leave remaining abstract methods for its subclasses.

```java
interface Vehicle {
    void start();
    void stop();
}

abstract class TwoWheeler implements Vehicle {
    public void start() {                       // implemented
        System.out.println("Two-wheeler started");
    }
    // stop() remains abstract — must be implemented by subclass
}

class Bike extends TwoWheeler {
    public void stop() {
        System.out.println("Bike stopped");
    }
}
```

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common Compilation Errors

| Error Scenario | Compilation Result |
| :--- | :--- |
| `new AbstractClass()` attempted | **Error:** "Abstract class cannot be instantiated" |
| Subclass missing an abstract method override | **Error:** "Class must be declared abstract" |
| Interface method marked `private` (pre-Java 9) | **Error:** "Modifier private not allowed here" |
| Interface variable not initialized | **Error:** "Variable must be initialized in interface" |
| Class implements two interfaces with conflicting same-signature default methods | **Error:** "Duplicate default methods" unless overridden |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Abstract Class Hierarchy — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Shape ABSTRACT CLASS] --> B[Circle CONCRETE CLASS]
    A --> C[Rectangle CONCRETE CLASS]
    A --> D[Triangle CONCRETE CLASS]

    A1[Method area ABSTRACT NO BODY] -.-> A
    A2[Method setColor CONCRETE] -.-> A

    B1[Method area OVERRIDDEN] -.-> B
    C1[Method area OVERRIDDEN] -.-> C
    D1[Method area OVERRIDDEN] -.-> D
```

### 4.2 Interface Multiple Inheritance — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    I1[Interface Printable] --> M[Class Document CONCRETE]
    I2[Interface Showable]  --> M
    I3[Interface Drawable]  --> M

    I1A[Method print ABSTRACT] -.-> I1
    I2A[Method show ABSTRACT]  -.-> I2
    I3A[Method draw ABSTRACT]  -.-> I3

    M1[Implements print] -.-> M
    M2[Implements show]  -.-> M
    M3[Implements draw]  -.-> M
```

### 4.3 Abstract Class Implementing an Interface — Block-Level Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    IV[Interface Vehicle] --> TW[Abstract Class TwoWheeler]
    TW --> BK[Concrete Class Bike]

    IV1[Method start] -.-> IV
    IV2[Method stop]  -.-> IV

    TW1[Implements start CONCRETE] -.-> TW
    TW2[Stop left ABSTRACT]         -.-> TW

    BK1[Implements stop] -.-> BK
```

### 4.4 Decision Flow — Choosing Abstract Class vs Interface

```mermaid
graph TD
    Q1{Need shared code or state?} --> Q2{Need multiple inheritance?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| AC[Use ABSTRACT CLASS]
    Q1 -->|No| Q2
    Q2 -->|Yes| IF[Use INTERFACE]
    Q2 -->|No| Q3{Defining a capability role?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| IF
    Q3 -->|No| AC
```

> [!NOTE]
> These Mermaid diagrams follow the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** pattern, mapping class-level interactions, method obligations, and inheritance paths. This is the recommended visualization style for KTU board answers when asked to "draw the class diagram" or "explain the hierarchy."
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is an abstract class in Java? Why can't we instantiate it directly?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
An abstract class in Java is a class declared with the `abstract` keyword that may contain abstract methods (methods without a body) as well as concrete methods. It represents an **incomplete blueprint**. It cannot be instantiated directly because it is considered logically incomplete — Java enforces this rule to ensure that abstract methods are always overridden by a concrete subclass before any object is created in memory. Attempting `new AbstractClass()` results in a **compile-time error**: *"Abstract class cannot be instantiated."*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*State any three differences between an abstract class and an interface in Java.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **Abstract class** supports **partial abstraction** (0–100%), whereas an **interface** supports **full abstraction** (100%, pre-Java 8).
2. An abstract class can have **constructors**, but an **interface cannot**.
3. An abstract class uses the `extends` keyword, while an interface uses the `implements` keyword; multiple inheritance is allowed only through interfaces.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Explain the concept of abstract classes in Java with a suitable example. Discuss the rules that must be followed while using abstract classes. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** What is an interface in Java? Write a Java program to demonstrate how a class implements multiple interfaces. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Abstract Classes — Concept & Rules (7 Marks):**

An **abstract class** is a class declared with the `abstract` keyword. It may declare abstract methods (without a body) that subclasses are obligated to implement, while optionally providing shared concrete methods.

**Rules:**
1. Declared using `abstract` keyword.
2. Cannot be instantiated (`new` not allowed).
3. May contain zero or more abstract methods.
4. Can have constructors, static members, and `final` methods.
5. Abstract methods cannot be `private`, `static`, or `final`.
6. A subclass must implement all abstract methods or itself be declared abstract.

**Example:**

```java
abstract class Animal {
    abstract void sound();
    void sleep() { System.out.println("Sleeping..."); }
}
class Dog extends Animal {
    void sound() { System.out.println("Bark"); }
}
```

**(b) Interface & Multiple Interface Implementation (7 Marks):**

An **interface** in Java is a reference type, declared with the `interface` keyword, that defines a contract of abstract methods. From Java 8, it can also hold `default` and `static` methods. A class uses the `implements` keyword and can implement **multiple interfaces** separated by commas.

**Program:**

```java
interface Camera {
    void takePhoto();
}
interface Phone {
    void makeCall();
}
class Smartphone implements Camera, Phone {
    public void takePhoto() { System.out.println("Photo clicked"); }
    public void makeCall()  { System.out.println("Calling...");    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Smartphone s = new Smartphone();
        s.takePhoto();
        s.makeCall();
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
Photo clicked
Calling...
```

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
**(a)** Compare abstract classes and interfaces in Java with a neat comparison table. Mention any four differences. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that uses an abstract class `BankAccount` with an abstract method `calculateInterest()`, and two concrete subclasses `SavingsAccount` and `CurrentAccount` that override this method. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks):**

| Feature | Abstract Class | Interface |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Abstraction | Partial | Full (100%) |
| Keyword | `abstract` | `interface` |
| Multiple Inheritance | Not supported | Supported |
| Constructors | Allowed | Not allowed |
| Method Types | Abstract + Concrete | Abstract + (Default / Static) |
| Variables | Any type | `public static final` only |
| Access Specifiers | Any | Implicitly `public` |

**(b) Java Program with Abstract Class (7 Marks):**

```java
abstract class BankAccount {
    double principal;
    BankAccount(double p) { this.principal = p; }
    abstract double calculateInterest();
}
class SavingsAccount extends BankAccount {
    SavingsAccount(double p) { super(p); }
    double calculateInterest() { return principal * 0.04; }
}
class CurrentAccount extends BankAccount {
    CurrentAccount(double p) { super(p); }
    double calculateInterest() { return principal * 0.02; }
}
public class BankDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        BankAccount acc;
        acc = new SavingsAccount(10000);
        System.out.println("Savings Interest  = " + acc.calculateInterest());
        acc = new CurrentAccount(10000);
        System.out.println("Current Interest  = " + acc.calculateInterest());
    }
}
```

**Output:**

$$
\text{Savings Interest} = 400.0, \quad \text{Current Interest} = 200.0
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `abstract` keyword** on a method declared without a body inside an abstract class → **−1 mark**.
> 2. **Trying to use `new AbstractClass()`** in code examples → entire program becomes invalid.
> 3. **Confusing `extends` with `implements`** — a class that *implements* an interface must use `implements`, not `extends` (unless it is an abstract class).
> 4. **Not overriding ALL abstract methods** in the concrete subclass → marks deducted for incomplete code.
> 5. **Forgetting `public` modifier** on overridden interface methods (interface methods are implicitly `public`, so the override cannot have weaker access).
> 6. **Writing `final` with interface methods** — this is illegal and loses marks instantly.
> 7. **Forgetting to initialize interface variables** — they must be initialized at declaration since they are implicitly `final`.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Abstract class** = partial abstraction; can have abstract + concrete methods, constructors, instance variables.
- **Interface** = full abstraction (pre-Java 8); supports multiple inheritance; variables are constants.
- A class uses `extends` to inherit from an abstract class, and `implements` to fulfill an interface contract.
- An abstract class **can implement** an interface without providing implementations for all its methods.
- Java does **NOT support multiple class inheritance** — interfaces are the only mechanism to achieve it.
- All interface methods (pre-Java 8) are implicitly `public abstract`; all variables are implicitly `public static final`.
- A subclass that does not implement all abstract methods **must itself be declared abstract**.
- Java 8 introduced `default` and `static` methods in interfaces; Java 9 added `private` methods.
- Abstract classes are **faster** than interfaces (less indirection) but interfaces are more **flexible** for capability-based design.
- Always remember: **abstract class → "is-a" relationship** (inheritance), **interface → "can-do" relationship** (capability).
- KTU expects Java code examples to be **compilable**; syntax errors lead to significant mark loss.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Handling Exceptions (Try-Catch Blocks)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT611 - OBJECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING IN JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Handling Exceptions (Try-Catch Blocks)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Exception Handling** in Java is a robust, object-oriented mechanism designed to detect, intercept, and gracefully recover from runtime anomalies (exceptions) that disrupt the normal flow of program execution. The **try-catch block** is the foundational control structure of this mechanism, where:

- The **try block** encapsulates a *guarded region* of code that is *monitored* for exceptions.
- The **catch block** (also termed the *exception handler*) intercepts a thrown exception object of a specific type and executes a recovery routine.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (Module 4 – *Handling Exceptions*), exception handling is a critical pillar of **robust software engineering**, ensuring programs achieve *fault tolerance*, *graceful degradation*, and *resource safety*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Syllabus Highlight):** An *exception* is an event that occurs during the execution of a program that disrupts the normal flow of the program's instructions. In Java, exceptions are objects that are *thrown* by code that encounters an error condition and *caught* by code that can handle it.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy – The Airbag System in a Car:**
> Imagine you are driving a car (your program). You cannot predict exactly when an accident (exception) will occur, but you can *prepare* for it. You wear a **seatbelt** and your car has **airbags** installed.
> - The **try block** is your *driving routine* — the regular code path.
> - The **catch block** is the **airbag mechanism** — it activates *only if* a crash happens.
> - Without an airbag, a small bump could throw the passenger (the program) out of control and crash the entire vehicle (terminate the program).
> - With an airbag, even after a crash, the passenger is safe, and the journey (program execution) can continue.
>
> The car's airbag does not *prevent* the crash — it *handles* it. Similarly, a try-catch block does not *prevent* exceptions — it *handles* them.

## 1.3 Why Exception Handling? – The Three Pillars

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Concept:** Why do we need exception handling in Java? Justify with respect to the following three engineering requirements:
>
> 1. **Separation of Error-Handling Code from Normal Logic Code** — improves readability and maintainability.
> 2. **Propagation of Errors Up the Call Stack** — methods can delegate error handling to their callers.
> 3. **Grouping and Differentiation of Error Types** — using exception class hierarchies, distinct error categories can be handled differently.

## 1.4 The Java Exception Class Hierarchy (Essential Foundation)

Every exception in Java is an object derived from the `java.lang.Throwable` class. The hierarchy is as follows:

- **`java.lang.Throwable`** (the root)
  - **`java.lang.Error`** — Serious, unrecoverable problems (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`). *Not* typically caught by applications.
  - **`java.lang.Exception`** — Conditions that applications *should* catch.
    - **Checked Exceptions** — Checked at compile time (e.g., `IOException`, `SQLException`).
    - **Unchecked Exceptions (Runtime Exceptions)** — Occur at runtime, derived from `RuntimeException` (e.g., `ArithmeticException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `NullPointerException`).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Students frequently confuse *checked* and *unchecked* exceptions. **Checked** = compile-time verification. **Unchecked** = `RuntimeException` subclasses. This is a high-weightage 3-mark question.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the Try-Catch Block — Structured Logic Flow

The basic `try-catch` syntax in Java is:

```java
try {
    // Guarded code: code that might throw an exception
    // Statement 1
    // Statement 2
    // ...
} catch (ExceptionType1 e1) {
    // Handler for ExceptionType1
} catch (ExceptionType2 e2) {
    // Handler for ExceptionType2
} finally {
    // Cleanup code: ALWAYS executes (whether exception occurred or not)
}
```

### Step-by-Step Operational Breakdown

> [!NOTE]
> **Operational Mechanics — How a try-catch block works in the JVM:**
>
> 1. **Entry:** The JVM enters the `try` block and begins executing statements sequentially.
> 2. **Monitoring:** The JVM sets up an *implicit exception table* referencing the `try` block's range and the corresponding `catch` handler addresses (this is bytecode-level metadata).
> 3. **Normal Path:** If no exception occurs, all statements in the `try` block execute, the `catch` block is **skipped**, and control transfers to the `finally` block (if present) and then continues after the entire construct.
> 4. **Abnormal Path:** If a statement throws an exception:
>    - The JVM creates an *exception object* containing the stack trace, error message, and exception type.
>    - The `try` block is **aborted immediately** — no further statements within the `try` block are executed.
>    - The JVM searches for a matching `catch` handler, comparing the thrown exception's class against each `catch` parameter type from **top to bottom**.
>    - The **first matching** `catch` block executes. The exception object is bound to its parameter (e.g., `e1`, `e2`).
>    - After the `catch` block, control flows to the `finally` block (if any) and then continues.
> 5. **No Match:** If no `catch` block matches, the exception *propagates* up the call stack to the calling method, terminating the current method.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Try-Catch Mechanics

| # | Concept | KTU Board-Exam Standard Definition | Mnemonic / Key Point |
|---|---------|--------------------------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | `try` block | Code that *might* throw an exception; cannot exist alone | **Try = Tentative Code** |
| 2 | `catch` block | Handles a specific exception type; parameter declares type | **Catch = Catches the thrown object** |
| 3 | `finally` block | Executes **always** (except `System.exit()`) | **Finally = Final Cleanup** |
| 4 | `throw` keyword | Manually throws an exception object | `throw new ArithmeticException("...")` |
| 5 | `throws` keyword | Declares that a method *might* throw a checked exception | `void read() throws IOException` |
| 6 | Exception Object | Carries stack trace, message, and type information | Inherits from `Throwable` |
| 7 | Multiple catches | Order matters: **subclass first, superclass last** | Else compile error |
| 8 | Multi-catch (Java 7+) | `catch (IOException \| SQLException e)` | Single handler, multiple types |
| 9 | Unreachable catch | Compile-time error if exception is already caught above | Compiler enforces it |
| 10 | Nested try | A `try` block *inside* another `try` or `catch` block | Inner exceptions handled locally |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Application Spotlight:** Exception handling is not merely an academic exercise. It is the backbone of:
> - **Banking Software:** Handling `InsufficientFundsException` without crashing the ATM.
> - **Network Applications:** Recovering from `SocketTimeoutException` and retrying operations.
> - **Database Systems:** Managing `SQLException` and rolling back incomplete transactions.
> - **Embedded/IoT Systems (KTU Focus):** Graceful recovery from sensor read failures or memory overflows without rebooting the entire microcontroller.
> - **Aerospace & Automotive (Safety-Critical):** The airbag analogy is literal here — software faults in flight control systems are caught and handled, never allowed to crash the system.

## 2.4 The `finally` Block — Special Cases (High-Yield KTU Question)

> [!WARNING]
> **When does `finally` NOT execute?** This is a classic KTU trick question:
>
> 1. When `System.exit(0)` is called inside the `try` or `catch` block.
> 2. When the JVM itself crashes (e.g., `StackOverflowError` due to infinite recursion, in some JVMs).
> 3. When the thread executing the `try-catch-finally` is killed externally.
>
> In *all other* cases — including when an exception is caught, uncaught, or even when a `return` statement is executed — the `finally` block **always** runs. This guarantees resource cleanup (e.g., closing files, releasing database connections).

## 2.5 Common Built-in Exception Classes (Examiner's Favourite Table)

| Exception Class | Type | When Thrown |
|-----------------|------|-------------|
| `ArithmeticException` | Unchecked | Division by zero in integer arithmetic |
| `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Unchecked | Invalid array index access |
| `NullPointerException` | Unchecked | Accessing a method/field on `null` |
| `NumberFormatException` | Unchecked | Invalid string-to-number conversion |
| `IOException` | Checked | I/O operation failure |
| `FileNotFoundException` | Checked | Trying to open a non-existent file |
| `ClassNotFoundException` | Checked | Class loading failure |
| `SQLException` | Checked | Database access error |
| `InterruptedException` | Checked | Thread interrupted while waiting/sleeping |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Basic Try-Catch Execution Trace

Consider the following Java program. We will trace its execution line by line, mimicking the JVM's runtime behavior.

```java
public class TryCatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int a = 10;
        int b = 0;
        int result = 0;
        try {
            System.out.println("Step 1: Entering try block");
            result = a / b;
            System.out.println("Step 2: This line will NOT execute");
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Step 3: Exception caught: " + e.getMessage());
            result = -1;
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Step 4: Finally block always runs");
        }
        System.out.println("Step 5: Result = " + result);
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

1. **Line 1–3:** Variables `a = 10`, `b = 0`, `result = 0` are initialized. No exception yet.
2. **Enter `try` block:** The JVM marks the start of the guarded region.
3. **`System.out.println("Step 1...")`** executes successfully — output: `"Step 1: Entering try block"`.
4. **`result = a / b;`** evaluates `10 / 0`. The JVM detects integer division by zero and throws a new `ArithmeticException` object.
5. **Abnormal Path:** The `try` block is *aborted immediately*. The statement `System.out.println("Step 2...")` is **skipped**.
6. **Exception Matching:** The JVM scans the `catch` blocks. The first `catch` declares `ArithmeticException` — this matches exactly. Control transfers to it.
7. **Inside `catch`:** Output: `"Step 3: Exception caught: / by zero"`. Then `result` is set to `-1`.
8. **`finally` block:** Output: `"Step 4: Finally block always runs"`.
9. **After the construct:** Output: `"Step 5: Result = -1"`.
10. **Program terminates normally.**

### Final Console Output

```
Step 1: Entering try block
Step 3: Exception caught: / by zero
Step 4: Finally block always runs
Step 5: Result = -1
```

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Multiple Catch Blocks (Order Matters!)

```java
public class MultiCatchDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            int[] arr = new int[3];
            arr[5] = 100;
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("ArithmeticException caught");
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException caught");
        } catch (RuntimeException e) {
            System.out.println("RuntimeException caught");
        }
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace

1. The array `arr` of size 3 is created. Valid indices are 0, 1, 2.
2. `arr[5] = 100;` attempts to access index 5, which is out of bounds.
3. The JVM throws an `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` (which is a subclass of `RuntimeException`).
4. The JVM scans `catch` blocks in order:
   - `catch (ArithmeticException e)` — **No match**.
   - `catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e)` — **Exact match!**
5. Output: `"ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException caught"`.
6. The third `catch` is **not** reached (would be unreachable if the first match were a superclass).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Compilation Rule:** If we reorder the catches as `RuntimeException` → `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, the compiler throws an **error: unreachable catch**. This is a 7-mark short-answer question worth mastering.

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Nested Try-Catch with File Handling

```java
import java.io.*;

public class NestedTryDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt");
            try {
                fr.read();
                int x = 10 / 0;
            } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
                System.out.println("Inner: ArithmeticException handled");
            } finally {
                fr.close();
                System.out.println("Inner: File closed");
            }
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("Outer: IOException handled");
        }
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Trace

1. Outer `try` block opens `data.txt` (assume file exists).
2. Inner `try` block calls `fr.read()` (succeeds) and then evaluates `10 / 0`.
3. `ArithmeticException` is thrown — but it is **caught by the inner `catch`** first.
4. Inner `finally` closes the file and prints `"Inner: File closed"`.
5. Control returns to the outer `try` block. Since the inner exception was caught, no exception propagates to the outer `catch`.
6. The outer `catch (IOException)` is **not** triggered.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Nested try-catch is used when an inner operation (e.g., reading a file) might fail with a *specific* exception that we want to handle locally, while *enclosing* operations (e.g., establishing a network connection) might fail with a *broader* exception.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Try-Catch Behavior Under Various Conditions

| Scenario | What Happens | Does `finally` Run? | Output After Try-Catch |
|----------|--------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| No exception | All `try` statements run; `catch` skipped | Yes | Normal continuation |
| Exception caught | `try` aborts; matching `catch` runs | Yes | After `finally` |
| Exception not caught | `try` aborts; all `catch` skipped; propagates up | Yes | Exception propagates to caller |
| `return` in `try` | `try` exits; `catch` skipped; `finally` runs **before** return | Yes | Return value is sent after `finally` |
| `return` in `catch` | `catch` exits; `finally` runs **before** return | Yes | Return value is sent after `finally` |
| `System.exit(0)` in `try` | JVM exits immediately | **No** | Program terminates |
| `System.exit(0)` in `catch` | JVM exits immediately | **No** | Program terminates |
| Exception in `catch` itself | `try-catch` exits; new exception propagates | Yes (if present) | Original handler fails |
| Exception in `finally` | Original exception is **lost**; new one propagates | — | `finally`'s exception wins |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall (Frequently Tested in KTU):** If an exception is thrown *both* in the `try` block *and* in the `finally` block, the exception from the `finally` block **overrides** the original exception. This is called *exception masking* and is a major source of bugs in production code.

## 3.5 Mathematical Representation of Exception Propagation Cost

The runtime overhead of a try-catch block (in modern JVMs with HotSpot optimization) can be approximated as:

$$T_{\text{handler}} = T_{\text{enter}} + T_{\text{exit}} + \sum_{i=1}^{n} P(\text{Exception}_i) \cdot C(\text{Handler}_i)$$

Where:
- $T_{\text{enter}}$ is the cost of setting up the exception table entry (negligible in HotSpot: typically 1–2 ns).
- $T_{\text{exit}}$ is the cost of exiting the try-catch construct.
- $P(\text{Exception}_i)$ is the probability of the $i$-th exception occurring.
- $C(\text{Handler}_i)$ is the cost of executing the $i$-th catch handler (stack trace generation dominates: ~1–10 µs).

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Exception handling is *expensive* when an exception is actually thrown (because the JVM must generate a stack trace by walking the call stack). Therefore, exceptions should be used for *exceptional* conditions, not for *normal control flow*. This aligns with the KTU syllabus emphasis on *robust* and *efficient* coding practices.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Control Flow Diagram — How the JVM Processes a Try-Catch Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Program Enters Try Block] --> B{Execute Try Statements}
    B --> C{Exception Thrown?}
    C -->|No| D[Skip All Catch Blocks]
    C -->|Yes| E[Create Exception Object]
    E --> F[Abort Remaining Try Statements]
    F --> G[Scan Catch Blocks Top to Bottom]
    G --> H{Type Matches?}
    H -->|No Match in Any Catch| I[Propagate Exception Up Call Stack]
    H -->|Match Found| J[Execute Matching Catch Block]
    I --> K[Finally Block Executes]
    D --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L[Continue Normal Execution]
    L --> M[End of Try-Catch-Finally Construct]
```

## 4.2 Exception Class Hierarchy — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    ThrowableNode[java.lang.Throwable]
    ErrorNode[java.lang.Error]
    ExceptionNode[java.lang.Exception]
    RuntimeExceptionNode[java.lang.RuntimeException]
    IOErrorNode[java.lang.IOError]
    ArithmeticNode[ArithmeticException]
    ArrayIndexNode[ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException]
    NullPointerNode[NullPointerException]
    NumberFormatNode[NumberFormatException]
    IOExceptionNode[java.io.IOException]
    FileNotFoundNode[FileNotFoundException]
    SQLExceptionNode[java.sql.SQLException]

    ThrowableNode --> ErrorNode
    ThrowableNode --> ExceptionNode
    ErrorNode --> IOErrorNode
    ExceptionNode --> RuntimeExceptionNode
    ExceptionNode --> IOExceptionNode
    ExceptionNode --> SQLExceptionNode
    RuntimeExceptionNode --> ArithmeticNode
    RuntimeExceptionNode --> ArrayIndexNode
    RuntimeExceptionNode --> NullPointerNode
    RuntimeExceptionNode --> NumberFormatNode
    IOExceptionNode --> FileNotFoundNode
```

## 4.3 Try-Catch-Finally Execution State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> EnteringTry
    EnteringTry --> ExecutingTry
    ExecutingTry --> ExceptionThrown : Error detected
    ExecutingTry --> ExecutingFinally : All statements OK
    ExceptionThrown --> MatchingCatch : Scan catches
    MatchingCatch --> ExecutingCatch : Type matches
    MatchingCatch --> PropagatingUp : No match
    ExecutingCatch --> ExecutingFinally
    PropagatingUp --> ExecutingFinally : Cleanup before leaving
    ExecutingFinally --> [*] : Normal exit
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Architecture — Exception Handling Subsystem

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AppLayer[Application Code Layer]
        A1[Business Logic in Try]
        A2[Recovery Code in Catch]
        A3[Cleanup Code in Finally]
    end
    subgraph JVMLayer[JVM Runtime Layer]
        B1[Exception Table Generator]
        B2[Exception Object Constructor]
        B3[Stack Trace Walker]
        B4[Exception Dispatcher]
    end
    subgraph OSLayer[Operating System Layer]
        C1[Signal Handlers SIGSEGV SIGFPE]
        C2[Memory Allocator]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> B4
    A1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> A2
    A2 --> A3
    B4 --> C1
    B2 --> C2
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Architecture Diagram:** The Application Layer is where you write `try-catch-finally` blocks. The JVM Layer is the *engine* that detects exceptions, builds exception objects with stack traces, and dispatches them to the appropriate handler. The OS Layer is involved only for fatal errors (e.g., segmentation faults), which typically result in `Error` subclasses (not `Exception`).

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Q: Differentiate between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (Mark-Wise Breakdown):**

| Aspect | Checked Exceptions | Unchecked Exceptions |
|--------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **When checked** | At compile time | At runtime |
| **Compiler enforcement** | Must be either caught or declared with `throws` | No such requirement |
| **Parent class** | Direct subclasses of `Exception` (not `RuntimeException`) | Subclasses of `RuntimeException` |
| **Examples** | `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException`, `FileNotFoundException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `NumberFormatException` |
| **Typical use** | External resource failures (file, network, DB) | Programming bugs (logic errors, invalid input) |

*(1 mark for clear definition of each + ½ mark for each pair of examples = 3 marks total)*

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Q: What is the purpose of the `finally` block in Java exception handling? Will it execute if the `try` block contains a `return` statement? Justify your answer.**

**Model Answer:**

The `finally` block in Java is used to **execute critical cleanup code** that must run regardless of whether an exception is thrown, caught, or not thrown at all. Typical uses include closing file handles, releasing database connections, and freeing network sockets — operations that must not be skipped to avoid resource leaks.

**Yes**, the `finally` block executes even if the `try` block contains a `return` statement. The JVM defers the actual return until the `finally` block has completed execution. This guarantees resource cleanup before the method exits.

**Exception:** If `System.exit(0)` is called inside the `try` block, the JVM terminates immediately and the `finally` block is **not** executed.

*(1 mark purpose + 1 mark execution guarantee + 1 mark exception case = 3 marks total)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Modified]`
**(a)** Explain the Java exception class hierarchy with a neat diagram. Differentiate between `Error` and `Exception`. **[7 Marks]**
