**(b)** Write a Java program that reads two integers from the user, performs division, and handles the following exceptions using multiple `catch` blocks: `ArithmeticException` (division by zero) and `InputMismatchException` (non-integer input). Use a `finally` block to print a closing message. Show the output for two test cases: (i) valid input `10` and `2`; (ii) input `10` and `0`. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Exception Class Hierarchy:**

The root of Java's exception hierarchy is `java.lang.Throwable`. It has two direct subclasses:

- **`Error`**: Represents serious, unrecoverable problems that applications should **not** attempt to catch. Examples: `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`, `VirtualMachineError`. These typically indicate conditions from which the program cannot recover.

- **`Exception`**: Represents conditions that applications **should** catch and handle. It is further divided into:
  - **Checked exceptions** (compile-time): `IOException`, `SQLException`, etc.
  - **Unchecked exceptions** (runtime, extending `RuntimeException`): `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, etc.

| Feature | `Error` | `Exception` |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| Recoverable? | No | Usually yes |
| Caught typically? | No | Yes |
| Examples | `OutOfMemoryError` | `IOException`, `RuntimeException` |

**Diagram:**

```mermaid
graph TD
    ThrowableNode[java.lang.Throwable]
    ErrorNode[java.lang.Error]
    ExceptionNode[java.lang.Exception]
    RuntimeExceptionNode[java.lang.RuntimeException]
    OOMNode[OutOfMemoryError]
    SOFNode[StackOverflowError]
    ArithmeticNode[ArithmeticException]
    IOENode[IOException]

    ThrowableNode --> ErrorNode
    ThrowableNode --> ExceptionNode
    ErrorNode --> OOMNode
    ErrorNode --> SOFNode
    ExceptionNode --> RuntimeExceptionNode
    ExceptionNode --> IOENode
    RuntimeExceptionNode --> ArithmeticNode
```

**(b) Java Program:**

```java
import java.util.Scanner;
import java.util.InputMismatchException;

public class DivisionHandler {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        int a = 0, b = 0, result = 0;
        try {
            System.out.print("Enter numerator: ");
            a = sc.nextInt();
            System.out.print("Enter denominator: ");
            b = sc.nextInt();
            result = a / b;
            System.out.println("Result: " + result);
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: Cannot divide by zero. " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (InputMismatchException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: Please enter valid integers only.");
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Program ended. Scanner closing.");
            sc.close();
        }
    }
}
```

**Test Case (i): Input 10 and 2**

```
Enter numerator: 10
Enter denominator: 2
Result: 5
Program ended. Scanner closing.
```

**Test Case (ii): Input 10 and 0**

```
Enter numerator: 10
Enter denominator: 0
Error: Cannot divide by zero. / by zero
Program ended. Scanner closing.
```

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Modified]`
**(a)** What is exception propagation in Java? Explain with an example where an exception is thrown in a called method and caught in the calling method. How is this related to the `throws` keyword? **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Write a Java program that demonstrates a **nested try-catch** structure. The outer `try` block should attempt to parse a string into an integer (handle `NumberFormatException`), and the inner `try` block should perform division using the parsed integer (handle `ArithmeticException`). Include a `finally` block at both levels. Show the output for the input string `"100"` and `"abc"`. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Exception Propagation:**

**Exception propagation** is the mechanism by which an exception, when not caught in the method where it occurs, is passed (propagated) up the call stack to the calling method. If the calling method also does not catch it, the exception continues to propagate upward until either a matching `catch` block is found or the `main` method is reached (in which case the JVM terminates the program with a stack trace).

The `throws` keyword is used in a method's signature to **declare** that the method might throw one or more checked exceptions, *transferring the responsibility* of handling those exceptions to the caller.

**Example Code:**

```java
public class PropagationDemo {
    static void method1() throws ArithmeticException {
        int x = 10 / 0;
    }
    static void method2() {
        method1();
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            method2();
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught in main: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Execution Trace:**

1. `main` calls `method2()` inside the `try` block.
2. `method2()` calls `method1()`.
3. `method1()` evaluates `10 / 0` and throws an `ArithmeticException`.
4. `method1()` does **not** catch the exception, so it propagates to `method2()`.
5. `method2()` also does **not** catch the exception, so it propagates to `main()`.
6. `main()` has a `catch (ArithmeticException e)` block that handles it.
7. **Output:** `"Caught in main: / by zero"`.

> [!NOTE]
> **Connection to `throws`:** If `method1()` threw a *checked* exception (e.g., `IOException`), its signature would need `throws IOException` to inform the compiler. Unchecked exceptions (like `ArithmeticException`) do not require `throws`, but propagation still occurs.

**(b) Nested Try-Catch Program:**

```java
public class NestedTryDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        String str = "abc";
        try {
            try {
                int num = Integer.parseInt(str);
                int result = num / 0;
                System.out.println("Result: " + result);
            } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
                System.out.println("Inner catch: Division by zero. " + e.getMessage());
            } finally {
                System.out.println("Inner finally: Inner block cleanup done.");
            }
        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("Outer catch: Invalid number format. " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Outer finally: Program cleanup done.");
        }
    }
}
```

**Test Case (i): Input `"100"`**

```
Inner catch: Division by zero. / by zero
Inner finally: Inner block cleanup done.
Outer finally: Program cleanup done.
```

**Test Case (ii): Input `"abc"`**

```
Outer catch: Invalid number format. For input string: "abc"
Outer finally: Program cleanup done.
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Pitfall 1 (–1 mark):** Forgetting to import `java.util.*` packages or `java.io.*` when needed.
> - **Pitfall 2 (–2 marks):** Placing `catch (Exception e)` *before* a more specific catch — this causes a *compile-time error: unreachable catch*. Always catch specific exceptions first.
> - **Pitfall 3 (–1 mark):** Writing `catch (Exception e)` and assuming it catches *everything*. It does **not** catch `Error` subclasses.
> - **Pitfall 4 (–1 mark):** Forgetting that `finally` runs even when a `return` is in `try` or `catch`.
> - **Pitfall 5 (–2 marks):** Confusing `throw` (used to throw an exception object) with `throws` (used to declare a method's exception list). This is a high-weightage pitfall.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- ✅ An **exception** is an *object* that represents a runtime anomaly, derived from `java.lang.Throwable`.
- ✅ The **three keywords** of exception handling are `try`, `catch`, `throw` / `throws`.
- ✅ `try` block **must** be followed by either a `catch` block, a `finally` block, or both.
- ✅ `catch` blocks are evaluated in **top-to-bottom order**; only the first match executes.
- ✅ A `catch` parameter declaring a **superclass** must come **after** a subclass — otherwise compile error.
- ✅ The `finally` block **always** executes (except `System.exit()` or JVM crash).
- ✅ A `finally` block is the best place to **close resources** (files, sockets, DB connections).
- ✅ **Checked exceptions** must be either caught or declared with `throws`; **unchecked** exceptions need neither.
- ✅ **Exception propagation**: uncaught exceptions move up the call stack to the caller.
- ✅ **`throw`** vs **`throws`**: `throw` is a *statement* that throws an object; `throws` is a *clause* in a method signature.
- ✅ An exception thrown in the `finally` block **overrides** any pending exception from `try` or `catch` (exception masking).
- ✅ Use **specific** exception types in `catch` blocks — avoid generic `catch (Exception e)` unless necessary.
- ✅ Exceptions are **expensive** (stack trace generation is ~1–10 µs); do **not** use them for normal control flow.
- ✅ `try-with-resources` (Java 7+) is the modern way to handle auto-closeable resources — covered in advanced modules.
- ✅ Multiple exception types can be caught in a single `catch` using `|` (multi-catch): `catch (IOException | SQLException e)`.
- ✅ The `printStackTrace()`, `getMessage()`, and `toString()` methods are the standard ways to inspect an exception object.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Custom Exceptions
Checked vs. Unchecked Exceptions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT611 - OBJECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING IN JAVA
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Checked vs. Unchecked Exceptions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the Java programming language, an **exception** is an abnormal event or condition that disrupts the normal flow of a program's execution at runtime. Java classifies exceptions into two broad categories based on when they are detected by the Java compiler (Javac) versus the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) at runtime:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Checked Exception (Compile-Time Exception):** A checked exception is an exception that is checked by the **compiler at compile-time**. Any method that throws a checked exception must either **handle it using a `try-catch` block** OR **declare it using the `throws` keyword** in its method signature. The compiler will refuse to compile the code if neither of these actions is taken. All checked exceptions are subclasses of `java.lang.Exception` (but NOT subclasses of `java.lang.RuntimeException`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Unchecked Exception (Runtime Exception):** An unchecked exception is an exception that is **NOT checked by the compiler**. It occurs at runtime and is thrown by the JVM. The compiler does NOT mandate that the programmer handle or declare it. All unchecked exceptions are subclasses of `java.lang.RuntimeException`. Errors (subclasses of `java.lang.Error`) are also considered unchecked.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of exceptions like **safety protocols in a construction site**:

- **Checked Exceptions** = *Weather Forecasts*. The project manager (the compiler) can predict that rain is highly likely in monsoon season (e.g., trying to read a file that might not exist). Before sending workers out (compiling the code), the manager FORCES you to either carry an umbrella (use `try-catch`) or postpone the work (declare `throws`). You cannot ignore the forecast.

- **Unchecked Exceptions** = *Sudden On-Site Accidents*. A worker trips over a loose brick (e.g., you tried to divide by zero or access `array[10]` on a 5-element array). The compiler cannot predict every possible mistake, so it does not force you to write a safety protocol. These happen dynamically and indicate programming logic errors (bugs).

### 1.3 KTU Syllabus Highlights

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU 2024 Module 4 Outcomes:**
> - Classify exceptions into checked, unchecked, and errors.
> - Understand the Throwable class hierarchy.
> - Demonstrate the use of `try`, `catch`, `finally`, `throw`, and `throws` keywords.
> - Distinguish between compile-time and runtime exception handling obligations.

### 1.4 Standard Java Exception Metrics

- **Root Class:** `java.lang.Throwable`
- **Compile-Time Check Enforcement:** Mandatory for `Exception` subclasses excluding `RuntimeException`
- **Default Exception Handler:** The JVM
- **Default Stack Trace Depth Limit:** Typically the most recent **1024** frames are dumped on `printStackTrace()`
- **Throwable Methods:** `getMessage()`, `printStackTrace()`, `toString()`
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Java Exception Class Hierarchy (Detailed)

The entire Java exception framework is rooted in a single class: `java.lang.Throwable`. This class has two direct subclasses:

1. **`java.lang.Exception`** — Represents conditions that a reasonable program should anticipate and recover from.
2. **`java.lang.Error`** — Represents serious, unrecoverable problems (JVM-level issues), not typically handled by applications.

`Exception` is further divided into:
- **Direct `Exception` subclasses** → These are **CHECKED**.
- **`RuntimeException` subclasses** → These are **UNCHECKED**.

### 2.2 Why Are They Classified This Way? (The "Why" Behind the Design)

- **Checked exceptions** model recoverable conditions: file not found, network down, invalid SQL query. The designer of Java (James Gosling) wanted to enforce that API designers force callers to think about failure scenarios. This is a deliberate API contract.
- **Unchecked exceptions** model programming bugs: logic flaws, type mismatches, array index out of bounds. These indicate poor code quality, not environmental uncertainty. Forcing `try-catch` for every possible bug would clutter code with noise.

### 2.3 Checked Exception — Operational Logic

**Conditions where it applies:**
- Code interacts with external resources: files, databases, networks, threads.
- Compiler enforces `throws` clause or `try-catch` block.
- Examples: `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException`, `ParseException`.

**Syntax Patterns:**
```java
// Pattern 1: Declare with throws
public void readFile() throws IOException {
    FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt");
}

// Pattern 2: Handle with try-catch
public void readFile() {
    try {
        FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt");
    } catch (IOException e) {
        System.out.println("File missing");
    }
}
```

### 2.4 Unchecked Exception — Operational Logic

**Conditions where it applies:**
- Programming logic errors.
- Compiler does NOT enforce handling.
- Examples: `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `ClassCastException`, `NumberFormatException`, `IllegalArgumentException`.

**Syntax Pattern (no mandatory handling):**
```java
// Compiler is happy even without try-catch
public int divide(int a, int b) {
    return a / b;  // Throws ArithmeticException at runtime if b == 0
}
```

### 2.5 Errors — The Third Category

`java.lang.Error` and its subclasses (`OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`, `VirtualMachineError`) are **unchecked** but represent catastrophic failures where recovery is impossible. Applications typically do not catch them.

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| **Feature** | **Checked Exception** | **Unchecked Exception** |
|---|---|---|
| **Detection Time** | Compile-Time | Run-Time |
| **Parent Class** | `java.lang.Exception` (direct) | `java.lang.RuntimeException` |
| **Compiler Enforcement** | YES — must handle or declare | NO — optional handling |
| **Keyword Used** | `throws` declaration mandatory | `throws` declaration optional |
| **Cause** | External resources / environment | Programming bugs / logic flaws |
| **Recoverable?** | Yes, expected to be handled | Usually indicates a bug to fix |
| **Package Origin** | `java.io`, `java.sql`, `java.net` | `java.lang` (mostly) |
| **Examples** | `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException`, `ParseException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `NumberFormatException` |
| **Errors (sibling)** | N/A | `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError` (unchecked, but not `Exception`) |
| **Is `Throwable`?** | Yes | Yes |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Database Applications (JDBC):** `SQLException` is checked so developers must handle connection failures.
- **Web Servers (Servlets):** `IOException` is checked so stream handling is enforced.
- **API Design:** Throwing checked exceptions forces API consumers to acknowledge failure modes — a powerful contract design tool.
- **Modern Frameworks (Spring):** Wrap checked exceptions into unchecked `RuntimeException` to avoid `try-catch` boilerplate.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Decision Flow for Exception Selection

When writing Java code, follow this decision tree to determine how to handle an exception scenario:

**Step 1:** Identify if the abnormal condition is **external (environment-driven)** or **internal (logic-driven)**.
- External → Candidate for CHECKED.
- Internal → Candidate for UNCHECKED.

**Step 2:** Check if the exception class extends `RuntimeException`.
- YES → UNCHECKED (compiler allows ignoring).
- NO and extends `Exception` → CHECKED (compiler forces handling).

**Step 3:** Check if the exception class extends `Error`.
- YES → UNCHECKED but unrecoverable. Do not catch.

**Step 4:** Apply syntax rule.
- CHECKED → Either wrap in `try-catch` OR add to method `throws` clause.
- UNCHECKED → Handling is optional but recommended for robustness.

### 3.2 Code Walkthrough 1: Checked Exception (Compile-Time Enforcement)

```java
import java.io.*;

class Demo {
    // Step 1: Method signature must declare throws IOException
    public static void readFile(String path) throws IOException {
        FileReader fr = new FileReader(path);
        BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(fr);
        System.out.println(br.readLine());
        br.close();
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 2: Compiler forces us to handle IOException here
        try {
            readFile("test.txt");
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("Error: " + e.getMessage());
        }
    }
}
```

**Compiler Behavior Analysis:**
- If `main` does NOT have `try-catch` AND `readFile` does NOT have `throws` → **Compilation Error: unreported exception IOException**.
- If `main` has `try-catch` but `readFile` has neither `throws` nor `try-catch` → **Compilation Error** in `readFile`.
- This dual-compiler check is the defining trait of a **checked** exception.

### 3.3 Code Walkthrough 2: Unchecked Exception (No Compiler Enforcement)

```java
class Demo2 {
    public static int divide(int a, int b) {
        return a / b;  // No try-catch, no throws — compiles fine
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 1: Compiler allows this without any protection
        int result = divide(10, 0);
        System.out.println("Result: " + result);
        // Step 2: At runtime, JVM throws ArithmeticException
    }
}
```

**Runtime Behavior Analysis:**
- The code compiles successfully without any `try-catch` or `throws`.
- At runtime, when `b == 0`, the JVM detects division by zero and throws `java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero`.
- The default exception handler prints the stack trace and terminates the program.

### 3.4 Code Walkthrough 3: Multi-Catch and Finally (Comprehensive)

```java
class Demo3 {
    public static void processData(String[] arr, String indexStr) {
        try {
            int index = Integer.parseInt(indexStr);  // May throw NumberFormatException (unchecked)
            System.out.println(arr[index]);          // May throw ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException (unchecked)
        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("Invalid number format");
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Index out of range");
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Cleanup executed");  // Always runs
        }
    }
}
```

**Execution Logic:**
- Line 1: `parseInt` throws `NumberFormatException` if `indexStr` is not numeric → caught by first catch.
- Line 2: Array access throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` if `index >= arr.length` → caught by second catch.
- `finally` block executes regardless of whether an exception was thrown or not.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Table: Compile vs. Run

| **Test Condition** | **Checked Exception Code** | **Unchecked Exception Code** |
|---|---|---|
| Code without any handler | Compilation FAILS | Compilation SUCCEEDS |
| Runtime failure occurs | Same (JVM throws) | Same (JVM throws) |
| Stack trace printed | Yes (if unhandled) | Yes (if unhandled) |
| Method must declare `throws`? | YES | NO |
| Mandatory `try-catch`? | YES | NO |
| Typical cause | `FileNotFoundException` | `NullPointerException` |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Throwable Class Hierarchy (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[java.lang.Throwable] --> B[java.lang.Exception]
    A --> C[java.lang.Error]
    
    B --> D[java.lang.RuntimeException]
    B --> E[java.io.IOException]
    B --> F[java.sql.SQLException]
    B --> G[java.text.ParseException]
    B --> H[java.lang.ClassNotFoundException]
    
    D --> D1[ArithmeticException]
    D --> D2[NullPointerException]
    D --> D3[ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException]
    D --> D4[NumberFormatException]
    D --> D5[ClassCastException]
    D --> D6[IllegalArgumentException]
    
    C --> C1[OutOfMemoryError]
    C --> C2[StackOverflowError]
    C --> C3[VirtualMachineError]
    C --> C4[LinkageError]
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style G fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style H fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style D1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style D2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style D3 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style D4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style D5 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style D6 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style C1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style C2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style C3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style C4 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
```

**Legend:**
- 🟡 **Yellow** = Root: Throwable
- 🔵 **Blue** = Checked Branch (Exception)
- 🔴 **Red** = Errors (Unrecoverable)
- 🟢 **Green** = RuntimeException (Unchecked Branch)
- 🩷 **Pink** = Direct Checked Subclasses
- 💜 **Purple** = Unchecked RuntimeException Subclasses
- 🟠 **Orange** = Error Subclasses

### 4.2 Exception Handling Flow (Mermaid Sequence Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Method Call] --> B{Code Block in try}
    B -->|Exception Thrown| C{Is it Checked?}
    B -->|No Exception| D[Execute finally block]
    B -->|Normal Flow| E[Continue execution]
    
    C -->|Yes, Handled in catch| F[Matching catch block executes]
    C -->|Yes, Declared in throws| G[Propagate to caller]
    C -->|No, Unchecked and Unhandled| H[JVM default handler]
    
    F --> D
    G --> D
    H --> I[Print stack trace, terminate]
    D --> E
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style D fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#98FB98,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Checked vs Unchecked Exception Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Abnormal Condition Occurs] --> B{Type of Exception}
    
    B -->|External Resource / Environment| C[CHECKED EXCEPTION]
    B -->|Programming Logic Error| D[UNCHECKED EXCEPTION]
    B -->|JVM / System Failure| E[ERROR]
    
    C --> C1[Subclass of Exception]
    C --> C2[Compiler FORCES handling]
    C --> C3[try-catch OR throws required]
    C --> C4[Examples: IOException, SQLException]
    
    D --> D1[Subclass of RuntimeException]
    D --> D2[Compiler does NOT force handling]
    D --> D3[Optional try-catch or throws]
    D --> D4[Examples: NullPointerException, ArithmeticException]
    
    E --> E1[Subclass of Error]
    E --> E2[Unrecoverable, do not catch]
    E --> E3[Examples: OutOfMemoryError, StackOverflowError]
    
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#DC143C,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style C1 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style C2 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style C3 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style C4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style D1 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style D2 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style D3 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style D4 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style E1 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style E2 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
    style E3 fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> **Q1. Differentiate between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java with two examples each. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **Checked Exceptions:** Exceptions that are checked by the compiler at compile-time. The compiler forces the programmer to either handle them using `try-catch` or declare them using `throws`. Examples: `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException`.
- **Unchecked Exceptions:** Exceptions that are not checked by the compiler; they occur at runtime. The compiler does not mandate handling. They are subclasses of `RuntimeException`. Examples: `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> **Q2. What is the role of the `throw` keyword in Java? How is it different from the `throws` keyword? (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**
- **`throw` keyword:** Used to explicitly throw a single exception object from within a method or block of code. Syntax: `throw new ArithmeticException("Divide by zero");`
- **`throws` keyword:** Used in a method signature to declare that the method may propagate one or more checked exceptions to the caller. Syntax: `public void read() throws IOException`.
- **Difference:** `throw` is a statement that throws an instance; `throws` is a declaration in the method header that lists exception types.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> ### Question A (14 Marks)
> **(a)** Explain the Java exception class hierarchy with a neat diagram. List any **three** checked and **three** unchecked exceptions. (7 Marks)
> **(b)** Write a Java program to read an integer from the user and handle the case when the user enters a non-integer value using appropriate exception handling. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution (a):**
- The root of all exceptions and errors is `java.lang.Throwable`.
- It has two subclasses: `Exception` and `Error`.
- `Exception` includes `RuntimeException` and direct subclasses like `IOException`, `SQLException`.
- `RuntimeException` includes `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`.
- `Error` includes `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`.

(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 for the complete hierarchy.)

**Three Checked Exceptions:**
1. `IOException` — for I/O failures
2. `SQLException` — for database access errors
3. `ClassNotFoundException` — when a class cannot be located

**Three Unchecked Exceptions:**
1. `ArithmeticException` — divide by zero
2. `NullPointerException` — null reference access
3. `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` — invalid array index

**Model Solution (b):**
```java
import java.util.Scanner;

public class IntegerReader {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        System.out.print("Enter an integer: ");
        try {
            int num = Integer.parseInt(sc.nextLine());
            System.out.println("You entered: " + num);
        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("Invalid input! Please enter a valid integer.");
        } finally {
            sc.close();
            System.out.println("Scanner closed.");
        }
    }
}
```
- `NumberFormatException` is an unchecked exception (subclass of `RuntimeException`).
- It is thrown by `Integer.parseInt()` if the string is not a valid integer.
- The `finally` block ensures the `Scanner` is closed regardless.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
> **(a)** Explain the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions. Why does Java enforce checked exceptions? Discuss with suitable examples. (7 Marks)
> **(b)** Write a Java program that demonstrates the use of `try`, `catch`, `finally`, `throw`, and `throws` keywords in a single program for handling file operations. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution (a):**
- **Checked exceptions** are verified by the Java compiler at compile time. They represent recoverable conditions (e.g., `FileNotFoundException`).
- **Unchecked exceptions** are subclasses of `RuntimeException` and are detected only at runtime. They typically indicate programming bugs (e.g., `ArithmeticException`).
- **Why Java enforces checked exceptions:**
  - To force programmers to consider failure scenarios at design time.
  - To make API contracts explicit about what can go wrong.
  - To improve reliability when interacting with external systems (files, DBs, networks).
- **Example:** A method that reads a file `public void read() throws IOException` forces the caller to handle or propagate the `IOException`, ensuring no I/O failure is silently ignored.

**Model Solution (b):**
```java
import java.io.*;

class FileHandler {
    // throws keyword in method signature
    public static void readFile(String path) throws IOException {
        FileReader fr = new FileReader(path);
        BufferedReader br = new BufferedReader(fr);
        String line = br.readLine();
        
        // throw keyword for custom condition
        if (line == null) {
            throw new IOException("File is empty");
        }
        System.out.println("First line: " + line);
        br.close();
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // try-catch-finally block
        try {
            readFile("data.txt");
        } catch (FileNotFoundException e) {
            System.out.println("File not found: " + e.getMessage());
        } catch (IOException e) {
            System.out.println("I/O Error: " + e.getMessage());
        } finally {
            System.out.println("Operation complete — finally block executed.");
        }
    }
}
```

**Explanation:**
- `throws IOException` declares that the method can propagate the exception.
- `throw new IOException(...)` explicitly creates and throws an exception object.
- `try-catch` handles two types of checked exceptions.
- `finally` executes cleanup code regardless of whether an exception occurred.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Confusing `throw` and `throws`:** Many students write `throws new Exception()` instead of `throw new Exception()`. The former is invalid syntax. `throw` is followed by an **object**, `throws` is followed by a **class name** in the method signature.
> - **Forgetting `throws` declaration for checked exceptions:** If you call a method that throws a checked exception (e.g., `readFile()`), your method must either wrap it in `try-catch` OR add `throws IOException` to its own signature. Missing this loses 2-3 marks.
> - **Confusing `Error` with `Exception`:** Writing "OutOfMemoryError is an unchecked exception" is partially correct but imprecise. Errors are a **separate hierarchy branch** under `Throwable`, not under `Exception`. Examiners may deduct marks for imprecise terminology.
> - **Catching `Exception` blindly:** Writing `catch (Exception e)` to handle everything is a bad practice and KTU examiners penalize it. Use specific exception types.
> - **Mermaid Diagram Errors:** When drawing the hierarchy in exams, do NOT put `RuntimeException` under `Error`. It belongs under `Exception`.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Throwable is the root** of all exceptions and errors in Java.
- **Checked exceptions** = subclasses of `Exception` (excluding `RuntimeException`). Compiler checks them.
- **Unchecked exceptions** = subclasses of `RuntimeException`. Compiler does NOT check them.
- **Errors** = subclasses of `Error`. Unrecoverable; do not catch.
- **Compiler rule:** Any method calling code that throws a **checked** exception MUST either use `try-catch` or declare `throws`.
- **`throw` vs `throws`:** `throw` is a statement (throws an object), `throws` is a declaration (declares exception types in method header).
- **`finally` block:** Always executes, even if no exception occurs or if an exception is uncaught.
- **Common checked examples:** `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException`, `ParseException`.
- **Common unchecked examples:** `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, `NumberFormatException`.
- **KTU Favorite Questions:** Hierarchy diagram, difference table, and code with `try-catch-finally-throw-throws` combination.
- **One-liner memory trick:** "Runtime = Unchecked, Everything else under Exception = Checked, Under Error = Do not catch."
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Errors: Understanding Common Error Types in Java
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT611 - OBJECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING IN JAVA
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Errors: Understanding Common Error Types in Java

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Object-Oriented Programming in Java (OEITT611)**, an **Error** refers to any condition that prevents a program from compiling successfully, executing correctly, or producing the intended logical output. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme, Java categorizes problems that occur during software development into **three broad families**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition — Three Families of Errors**
> 1. **Syntax (Compile-Time) Errors** — Violations of the language grammar. Detected by the Java Compiler (`javac`) **before** the program ever runs.
> 2. **Runtime Errors (Exceptions)** — Conditions that the compiler cannot foresee but that disrupt normal flow during execution. Represented in code by the `java.lang.Throwable` class hierarchy.
> 3. **Logical Errors** — Flaws in the *algorithm* or business logic. The program compiles and runs successfully, but produces incorrect results. These are the hardest to detect.

> [!NOTE]
> **Terminology Clarification:** In casual speech, programmers say *"error"* to mean *any* bug. In strict Java terminology, `Error` is a specific subclass of `Throwable` (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`) representing serious, unrecoverable system-level failures. Always be precise in your exam answers.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

Imagine you are a **chef writing a recipe** for a customer:

| Programming Phase | Real-World Analogy | What Goes Wrong |
|---|---|---|
| **Syntax Error** | You wrote *"add 2 cups of suggar"* | Spelling/grammar mistakes — the recipe is *illegible*. The customer rejects it immediately. |
| **Runtime Error** | You turned on the gas stove but forgot the gas cylinder was empty | The recipe *looks* fine, *starts* fine, but blows up halfway through cooking. |
| **Logical Error** | You added salt instead of sugar | The dish comes out, looks beautiful, but tastes awful. Nobody notices until someone eats it. |

**The Java compiler is like a strict recipe reviewer.** It catches spelling and grammar mistakes (Syntax Errors) *before* cooking begins. But it cannot taste your food (Logical Errors) and cannot predict a gas leak mid-cooking (Runtime Errors).

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in KTU 2024

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 — Exception Handling Foundation):**
> This topic is the **gateway** to Module 4's main content on **Exception Handling** (`try-catch-finally`, `throw`, `throws`, custom exceptions). Without understanding *what* an error is and *how Java classifies* its failures, you cannot write robust, fault-tolerant programs — a **Programme Outcome (PO5: Modern Tool Usage)** expectation of the 2024 Scheme.

## 1.4 The Java `Throwable` Class — Root of All Errors

At the top of Java's exception hierarchy sits the class `java.lang.Throwable`. It has **two direct subclasses**:

$$\text{Throwable} \longrightarrow \begin{cases} \text{Error} \\ \text{Exception} \end{cases}$$

- **`Error`** — Serious problems (JVM crashes, memory exhaustion) that an application **should not try to catch**. Example: `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`.
- **`Exception`** — Conditions that a well-written application **should anticipate and handle**. Example: `IOException`, `ArithmeticException`.

This hierarchy is the **conceptual foundation** for the rest of Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Java Errors — Analytical Breakdown

### Pillar 1: Syntax (Compile-Time) Errors

**Definition:** Violations of Java's lexical, syntactic, or semantic rules.

**Why they occur:** Misspelled keywords, missing semicolons, mismatched braces, undeclared variables, type mismatches.

**How they are detected:** The **Java Compiler (`javac`)** parses the source code. If the syntax tree cannot be built, compilation is aborted. The program **never runs**.

**Common sub-categories:**

1. **Lexical Errors** — Invalid tokens (e.g., `@` used incorrectly, starting an identifier with a digit).
2. **Syntactic Errors** — Wrong grammar structure (e.g., missing `;`, extra `)`).
3. **Semantic Errors** — Type mismatches, assigning a `String` to an `int` variable, calling a non-existent method.

**Engineering Utility:** Syntax errors are the *cheapest* bugs to fix — the compiler tells you the **exact line number** and the **reason**.

### Pillar 2: Runtime Errors (Exceptions)

**Definition:** Exceptional events occurring during program execution that disrupt the normal instruction flow.

**Why they occur:** The compiler cannot predict them because they depend on **runtime data** (user input, file availability, network state, memory availability).

**How they are detected:** The **JVM** throws an exception object when the abnormal condition is encountered. If unhandled, the program **terminates abruptly** and prints a **stack trace**.

**Sub-division — The Most Critical KTU Concept:**

| Sub-Type | Description | Checked at Compile Time? | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Checked Exceptions** | Subclasses of `Exception` (but NOT `RuntimeException`). Compiler *forces* you to handle them. | ✅ **Yes** | `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException` |
| **Unchecked Exceptions** | Subclasses of `RuntimeException`. Compiler does **not** force handling. | ❌ **No** | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` |
| **Errors (JVM-level)** | Subclasses of `Error`. Fatal, unrecoverable. | ❌ **No** | `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`, `VirtualMachineError` |

### Pillar 3: Logical Errors

**Definition:** The program runs to completion and produces output, but the output is **incorrect** relative to the intended specification.

**Why they occur:** Wrong formula, off-by-one error, incorrect loop boundary, wrong operator (`=` vs `==`), flawed algorithm.

**How they are detected:** **Manual testing, code review, debugging with breakpoints, unit testing frameworks (JUnit).** The compiler cannot help.

**Engineering Utility:** Logical errors are the **most expensive** bugs. They can ship to production and cause financial loss or safety issues (e.g., medical software, aerospace).

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Java's Checked vs Unchecked Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Philosophy:** Checked exceptions represent conditions that **a well-written program *should* anticipate** (e.g., *"the file I want to read might not exist"*). Unchecked exceptions usually represent **programming bugs** (e.g., *"you divided by zero because you forgot to validate input"*). This forces the developer to consciously decide what to do when things go wrong.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet — Error Classification Table

| # | Error Type | Detected By | When? | Recoverable? | Example Code That Causes It | Handling Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Syntax Error** | `javac` compiler | Compile time | Fix the source | `int x = 10` (missing `;`) | Re-compile |
| 2 | **Semantic Error** | `javac` compiler | Compile time | Fix the source | `int x = "hello";` | Re-compile |
| 3 | **Checked Exception** | `javac` + JVM | Compile time + Runtime | **Yes** (must handle) | `new FileReader("absent.txt");` | `try-catch` or `throws` |
| 4 | **Unchecked Exception** | JVM | Runtime | Recommended (not forced) | `int x = 10 / 0;` | `try-catch` (optional) |
| 5 | **Error (JVM)** | JVM | Runtime | **No** (fatal) | Infinite recursion | Do not catch — let JVM die |
| 6 | **Logical Error** | Manual / Debugger | After execution | Fix the algorithm | `int avg = (a + b) / 3;` (should be 2) | Code review, unit tests |

> **Note on units/physical constants:** This topic is conceptual, so the standard "SI units" do not apply. However, all Java memory-related errors use the unit **bytes** (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError` is raised when the JVM heap exceeds the **-Xmx** configured limit, defaulting to **256 MB** for many configurations).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding common Java error types is critical in:
- **Enterprise Application Development** (handling `SQLException` in banking systems)
- **Android App Development** (preventing `NullPointerException` crashes — historically the #1 cause of app crashes)
- **Web Services** (gracefully returning HTTP 500 errors on unhandled `RuntimeException`)
- **Embedded & IoT Systems** (catching `StackOverflowError` in deeply nested sensor processing)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough & Code-Level Diagnosis

This section demonstrates **each error type with a complete code example, the exact compiler/JVM message, and a line-by-line diagnosis.** This is the style KTU board examiners reward with full marks.

---

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Syntax Error

```java
// File: SyntaxDemo.java
public class SyntaxDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int marks = 95
        System.out.println("Marks = " + marks)
    }
}
```

**Line-by-Line Diagnosis:**

| Line | Code | Issue | KTU Mark Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 | `int marks = 95` | **Missing semicolon (`;`)** | A *syntactic error* — Java grammar requires every statement to end with `;`. |
| 5 | `System.out.println("Marks = " + marks)` | **Missing semicolon (`;`)** | A second syntactic error. The compiler typically reports the *first* error it encounters. |

**Compiler Output (Simulated):**
```
SyntaxDemo.java:4: error: ';' expected
        int marks = 95
                      ^
SyntaxDemo.java:5: error: ';' expected
        System.out.println("Marks = " + marks)
                                            ^
2 errors
```

**Fix:**
```java
int marks = 95;        // Added ;
System.out.println("Marks = " + marks);  // Added ;
```

> [!NOTE]
> The arrow `^` in compiler output **points to the exact column** where the parser detected the problem. KTU examiners love when students mention this detail.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Semantic (Compile-Time) Error

```java
public class SemanticDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int count = 10;
        String msg = "Hello";
        count = msg;        // Type mismatch!
    }
}
```

**Diagnosis:**

$$\text{int} \not\Longleftrightarrow \text{String} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Compile-Time Type Mismatch Error}$$

**Compiler Output:**
```
SemanticDemo.java:4: error: incompatible types: String cannot be converted to int
        count = msg;
               ^
1 error
```

**Why it is "semantic" and not "syntactic":** The grammar of the statement is valid (it *could* be an assignment). The problem is the **meaning** — Java is strongly typed and refuses to assign a `String` reference to an `int` primitive.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Unchecked Runtime Exception (`ArithmeticException`)

```java
public class ArithmeticDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int a = 10;
        int b = 0;
        int result = a / b;        // Division by zero
        System.out.println("Result = " + result);
    }
}
```

**Step-by-step execution trace:**

1. **Line 1-3:** Compiler is satisfied — `a` and `b` are both `int`. Grammar is correct. No checked exception. Compilation **succeeds**.
2. **Line 4 (`a / b`):** The JVM executes the integer division instruction. The CPU raises a hardware divide-by-zero trap. The JVM translates this into a **Java exception object** of type `java.lang.ArithmeticException`.
3. **Line 5:** **Never executes.** Control jumps to the JVM's default uncaught-exception handler.

**JVM Output:**
```
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.ArithmeticException: / by zero
        at ArithmeticDemo.main(ArithmeticDemo.java:4)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The first line of the output is the **exception type and message**. The following lines form the **stack trace** — a reverse chronological map of method calls. KTU questions frequently ask you to interpret this output.

**Class Hierarchy of `ArithmeticException`:**
$$\text{Throwable} \rightarrow \text{Exception} \rightarrow \text{RuntimeException} \rightarrow \text{ArithmeticException}$$

Because it extends `RuntimeException`, it is **unchecked** — the compiler did not require us to wrap it in `try-catch`.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Checked Exception (`IOException`)

```java
import java.io.*;
public class CheckedDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt");  // No try-catch!
    }
}
```

**Compiler Output:**
```
CheckedDemo.java:4: error: unreported exception FileNotFoundException;
        must be caught or declared to be thrown
        FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt");
                                 ^
1 error
```

**Key Insight:** The compiler **refuses to compile** this code. It is warning you: *"The constructor `FileReader(String)` declares that it may throw `FileNotFoundException`, which is a checked exception. You MUST either catch it OR declare that your method also throws it."*

**Fix Option 1 — Handle with `try-catch` (introduces Module 4's main topic):**

```java
try {
    FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt");
} catch (FileNotFoundException e) {
    System.out.println("File not found: " + e.getMessage());
}
```

**Fix Option 2 — Defer with `throws`:**

```java
public static void main(String[] args) throws FileNotFoundException {
    FileReader fr = new FileReader("data.txt");
}
```

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Logical Error (The Silent Killer)

```java
public class LogicalDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int a = 80, b = 90, c = 70;
        int total = a + b + c;
        int average = total / 4;       // BUG! Should be 3, not 4.
        System.out.println("Average = " + average);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Average = 80
```

**Diagnosis:**
- The program **compiles** ✅
- The program **runs** ✅
- The output is **80**, but the correct average is $\frac{80+90+70}{3} = 80$. 

Wait — let me correct the values so the bug is visible:

```java
int a = 80, b = 90, c = 70;
int average = (a + b + c) / 4;   // BUG! Should be /3
```

- Expected average: $\frac{240}{3} = 80$
- Actual computed value: $\frac{240}{4} = 60$
- **The compiler cannot detect this.** The algorithm is syntactically and semantically valid.
- **The JVM cannot detect this.** No exception is thrown.
- **Only a human reviewer or a unit test** like `assert average == 80` would catch it.

**Why this is critical:** Logical errors can persist for years in production. The **Ariane 5 rocket failure (1996)** and the **Therac-25 radiation machine** are famous real-world disasters traced to logical errors in software.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 6 — JVM `Error` (`StackOverflowError`)

```java
public class StackDemo {
    public static void infiniteRecursion() {
        infiniteRecursion();        // Calls itself forever
    }
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        infiniteRecursion();
    }
}
```

**JVM Output:**
```
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.StackOverflowError
        at StackDemo.infiniteRecursion(StackDemo.java:2)
        at StackDemo.infiniteRecursion(StackDemo.java:2)
        at StackDemo.infiniteRecursion(StackDemo.java:2)
        ... (thousands of identical lines) ...
```

**Diagnosis:** Each method call pushes a new **stack frame** onto the JVM call stack (default size ~512 KB). Infinite recursion fills the stack until the JVM throws `StackOverflowError`. This is a subclass of `Error`, meaning it is **fatal and should not be caught.**

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison for Exam Use

| Property | Syntax Error | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception | Logical Error |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Caught at compile time?** | ✅ Yes | ✅ Partially | ❌ No | ❌ No |
| **Caught at runtime?** | N/A (never runs) | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No |
| **Program halts?** | Compile fails | Only if unhandled | Only if unhandled | ❌ No, runs to end |
| **Tool to detect** | `javac` | `javac` + `java` | `java` | Manual / JUnit |
| **Hierarchy Root** | Compiler-side | `Exception` | `RuntimeException` | None (in code) |
| **Example class** | N/A | `IOException` | `ArithmeticException` | N/A |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The `java.lang.Throwable` Hierarchy (Mermaid Diagram)

> **Reading the diagram:** Walk **top-to-bottom**. The root is `Throwable`. It splits into **Error** (red — fatal) and **Exception** (blue). `Exception` further splits into direct checked exceptions (purple) and `RuntimeException` (green), under which most unchecked exceptions live.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — How Java Detects & Reports Errors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Source Code\n.java file"]:::input
    B["Javac Compiler"]:::compile
    C{"Syntax /\nSemantic\nErrors?"}:::decision

    J{"Caught by\ntry-catch?"}:::decision

    L["Stack Trace\nPrinted\nProgram Terminates"]:::failure

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C -- Yes --> ERR1["Compilation Aborted\nError message printed"]:::failure
    C -- No --> D
```

> **How to read this architecture:** The diagram shows the **lifecycle of an error** in Java. The two red gates (compile-time and runtime) are where Java intervenes. **Logical errors bypass every gate silently** — that is why they require human vigilance.
