<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**Differentiate between Checked Exceptions and Unchecked Exceptions in Java. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — structured layout expected):**

| Aspect | Checked Exception | Unchecked Exception |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Exceptions that the compiler **checks at compile time** and forces the programmer to handle. | Exceptions that the compiler **does not check**; handling is optional. |
| **Hierarchy** | Subclass of `Exception` (but not `RuntimeException`). | Subclass of `RuntimeException`. |
| **Example** | `IOException`, `SQLException`, `ClassNotFoundException` | `ArithmeticException`, `NullPointerException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` |
| **Handling Mandate** | **Mandatory** — `try-catch` or `throws` clause required. | **Optional** — typically indicates a programming bug. |

**One-line closing line for full marks:** *"Checked exceptions represent recoverable external conditions; unchecked exceptions usually represent programmer mistakes."*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
**Explain the difference between an `Error` and an `Exception` in Java. Why is it not advisable to catch `Error`?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition:** Both `Error` and `Exception` are direct subclasses of `java.lang.Throwable`.
- **`Error`:** Represents **fatal, unrecoverable conditions** at the JVM level. Examples: `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`.
- **`Exception`:** Represents **recoverable conditions** that a well-written program should anticipate and handle. Examples: `IOException`, `ArithmeticException`.
- **Why avoid catching `Error`:** Catching an `Error` does not fix the underlying problem (e.g., if the heap is exhausted, catching `OutOfMemoryError` will not create more memory). It merely delays the inevitable JVM crash and may leave the program in an inconsistent state. Best practice is to let the JVM terminate gracefully so the system can recover.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE format: Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the Java `Throwable` class hierarchy with a neat diagram. List any **four** unchecked exceptions with the situation in which each occurs. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Write a Java program that demonstrates the occurrence of `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` and `NumberFormatException`. Show the **output** of the program when both exceptions are triggered, and explain how a `try-catch` block can handle them. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**The `Throwable` Hierarchy:**

The class `java.lang.Throwable` is the **root** of Java's error and exception hierarchy. It has two direct subclasses:

1. **`Error`** — Represents fatal, unrecoverable problems originating from the JVM itself (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`).
2. **`Exception`** — Represents conditions that applications should anticipate.

`Exception` is further divided into:
- **Checked exceptions** (e.g., `IOException`, `SQLException`) — compiler-enforced handling.
- **Unchecked exceptions** — subclasses of `RuntimeException`.

**Textual Hierarchy Diagram (draw this in the exam):**

```
                    java.lang.Throwable
                    /               \
                   /                 \
              Error                Exception
           (fatal)               /          \
                              /            \
                       IOException      RuntimeException
                       SQLException         (Unchecked)
                                        ArithmeticException
                                        NullPointerException
                              ...
```

**Four Unchecked Exceptions:**

| # | Exception | When It Occurs |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | `ArithmeticException` | Integer division by zero (e.g., `10/0`). |
| 2 | `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException` | Accessing an array with an index `< 0` or `>= length`. |
| 3 | `NullPointerException` | Invoking a method on an object reference that is `null`. |
| 4 | `NumberFormatException` | Passing a non-numeric `String` to `Integer.parseInt()`. |

**Award marks:** Hierarchy = 3 marks, four exceptions = 4 marks.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

**Java Program:**

```java
public class ExceptionDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Trigger 1: ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException
        try {
            int[] arr = {10, 20, 30};
            System.out.println("Accessing index 5: " + arr[5]);
        } catch (ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught: " + e);
        }

        // Trigger 2: NumberFormatException
        try {
            int num = Integer.parseInt("Hello123");
            System.out.println("Parsed: " + num);
        } catch (NumberFormatException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught: " + e);
        }

        System.out.println("Program continued normally.");
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Caught: java.lang.ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException: Index 5 out of bounds for length 3
Caught: java.lang.NumberFormatException: For input string: "Hello123"
Program continued normally.
```

**Explanation:**

- **First `try-catch` block:** Tries to access `arr[5]`, but the array has only indices 0, 1, 2. The JVM throws `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`, which is caught and printed. **Normal flow continues.**
- **Second `try-catch` block:** Tries to parse `"Hello123"` as an integer. Since the string contains non-numeric characters, the JVM throws `NumberFormatException`, which is caught and printed.
- **Final `println`:** Proves the program did **not** crash despite both errors. This demonstrates the value of exception handling.

**Award marks:** Working program = 4 marks, output = 1 mark, explanation = 2 marks.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** What is a syntax error? How is it different from a logical error? Give a Java code example for each and explain why one is detected by the compiler and the other is not. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Differentiate between the `throws` keyword and the `throw` keyword in Java. Write a small Java method that uses both to propagate a custom checked condition. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]**

**Syntax Error:** A violation of Java's **grammar rules** — detected by the compiler **before** the program runs.

**Logical Error:** A flaw in the **algorithm or business logic** — the program compiles and runs but produces wrong output. Detected only by manual review, debugging, or testing.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Syntax Error | Logical Error |
|---|---|---|
| Detected at | Compile time | Runtime / post-execution |
| Detected by | `javac` compiler | Human / unit tests |
| Result | Compilation fails | Wrong output |
| Effort to fix | Easy (compiler points to line) | Difficult (no automatic hint) |

**Example — Syntax Error:**
```java
public class SynErr {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int x = 10  // Missing ;
        System.out.println(x);
    }
}
```
**Compiler Output:** `error: ';' expected`. The compiler aborts before running.

**Example — Logical Error:**
```java
public class LogErr {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        int radius = 5;
        double area = 2 * 3.14 * radius;   // BUG: Should be πr² not 2πr
        System.out.println("Area = " + area);   // Prints 31.4 instead of 78.5
    }
}
```
**No error is reported.** The program runs and prints `31.4`, which is incorrect. The formula computed the *circumference* (2πr) instead of the *area* (πr²). The compiler cannot catch this because all types and syntax are valid.

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]**

| Aspect | `throw` | `throws` |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Used to **explicitly throw** an exception object. | Used in a method **signature** to declare exceptions it may pass upward. |
| **Location** | Inside the method body. | After the method signature (and parameters). |
| **Number** | Throws **one** exception at a time. | Can declare **multiple** exceptions separated by commas. |
| **Syntax** | `throw new ExceptionType("message");` | `void myMethod() throws ExceptionType1, ExceptionType2 { }` |

**Demonstration Program:**

```java
public class ThrowThrowsDemo {
    // 'throws' declares that this method may throw a checked-style condition
    static void checkAge(int age) throws ArithmeticException {
        if (age < 18) {
            // 'throw' explicitly creates and throws an exception object
            throw new ArithmeticException("Age " + age + " is not eligible to vote.");
        } else {
            System.out.println("Welcome! You may vote.");
        }
    }

    public static void main(String[] args) {
        try {
            checkAge(15);
        } catch (ArithmeticException e) {
            System.out.println("Caught: " + e.getMessage());
        }
        checkAge(25);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Caught: Age 15 is not eligible to vote.
Welcome! You may vote.
```

**Explanation:**
- The `checkAge` method uses **`throws ArithmeticException`** in its signature to signal that it may pass the exception to the caller.
- Inside, the **`throw new ArithmeticException(...)`** statement **creates and dispatches** the exception object.
- The `main` method wraps the call in a **`try-catch`** to handle the situation gracefully.

**Award marks:** Comparison table = 3 marks, working program = 3 marks, output = 1 mark.

---

> [!WARNING]
> ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls
>
> 1. **Confusing `Error` (general English) with `Error` (Java class):** When a question says *"What is an error?"*, examiners expect the **three-fold classification** (Syntax / Runtime / Logical), not just a description of `java.lang.Error`. Mixing them up = **−2 marks**.
>
> 2. **Forgetting to write the class hierarchy path:** If you write *"ArithmeticException extends Throwable"*, you lose 1 mark. The correct chain is `Throwable → Exception → RuntimeException → ArithmeticException`. Examiners **look for the intermediate `RuntimeException` link**.
>
> 3. **Not showing output for runtime exceptions:** In code-based questions, you must **write the expected JVM output** after the program. Skipping output = **−2 marks** in 14-mark questions.
>
> 4. **Conflating `throw` and `throws`:** Students often write *"throws is used to throw an exception"* — technically true, but the precise answer requires the **distinction** (keyword vs signature declaration). Examiners deduct 1 mark for imprecise language.
>
> 5. **Skipping the stack-trace interpretation:** If a question shows a stack trace, write what each line means: the first line is the **exception type and message**; subsequent lines are the **call stack** in reverse chronological order.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Three families of Java errors:** Syntax (compile-time), Runtime (exceptions), Logical (silent).
- ✅ **Root of all:** `java.lang.Throwable` — has two children: `Error` and `Exception`.
- ✅ **`Error`** → fatal, unrecoverable, JVM-level (e.g., `OutOfMemoryError`). **Do not catch.**
- ✅ **`Exception`** → recoverable, application-level. Further splits into **Checked** and **Unchecked**.
- ✅ **Checked exceptions** → subclasses of `Exception` (NOT `RuntimeException`). Compiler **forces** handling. Example: `IOException`, `SQLException`.
- ✅ **Unchecked exceptions** → subclasses of `RuntimeException`. Compiler **does not force** handling. Example: `NullPointerException`, `ArithmeticException`, `ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException`.
- ✅ **Logical errors** → no compiler help, no JVM help; only **debugging, code review, JUnit** can find them.
- ✅ **`throw`** → keyword used inside method body to explicitly throw an exception object.
- ✅ **`throws`** → keyword used in method signature to declare that the method may pass exceptions to its caller.
- ✅ **Stack trace reading** → first line = exception type & message; remaining lines = call stack.
- ✅ **Default default Java heap size hint:** `-Xmx` (default often **256 MB**); exceeding it triggers `OutOfMemoryError`.
- ✅ **Default thread stack size:** typically **512 KB**; exceeding it triggers `StackOverflowError`.
- ✅ **Real-world mantra:** *"The compiler catches your spelling; the JVM catches your surprises; only humans catch your thinking mistakes."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Using Java Error Information for Root Cause Analysis and JVM Tuning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT611 - OBJECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING IN JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Using Java Error Information for Root Cause Analysis and JVM Tuning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Java Error Information** refers to the structured diagnostic data emitted by the Java Virtual Machine (JVM) and the Java standard library when an exceptional condition, runtime fault, or fatal error occurs. This information encompasses stack traces, exception messages, heap dumps, thread dumps, garbage collection (GC) logs, JVM crash logs (hs_err_pid files), and standard error/standard output streams.

**Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** in the context of Java applications is the systematic, evidence-driven methodology of tracing a reported defect, performance regression, or production outage back to its fundamental originating point by inspecting JVM-emitted error information, log files, runtime artifacts, and execution traces.

**JVM Tuning** is the disciplined engineering practice of configuring JVM command-line flags, garbage collection algorithms, heap and metaspace sizes, JIT compiler thresholds, and runtime monitoring parameters to achieve target service-level objectives (SLOs) such as latency, throughput, memory footprint, and startup time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The 2024 scheme explicitly links error interpretation to **observability** — the ability to ask questions about a system's internal state from its external outputs. Mastering error information is a prerequisite to performing meaningful JVM tuning.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine you are a **car mechanic** presented with a vehicle that will not start. You do not guess — you plug in an **OBD-II diagnostic scanner**, which prints a list of fault codes (P0301 = cylinder 1 misfire), freeze-frame data (RPM, speed, temperature at fault time), and a live sensor readout. The car's computer has already done the hard work of recording *what happened, when, and under what conditions*. Your job is to interpret this information to find the *root cause* (a faulty spark plug), not just the symptom (engine does not start).

**Mapping the analogy to Java:**

| Car Diagnostic Concept | Java Equivalent |
|---|---|
| OBD-II fault code (e.g. P0301) | Exception class name (e.g. `NullPointerException`) |
| Freeze-frame snapshot (state at fault) | Stack trace (call stack at the moment of failure) |
| Engine oil & coolant snapshot | Heap dump (memory contents at a moment) |
| Live dashboard of all cylinders firing | Thread dump (state of every thread) |
| Fuel-economy log over a trip | GC log (garbage collection activity over time) |
| Mechanic adjusting the carburetor | JVM tuning (adjusting `-Xmx`, GC algorithm, etc.) |

Just as a skilled mechanic does not replace the entire engine when a spark plug is faulty, a skilled Java developer does not "increase the heap to 16 GB" when an `OutOfMemoryError` occurs. They first **read the error information**, identify the root cause, and then tune accordingly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight (Board Exam Favourite):** More than **80%** of production Java performance issues are diagnosable from a careful reading of three artifacts: the **stack trace**, the **heap dump**, and the **GC log**. Tuning without diagnosis is superstition, not engineering.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants (Highlighted for Memory)

The following metrics are universally accepted benchmarks used in JVM tuning decisions and must be memorized:

- **Default initial heap size (`-Xms`)**: 1/64 of physical RAM (server-class machines, since Java 5)
- **Default maximum heap size (`-Xmx`)**: 1/4 of physical RAM (server-class machines, since Java 5)
- **Young Generation default ratio**: `NewRatio = 2` (i.e. Young:Old = 1:2)
- **Eden : Survivor default ratio**: `SurvivorRatio = 8` (Eden = 8/10, each Survivor = 1/10)
- **Stack size per thread (`-Xss`)**: defaults to **512 KB** on 64-bit Linux, **1 MB** on 64-bit Windows
- **Permanent Generation removed in Java 8**, replaced by **Metaspace** (native memory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Permanent Generation (`PermGen`) was removed in Java 8.** All references to `PermGen` in legacy codebases or older interview questions should be mapped to **Metaspace** in Java 8+. The KTU 2024 syllabus assumes Java 11+ or Java 17 LTS as the reference runtime.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Java Error Information

Java error information can be classified along two axes: **when it is generated** and **what aspect of the JVM it captures**.

#### A. By Generation Trigger

1. **Compile-Time Errors** — Detected by `javac`; syntax, type-mismatch, missing imports. Not runtime artifacts; cannot be used for RCA of deployed systems.
2. **Runtime Exceptions (`RuntimeException` and subclasses)** — Unchecked; caused by logic defects, null dereferences, array bounds, illegal arguments, etc. Emits a **stack trace** to `System.err`.
3. **Checked Exceptions** — Forced by the compiler; must be `try/catch`/`throws`-declared. Indicates recoverable conditions (I/O, network, parsing).
4. **Errors (`java.lang.Error` and subclasses)** — Severe, unrecoverable conditions. Examples: `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`, `InternalError`, `LinkageError`, `VirtualMachineError`. Most emit additional artifacts such as heap dumps or `hs_err_pid.log`.
5. **JVM Crash / Fatal Error** — SIGSEGV, native library faults, JIT compiler crashes. Produces a binary plus text crash log named `hs_err_pid<PID>.log` in the working directory.

#### B. By Captured Aspect

| Artifact | Captures | Typical File / Stream | Size Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| Stack Trace | Call hierarchy at the moment of exception | `System.err`, application logs | 1–50 KB |
| Heap Dump | All reachable objects in heap | `java.hprof`, `java.core`, or `*.hprof` | 100 MB – 10 GB |
| Thread Dump | State and stack of every thread | `jstack`, `jcmd`, kill `-3` (SIGQUIT) | 5–200 KB |
| GC Log | Garbage collection pauses and reclamation | `-Xlog:gc*` (Java 9+) or `-Xloggc:` | 10 MB – 1 GB / day |
| JVM Crash Log | Native fault context, registers, threads, heap | `hs_err_pid<PID>.log` | 50–500 KB |
| JFR (JDK Flight Recorder) | Continuous low-overhead profiling | `*.jfr` | Configurable, 1–500 MB |

### 2.2 Anatomy of a Java Stack Trace

A stack trace is the most frequently encountered Java error artifact. It is generated automatically by the JVM whenever an exception propagates uncaught to the top of a thread's call stack. Its anatomy is:

```
Exception in thread "main" java.lang.NullPointerException
    at com.ktu.bank.Account.withdraw(Account.java:42)
    at com.ktu.bank.ATMService.process(ATMService.java:87)
    at com.ktu.bank.Main.main(Main.java:23)
Caused by: java.io.IOException: Network unreachable
    at java.base/sun.nio.ch.Net.translateException(Net.java:579)
    ... 5 more
```

**Component-by-component breakdown:**

- **Exception in thread "main"** — Identifies the thread (here, the main thread). In multi-threaded servers, this is critical for identifying the offending worker.
- **Exception class fully-qualified name** — `java.lang.NullPointerException` tells you *what category* of fault.
- **Top frame** — `Account.withdraw(Account.java:42)` — the **deepest** frame is almost always the **root cause** (where the NPE actually happened).
- **Subsequent frames** — propagate upward through the call chain. The **bottom-most** frame is the entry point (`main`).
- **Caused by: ...** — exception chaining via `Throwable.initCause()` or the `cause` constructor argument. Indicates a higher-level operation failed because of a lower-level cause.
- **`... N more`** — the "ellipsis + N" indicates N additional frames identical to the previous cause's chain (output truncation for readability).

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Direction:** Always read a stack trace from **top to bottom** (deepest to shallowest). The first frame is the *symptom location*; the last frame is the *entry point*. The "Caused by" chain is read in the same order.

### 2.3 Heap Dump: The Memory Snapshot

A heap dump is a **serialized snapshot of all objects reachable from GC roots** (live threads, JNI references, static fields) at a specific instant. It is the primary artifact for diagnosing memory leaks and `OutOfMemoryError`.

**How to trigger a heap dump:**

```
# Automatic on OutOfMemoryError (recommended for production)
java -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError -XX:HeapDumpPath=/var/log/heapdumps/ MyApp

# On-demand via jmap (requires same JDK user)
jmap -dump:format=b,file=heap.hprof <PID>

# On-demand via jcmd (preferred, JDK 8u40+)
jcmd <PID> GC.heap_dump /var/log/heapdumps/heap.hprof
```

**Anatomy of `OutOfMemoryError` triggering heap dump:**

```
java.lang.OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space
Dumping heap to /var/log/heapdumps/heap.hprof ...
Heap dump file created [2,134,567,890 bytes in 8.412 secs]
```

**Analysis tools (must know for KTU):**

- **Eclipse MAT (Memory Analyzer Tool)** — Dominant tool; computes *leak suspects*, *retained heap*, *dominator tree*.
- **VisualVM** — Lightweight; visualizes class histograms.
- **JHat** — Built-in, read-only; obsolete but referenced in older syllabi.
- **JDK Mission Control (JMC)** — Correlates JFR + heap dump.

**Two key MAT metrics:**

- **Shallow Heap** — Memory consumed by the object itself (header + instance fields, aligned to 8 bytes on 64-bit).
- **Retained Heap** — Total memory that would be reclaimed if this object were GC'd (i.e. the object's shallow heap *plus* the shallow heap of all objects that would become unreachable if it were removed).

### 2.4 Thread Dump: The Concurrency Snapshot

A thread dump captures the **state, call stack, and lock ownership of every Java thread** at a single instant. It is the primary tool for diagnosing deadlocks, thread starvation, and slow / hanging requests.

**How to trigger:**

```
# Linux / macOS — send SIGQUIT (signal 3) — does NOT kill the JVM
kill -3 <PID>

# Cross-platform via JDK tool
jstack <PID>
jcmd <PID> Thread.print
```

**Critical thread states to recognize:**

| State (in dump) | Meaning |
|---|---|
| `RUNNABLE` | Executing in JVM or OS |
| `BLOCKED` | Waiting to acquire a monitor lock (held by another thread) |
| `WAITING` | `Object.wait()`, `LockSupport.park()`, `join()` |
| `TIMED_WAITING` | `sleep()`, `wait(timeout)`, `parkNanos` |
| `NEW`, `TERMINATED` | Lifecycle states |

**Deadlock signature (auto-detected by `jstack`):**

```
Found one Java-level deadlock:
=============================
"Thread-1":
  waiting to lock monitor 0x00007f8b1c0d6e08 (object 0x000000076b6a4b78, a java.lang.Object),
  which is held by "Thread-2"
"Thread-2":
  waiting to lock monitor 0x00007f8b1c0d6ca0 (object 0x000000076b6a4b68, a java.lang.Object),
  which is held by "Thread-1"
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** A deadlock is detected by the JVM when it finds a cycle in the *wait-for graph*. `jstack` performs this detection automatically and prints the cycle at the top of the dump.

### 2.5 GC Log: The Performance Time-Series

The GC log is a chronological record of every garbage collection event. It is indispensable for tuning heap sizing, GC algorithm choice, and pause-time targets.

**Modern unified logging syntax (Java 9+):**

```
java -Xlog:gc*,gc+heap=trace,gc+age=trace,safepoint:file=gc.log:time,uptime,level,tags:filecount=10,filesize=50M MyApp
```

**Key fields per GC line (illustrative):**

```
[2024-11-15T10:23:45.123+0530][12.345s][info][gc] GC(42) Pause Young (Normal) (G1 Evacuation Pause)
[2024-11-15T10:23:45.456+0530][12.678s][info][gc] GC(42) Pre Evacuate Collection Set: 0.5ms
[2024-11-15T10:23:45.789+0530][13.011s][info][gc] GC(42) Evacuate Collection Set: 12.3ms
[2024-11-15T10:23:46.012+0530][13.234s][info][gc] GC(42) Post Evacuate Collection Set: 1.1ms
[2024-11-15T10:23:46.123+0530][13.345s][info][gc] GC(42) Other: 0.8ms
[2024-11-15T10:23:46.234+0530][13.456s][info][gc] GC(42) Eden regions: 156 -> 0
[2024-11-15T10:23:46.345+0530][13.567s][info][gc] GC(42) Survivor regions: 4 -> 6
[2024-11-15T10:23:46.456+0530][13.678s][info][gc] GC(42) Old regions: 234 -> 245
[2024-11-15T10:23:46.567+0530][13.789s][info][gc] GC(42) Humongous regions: 0 -> 0
[2024-11-15T10:23:46.678+0530][13.890s][info][gc] GC(42) Metaspace: 78M -> 78M
[2024-11-15T10:23:46.789+0530][14.001s][info][gc] GC(42) Pause: 14.7ms
[2024-11-15T10:23:46.890+0530][14.102s][info][gc] GC(42) User=0.08s Sys=0.00s Real=0.01s
```

**The five numbers that matter in any GC line:**

1. **Pause time** — wall-clock duration the application threads were stopped.
2. **Eden/Before → After** — how much was reclaimed in each region.
3. **Promotion rate** — Survivor → Old transfer; high promotion = Old Gen pressure.
4. **Cumulative pause total** — sum over JVM lifetime (the SLA metric).
5. **`User` vs `Real`** — `User` is CPU time consumed (multi-core adds); `Real` is wall-clock pause. Discrepancy reveals parallelism.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **mathematically verifiable** from OpenJDK source and the Java SE specification. They are the only formulas that appear on KTU 2024 examination papers for this topic.

| # | Formula / Flag | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X_{ms} \le X_{mx}$ | Initial heap $\le$ maximum heap | `1g`, `512m` |
| 2 | $X_{mn} = X_{mx} \cdot \dfrac{1}{N + 1}$ | Young Gen size from `NewRatio=N` | e.g. $N=2 \Rightarrow 1/3$ |
| 3 | $E = \dfrac{Y}{S+2} \cdot (S+2) = Y \cdot \dfrac{S}{S+2}$ | Eden size from `SurvivorRatio=S` and Young=Y | `S=8` $\Rightarrow$ Eden = 8/10 of Young |
| 4 | $S_{each} = \dfrac{Y}{S+2}$ | One Survivor space (two exist) | `S=8` $\Rightarrow$ 1/10 each |
| 5 | $T_{pause} = f(N_{GC}, t_{GC})$ | Total pause = count $\times$ mean pause | Optimize: lower both |
| 6 | $Throughput = \dfrac{T_{app}}{T_{app} + T_{GC}}$ | Application time / total time | Target $\ge 95\%$ |
| 7 | $Footprint = X_{mx} + M_{meta} + S_{native}$ | Total RSS = max heap + metaspace + native | `jcmd <pid> VM.native_memory` |
| 8 | $N_{threads}^{max} = \dfrac{X_{mx}}{X_{ss}}$ | Theoretical thread cap from stack | e.g. `4g / 512k = 8192` |
| 9 | $GC_{overhead} = \dfrac{T_{GC}}{T_{wall}}$ | GC time fraction | `>10%` is suspicious |
| 10 | $Promotion_{rate} = \dfrac{\Delta Old}{\Delta t}$ | Bytes/sec moving Young → Old | High = tuning Old Gen |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** When asked "how to tune a Java application", present the **systematic flow**: (1) capture baseline, (2) read GC log, (3) identify bottleneck (Young or Old), (4) adjust one parameter, (5) measure, (6) repeat. Never change more than one parameter between measurements.

### 2.7 The JVM Tuning Process (Engineering Methodology)

The scientifically rigorous tuning cycle has **six steps**, each tied to a specific artifact:

1. **Define SLA** — Target pause time (e.g. $\le 100$ ms p99), target throughput ($\ge 99\%$), target footprint (e.g. $\le 8$ GB).
2. **Baseline measurement** — Enable GC logging, JFR, and application metrics.
3. **Hypothesis** — "Old Gen is filling faster than it can be collected; we have a leak or undersized Old Gen."
4. **Experiment** — Change one parameter (e.g. `-Xmx`, `-XX:NewRatio`, or switch GC algorithm).
5. **Measurement** — Compare the post-change metrics to the baseline.
6. **Iterate or roll back** — Keep improvements; revert regressions.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Golden Rule" of JVM Tuning (board exam favourite):** *Set `-Xms` equal to `-Xmx`* in production. Resizing the heap at runtime is a **stop-the-world** operation and produces massive pauses. Telling the JVM "your heap will not grow" lets it skip the resize logic entirely.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Reading a Stack Trace to Find the Root Cause

**Scenario:** A KTU student receives the following log from a deployed banking application:

```
ERROR 2024-11-15 10:23:45 [http-nio-8080-exec-3] com.ktu.bank.TransferService - Transfer failed for account 12345
java.lang.NullPointerException: Cannot invoke "com.ktu.bank.Account.getBalance()" because "destination" is null
    at com.ktu.bank.TransferService.execute(TransferService.java:67)
    at com.ktu.bank.TransferController.transfer(TransferController.java:34)
    at sun.reflect.NativeMethodAccessorImpl.invoke0(Native Method)
    at sun.reflect.NativeMethodAccessorImpl.invoke(NativeMethodAccessorImpl.java:77)
    at sun.reflect.DelegatingMethodAccessorImpl.invoke(DelegatingMethodAccessorImpl.java:43)
    at java.lang.reflect.Method.invoke(Method.java:568)
    at org.springframework.web.method.support.InvocableHandlerMethod.doInvoke(InvocableHandlerMethod.java:189)
    at org.springframework.web.method.support.InvocableHandlerMethod.invokeForRequest(InvocableHandlerMethod.java:138)
    ... [Spring/Hibernate frames truncated for readability]
Caused by: java.lang.NullPointerException: destination account lookup returned null
    at com.ktu.bank.repository.AccountRepository.findById(AccountRepository.java:28)
    at com.ktu.bank.TransferService.execute(TransferService.java:62)
    ... 14 more
```

**Step-by-step analysis:**

**Step 1 — Identify the thread.** The thread is `http-nio-8080-exec-3`, a Tomcat worker thread. This is a *request-handling* thread, so the bug is on a real user code path, not a background job.

**Step 2 — Identify the exception type.** It is a `NullPointerException`, an *unchecked* `RuntimeException`. The JVM did not crash; the request failed and was caught by the application's error handler (`TransferService - Transfer failed...`).

**Step 3 — Read the message carefully.** The message reads: *"Cannot invoke `getBalance()` because `destination` is null."* This is a *helpful NPE message* introduced in **Java 14 (JEP 358)**. It tells us:
- The NPE occurred at a call to `getBalance()`.
- The receiver expression `destination` evaluated to `null`.

**Step 4 — Read the top frame (root symptom location).** `TransferService.execute(TransferService.java:67)`. Open the source and read line 67. It will be a line similar to:
```java
double balance = destination.getBalance();   // <-- line 67
```

**Step 5 — Read the "Caused by" chain.** The *root cause* is **not** line 67. The "Caused by" frame points to `AccountRepository.findById(AccountRepository.java:28)`, which *returned* `null` because the destination account does not exist (or the lookup query is wrong). The deeper `Caused by` frame is the *true root cause*; line 67 is the *symptom*.

**Step 6 — Formulate the fix.** Two correct fixes exist:
- (a) Defensive: Add a null check on line 67 — `if (destination == null) throw new AccountNotFoundException(...);`
- (b) Correctness: Fix the repository call at `AccountRepository.java:28` so it throws a proper exception when the account is missing, and let `TransferService` propagate that as an HTTP 404.

**Conclusion:** The stack trace's *outer* exception is the *symptom*; the *innermost* `Caused by` is the *root cause*. Always chase the chain to its end.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Trap (Common Mistake):** Students frequently answer "the bug is in `TransferService.java:67`" because it is the first frame. The correct board-exam answer identifies the *root cause* as the repository method at line 28 of `AccountRepository`. Always distinguish **symptom location** from **root cause**.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Deriving Heap Sizing from SLA

**Scenario:** Your application needs to handle 200 requests/second, each allocating 5 MB of short-lived objects. The 99th-percentile pause budget is 100 ms. You have 16 GB of physical RAM, 8 cores. Derive initial JVM flags.

**Step 1 — Calculate Young Generation size for the allocation rate.**

Allocation rate $A = 200 \cdot 5\,\text{MB/s} = 1000\,\text{MB/s} = 1\,\text{GB/s}$.

Young Gen must be at least $A \cdot T_{pause}$ to avoid allocating faster than we can collect. With pause budget $T_{pause} = 0.1$ s:

$$Y_{min} = A \cdot T_{pause} = 1\,\text{GB/s} \cdot 0.1\,\text{s} = 100\,\text{MB}$$

Round up for safety and to reduce promotion pressure: $Y \approx 1$–$2$ GB.

**Step 2 — Decide on `NewRatio`.** For a low-pause, allocation-heavy workload, set Young Gen to about 1/3 of the heap. With $N = 2$ (`-XX:NewRatio=2`):

$$Y = \frac{X_{mx}}{N+1} = \frac{X_{mx}}{3}$$

**Step 3 — Choose the maximum heap.** Reserve 4 GB for OS, 1 GB for native/Metaspace/other. Heap budget $\le 16 - 5 = 11$ GB. Round to $X_{mx} = 8$ GB (conservative, leaves headroom).

**Step 4 — Verify Young Gen derived:**

$$Y = \frac{8\,\text{GB}}{3} \approx 2.67\,\text{GB}$$

This is well above the 100 MB minimum, so the allocation rate is sustainable.

**Step 5 — Set `SurvivorRatio`.** With `SurvivorRatio=8`:

$$E = \frac{Y \cdot S}{S + 2} = \frac{2.67 \cdot 8}{10} \approx 2.14\,\text{GB}$$
$$S_{each} = \frac{Y}{S+2} = \frac{2.67}{10} \approx 0.27\,\text{GB}$$

This is sufficient to hold survivors between minor GCs.

**Step 6 — Choose GC algorithm.** Allocation-heavy, low-pause target → **G1 GC** (`-XX:+UseG1GC`) is the default since Java 9. Set pause goal: `-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=100`.

**Step 7 — Final flag set:**

```
-Xms8g -Xmx8g -XX:NewRatio=2 -XX:SurvivorRatio=8
-XX:+UseG1GC -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=100
-XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError -XX:HeapDumpPath=/var/log/heapdumps/
-Xlog:gc*,gc+heap=trace:file=/var/log/gc.log:time,uptime,level,tags:filecount=10,filesize=50M
```

> [!NOTE]
> **The formula $Y = X_{mx}/(N+1)$** is derived from the JVM's invariant: heap = Young + Old, and NewRatio = Old/Young = N, so $X_{mx} = Y + NY = Y(N+1)$, giving $Y = X_{mx}/(N+1)$. The same formula appears on KTU numerical questions.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Diagnosing a Memory Leak from a Heap Dump

**Scenario:** The application throws `OutOfMemoryError: Java heap space` after ~6 hours of uptime. A heap dump has been captured. Eclipse MAT reports the "Leak Suspects" pie chart:

```
1,145,234 instances of com.ktu.bank.dto.TransactionDTO
      |— 289,456 instances of com.ktu.bank.service.TransactionCache
      |— 89,231 instances of com.ktu.bank.dao.AccountDAO
Retained Heap: 4.2 GB of 8 GB total (52%)
```

**Step 1 — Verify with the Dominator Tree.** The top dominator in MAT is the static `CacheHolder.INSTANCE` (singleton) holding a `HashMap<Long, TransactionDTO>`. The map's keys are account IDs.

**Step 2 — Inspect the keyset.** All 1.1 million map entries have unique keys → no replacement, only growth → this is a **classic unbounded growth leak**.

**Step 3 — Trace the source code.** Search the codebase for `CacheHolder`. The offending code:

```java
public class CacheHolder {
    public static final Map<Long, TransactionDTO> CACHE = new HashMap<>();
    public static void put(TransactionDTO t) {
        CACHE.put(t.getAccountId(), t);   // unbounded put
    }
}
```

**Step 4 — Formulate the fix.** Three correct strategies, in increasing order of engineering quality:

1. **Bounded cache** — Replace `HashMap` with `LinkedHashMap` configured for LRU eviction, or use a `ConcurrentHashMap` with a `Caffeine` cache (`Caffeine.newBuilder().maximumSize(100_000).build()`).
2. **TTL cache** — Use `Caffeine` with `expireAfterWrite(Duration.ofMinutes(10))`.
3. **Weak references** — `WeakHashMap` (rarely appropriate; keys can vanish any GC).

**Step 5 — Verify the fix.** Re-deploy with `-XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError`. Re-run load test. Heap should plateau at $\approx 500$ MB instead of growing to 8 GB.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagnostic Rule:** A heap dump's *Leak Suspects* report is a *hypothesis*, not a verdict. The top dominator may be a legitimate singleton holding the application's *working set*. The leak is confirmed only if the size of the structure grows **monotonically** across multiple heap dumps taken at intervals (e.g. 10 min, 30 min, 60 min).

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Diagnosing a Deadlock from a Thread Dump

**Scenario:** A multi-threaded application hangs. The user issues `jstack <PID>`.

**Step 1 — Look at the top of the output.** JVM prints:
```
Found one Java-level deadlock:
=============================
"Worker-1":
  waiting to lock <0x000000076b6a4b78> (a java.lang.Object),
  which is held by "Worker-2"
"Worker-2":
  waiting to lock <0x000000076b6a4b68> (a java.lang.Object),
  which is held by "Worker-1"
```

**Step 2 — Map the addresses to code.** Both workers are in methods:
- `Worker-1` is in `AccountService.transfer()` holding lock on `B`, waiting for `A`.
- `Worker-2` is in `AccountService.transfer()` holding lock on `A`, waiting for `B`.

**Step 3 — Identify the bug.** Both threads acquired their first lock in a *different order* — a classic **lock-ordering deadlock**.

**Step 4 — Fix.** Impose a global lock ordering rule (e.g. always lock the account with the smaller ID first):

```java
public void transfer(Account a, Account b, double amount) {
    Account first  = (a.getId() < b.getId()) ? a : b;
    Account second = (a.getId() < b.getId()) ? b : a;
    synchronized (first) {
        synchronized (second) {
            // perform transfer
        }
    }
}
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** For "explain how you would diagnose a deadlock" questions, list: (1) take thread dump, (2) `jstack` auto-detects and prints the cycle, (3) map addresses to monitor objects, (4) trace back to source, (5) impose lock ordering, (6) verify with re-test.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Quantifying the Impact of `-Xms = -Xmx`

**Question:** A JVM starts with `-Xms256m -Xmx4g` and observes an initial **3.2-second pause** at startup. The application has no other configuration changes. What is the recommended change, and what is the *mechanism* of the improvement?

**Step-by-step reasoning:**

**Step 1 — Identify the cause of the pause.** With `-Xms256m`, the JVM starts with a small heap. As the application loads, the JVM must repeatedly **expand the heap** by requesting more memory from the OS. Each expansion is a *stop-the-world* operation involving a full GC, heap reallocation, and pointer updates across all threads.

**Step 2 — Quantify the number of expansions.** From 256 MB to 4 GB, the heap grows by $\Delta = 4096 - 256 = 3840$ MB. With the default expansion policy (doubling), the number of expansions is $\lceil \log_2(16) \rceil = 4$ expansions, each with a stop-the-world GC.

**Step 3 — Apply the fix.** Set `-Xms4g` (or remove `-Xms` to use the same value as `-Xmx`).

**Step 4 — Predict the outcome.** The JVM will:
- Request 4 GB of memory **once** at startup, in a single mmap call.
- Skip all four intermediate expansions.
- Each of the four stop-the-world GCs is eliminated.
- Trade-off: longer startup, **lower steady-state pause variance**.

**Step 5 — When NOT to do this.** For development environments or short-lived CLI tools, keeping a small `-Xms` reduces *committed* memory and lets other processes use the RAM. The rule applies primarily to **production servers**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Favourite:** "Explain why production JVMs should set `-Xms = -Xmx`." The model answer must mention: (1) heap resizing is stop-the-world, (2) it produces long pauses, (3) it complicates capacity planning because the RSS is not stable, (4) the trade-off is a longer startup and loss of memory elasticity.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Root Cause Analysis Workflow

The following diagram maps the **end-to-end error information analysis flow**, from incident detection to JVM tuning decisions. It is rendered as a Mermaid block diagram with strict compliance to the KTU engine's safe-rendering rules: every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed with a letter, and labels are pure uppercase alphanumeric text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[START - INCIDENT DETECTED] --> A1{ERROR TYPE}
    A1 -->|RUNTIME EXCEPTION| A2[CAPTURE STACK TRACE]
    A1 -->|OUT OF MEMORY| A3[CAPTURE HEAP DUMP]
    A1 -->|HANG OR SLOW| A4[CAPTURE THREAD DUMP]
    A1 -->|JVM CRASH| A5[READ HS ERR LOG]
    A1 -->|HIGH LATENCY| A6[COLLECT GC LOG]

    A2 --> B1[IDENTIFY SYMPTOM LOCATION]
    B1 --> B2[CHASE CAUSED BY CHAIN]
    B2 --> B3[PINPOINT ROOT CAUSE]
    B3 --> C1[FIX CODE]

    A3 --> B4[OPEN IN ECLIPSE MAT]
    B4 --> B5[RUN LEAK SUSPECTS REPORT]
    B5 --> B6{LEAK CONFIRMED}
    B6 -->|YES, ACROSS MULTIPLE DUMPS| C2[FIX MEMORY HOLDERS]
    B6 -->|NO, WORKING SET ONLY| C3[INCREASE XMX OR REDUCE FOOTPRINT]

    A4 --> B7[PARSE THREAD STATES]
    B7 --> B8{DEADLOCK DETECTED}
    B8 -->|YES| C4[IMPOSE LOCK ORDERING]
    B8 -->|NO| B9[CHECK BLOCKED AND WAITING]
    B9 --> C5[FIX CONTENTION OR STARVATION]

    A5 --> B10[IDENTIFY CRASH CONTEXT]
    B10 --> B11[UPGRADE JVM OR NATIVE LIB]

    A6 --> B12[COMPUTE GC OVERHEAD]
    B12 --> B13{PAUSE WITHIN SLA}
    B13 -->|YES| A0
    B13 -->|NO| C6[ADJUST JVM FLAGS]
    C6 --> A0
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each diamond (`{}`) is a decision point; each rectangle (`[]`) is a step; arrows are directed transitions. The flow is **cyclic** because tuning is iterative — you measure, change, measure again.

---

### 4.2 JVM Memory Architecture (Schematic Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph H[JVM PROCESS ADDRESS SPACE]
        direction TB
        subgraph HEAP[HEAP MEMORY -XMS XMX]
            subgraph YG[YOUNG GENERATION -X MN]
                EDEN[EDEN SPACE S EQUALS 8]
                S1[SURVIVOR 0 S EQUALS 1]
                S2[SURVIVOR 1 S EQUALS 1]
            end
            OG[OLD GENERATION TENURED SPACE]
        end
        subgraph NH[NON HEAP MEMORY]
            META[METASPACE NATIVE MEMORY]
            CC[CODE CACHE JIT COMPILED]
            CCS[COMPRESSED CLASS SPACE]
        end
        subgraph TS[THREAD STACKS -X SS EACH]
            T1[THREAD 1 STACK 512K]
            TN[THREAD N STACK 512K]
        end
    end
    EDEN -->|MINOR GC| S1
    S1 -->|AGE THRESHOLD| OG
    S1 -->|AGE THRESHOLD| S2
    S2 -->|AGE THRESHOLD| OG
    OG -->|MAJOR GC| HEAP
```

**Reading the diagram:** Objects are allocated in **Eden**. Surviving objects are copied between `Survivor 0` and `Survivor 1` (one is always empty). After surviving a configurable number of minor GCs (default **15**, the *tenuring threshold*), objects are *promoted* to the **Old Generation**. **Metaspace** holds class metadata in *native* memory (no longer in the heap since Java 8). Each thread has its own **stack** sized by `-Xss`.

---

### 4.3 Garbage Collector Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G0[CHOOSE GARBAGE COLLECTOR] --> G1{PRIMARY GOAL}
    G1 -->|THROUGHPUT| G2[USE PARALLEL GC -XX PLUS USE PARALLEL GC]
    G1 -->|LOW LATENCY| G3[USE G1 GC -XX PLUS USE G1 GC]
    G1 -->|VERY LARGE HEAP LESS THAN 1MS PAUSE| G4[USE ZGC -XX PLUS USE ZGC]
    G1 -->|HIGHLY RESPONSIVE CMS REPLACEMENT| G5[USE SHENANDOAH]
    G2 --> G6[SUITABLE FOR BATCH JOBS]
    G3 --> G7[SUITABLE FOR WEB SERVERS]
    G4 --> G8[SUITABLE FOR TRADING PLATFORMS]
    G5 --> G9[SUITABLE FOR INTERACTIVE APPLICATIONS]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The collector choice is **goal-driven**, not arbitrary. G1 GC is the **default** since Java 9 and is appropriate for the vast majority of server workloads. ZGC and Shenandoah are *concurrent, low-pause* collectors for sub-millisecond SLA targets.

---

### 4.4 Sequence of Events During an Uncaught Exception (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T as Worker Thread
    participant JVM as JVM Runtime
    participant SE as System.err
    participant LOG as Application Logger

    T->>JVM: throw new NullPointerException
    JVM->>JVM: walk current call stack
    JVM->>JVM: collect stack frames line numbers
    JVM->>SE: write Exception in thread
    JVM->>LOG: invoke Thread.UncaughtExceptionHandler
    Note over T: thread terminates
    JVM->>JVM: check for ShutdownHandler
    JVM->>JVM: continue serving other threads
```

**Reading the diagram:** The JVM itself (not the application) walks the stack and formats the trace. The application can intercept via `Thread.setDefaultUncaughtExceptionHandler()`. The failing thread terminates; **other threads continue**, so a single bad request does not crash the server (this is why Java is suited to long-running services).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1.** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]`

**Define a Java stack trace. List its major components and explain how a developer should interpret it to identify the root cause of a runtime exception.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **stack trace** is a textual report automatically generated by the JVM when an exception propagates uncaught to the top of a thread's execution stack. It contains:
1. The **thread name** in which the exception occurred (e.g. `main`, `http-nio-8080-exec-3`).
2. The **fully-qualified exception class name** (e.g. `java.lang.NullPointerException`).
3. The **error message** provided to the exception constructor (e.g. *"destination is null"*).
4. The **call stack** — a list of method invocations from the entry point down to the point of failure, each with source file and line number.

**Interpretation for root cause analysis:** The trace should be read **top-down**. The first listed frame is the **deepest** call — the *symptom location* where the exception was actually thrown. The last frame is the *entry point*. If a "**Caused by:**" chain is present, the **innermost** `Caused by` is the *true root cause*; outer frames are symptoms of that root cause. **[3 marks — full credit]**

---

**Question A2.** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]`

**Differentiate between a heap dump, a thread dump, and a GC log. State one diagnostic use case for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Artifact | Captures | Trigger / Tool | Diagnostic Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Heap dump** | All reachable objects in heap memory at a moment | `-XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError`, `jmap -dump`, `jcmd GC.heap_dump` | Diagnose `OutOfMemoryError` and memory leaks using Eclipse MAT (Leak Suspects, Dominator Tree). |
| **Thread dump** | State, stack, and lock ownership of every thread | `jstack <PID>`, `kill -3 <PID>`, `jcmd Thread.print` | Detect deadlocks (auto-printed by `jstack`), thread starvation, and blocked / hung requests. |
| **GC log** | Every garbage collection event with timestamps, regions, and pauses | `-Xlog:gc*` (Java 9+), `-Xloggc:<file>` (legacy) | Tune heap sizing, GC algorithm, and pause-time SLAs; compute throughput as $T_{app}/(T_{app}+T_{GC})$. |

**[1 mark per row — 3 marks total]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the **different types of Java error information** available to a developer for diagnosing production issues. For each type, mention the trigger, the file or stream it appears in, and a typical diagnostic use case. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Consider the following Java stack trace captured from a deployed servlet container. Identify the **thread**, the **symptom location**, the **root cause**, and propose **two specific code-level fixes**. **[7 marks]**

```
ERROR [http-nio-8080-exec-7] OrderService - Failed to place order
java.lang.NullPointerException
    at com.ktu.shop.OrderService.placeOrder(OrderService.java:54)
    at com.ktu.shop.OrderController.create(OrderController.java:31)
    at java.base/java.lang.Thread.run(Thread.java:840)
Caused by: java.lang.IllegalArgumentException: amount cannot be negative
    at com.ktu.shop.PricingService.calculate(PricingService.java:22)
    at com.ktu.shop.OrderService.placeOrder(OrderService.java:48)
    ... 3 more
```

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

The major categories of Java error information are:

1. **Stack Traces (2 marks)** — Emitted to `System.err` (or the application logger via `printStackTrace()`) whenever an exception propagates uncaught. Components: thread name, exception class, message, and call stack. Trigger: any `Throwable` reaching the thread's top. Use case: identifying the line of code that threw an exception.

2. **Heap Dumps (2 marks)** — Serialized binary snapshot of all objects reachable from GC roots. Trigger: `-XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError`, manual `jmap -dump`, or `jcmd <PID> GC.heap_dump`. Use case: diagnosing `OutOfMemoryError`, identifying memory leaks via Eclipse MAT, computing *retained heap* and *dominator tree*.

3. **Thread Dumps (1 mark)** — Snapshot of every thread's state, call stack, and owned monitors. Trigger: `jstack`, `jcmd Thread.print`, `kill -3`. Use case: deadlock detection, blocked thread analysis, hung-request investigation.

4. **GC Logs (1 mark)** — Chronological record of every GC event with pauses and region statistics. Trigger: `-Xlog:gc*`. Use case: tuning heap size, choosing GC algorithm, computing throughput.

5. **JVM Crash Logs (1 mark)** — `hs_err_pid<PID>.log` produced on native crash (SIGSEGV, JIT bug, JNI fault). Contains thread state, register dump, and instructions to file a bug. Use case: filing bugs with Oracle/OpenJDK when JVM itself crashes.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify the thread (1 mark).** The thread is `http-nio-8080-exec-7`, a Tomcat HTTP worker thread. This is a user request handler — the bug is on the live request path.

**Step 2 — Identify the symptom location (2 marks).** The first frame, `OrderService.placeOrder(OrderService.java:54)`, is the symptom. This is where the JVM actually threw the `NullPointerException` — likely a call on a `null` reference such as `order.getItems().get(0)`. The student should state that the `NullPointerException` is the *outer* (symptom) exception.

**Step 3 — Identify the root cause (2 marks).** The "**Caused by:**" line shows the *real* root cause: `java.lang.IllegalArgumentException: amount cannot be negative`, thrown at `PricingService.calculate(PricingService.java:22)`. This is the **innermost** exception, hence the root cause. The NPE in `OrderService` is the **secondary** failure — the order was constructed with a negative amount, the pricing service rejected it, and a downstream NPE occurred because some object was not properly initialized after the rejection.

**Step 4 — Two specific code-level fixes (2 marks):**
- **Fix 1 (defensive in `OrderService.placeOrder`):** Validate the `Order` object's state at line 48 (immediately after calling `PricingService`): if `totalAmount <= 0`, throw a checked `InvalidOrderException` rather than continuing. This prevents the NPE downstream.
- **Fix 2 (input validation at controller boundary):** In `OrderController.create` at line 31, validate the request DTO *before* invoking the service: reject negative amounts with an HTTP 400 response. This implements "fail fast" at the trust boundary and prevents invalid state from ever reaching `PricingService`.

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Internal Choice]`

**(a)** Discuss the **major memory areas** of the JVM runtime data areas. Explain the **Young Generation** and **Old Generation** in detail, including the role of Eden, Survivor spaces, and the *tenuring threshold*. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Explain the **important JVM command-line flags** for heap and metaspace sizing, including `-Xms`, `-Xmx`, `-Xmn`, `-XX:NewRatio`, `-XX:SurvivorRatio`, and `-XX:MaxMetaspaceSize`. Derive the relationship between `NewRatio` and the Young Generation size, and explain why production servers should set `-Xms = -Xmx`. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

The JVM runtime data areas are divided into **heap** and **non-heap** regions, plus per-thread areas:

1. **Heap (3 marks)** — Shared across all threads. Divided into:
   - **Young Generation** (1.5 marks) — Where new objects are allocated. Sub-divided into:
     - **Eden Space** — Where `new` objects are born. Minor GCs reclaim unreachable objects here.
     - **Two Survivor Spaces (S0, S1)** — One is always empty. Surviving objects are copied between them after each minor GC (the *copying* or *mark-compact* algorithm).
     - Objects that survive **15 minor GCs** (default *tenuring threshold*, controlled by `-XX:MaxTenuringThreshold`) are *promoted* to the Old Generation.
   - **Old Generation (1.5 marks)** — Long-lived objects. Collected by *major* (or *full*) GCs, which are more expensive. Major GCs trigger *Stop-The-World* pauses in most collectors.

2. **Non-Heap (2 marks):**
   - **Metaspace** (since Java 8) — Class metadata, method data, constant pool. Lives in *native* memory; auto-grows by default (but should be capped in production via `-XX:MaxMetaspaceSize`).
   - **Code Cache** — JIT-compiled native code.
   - **Compressed Class Space** — When class pointers are compressed (default for heaps < 32 GB).

3. **Per-Thread (1 mark):**
   - **JVM Stack** — One per thread, default **512 KB** on 64-bit Linux, configured by `-Xss`. Holds frames for active method invocations.
   - **PC Register** and **Native Method Stack**.

4. **Off-Heap (1 mark):** Direct memory (`ByteBuffer.allocateDirect`), used by NIO and Netty; not subject to GC the same way; release requires explicit `Cleaner`.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**1. Important JVM flags (4 marks):**

| Flag | Purpose | Default |
|---|---|---|
| `-Xms<size>` | Initial heap size | 1/64 RAM (server) |
| `-Xmx<size>` | Maximum heap size | 1/4 RAM (server) |
| `-Xmn<size>` | Young Generation size (overrides NewRatio) | unset |
| `-XX:NewRatio=N` | Ratio of Old:Young | 2 |
| `-XX:SurvivorRatio=N` | Ratio of Eden:OneSurvivor | 8 |
| `-XX:MaxMetaspaceSize=<size>` | Cap on Metaspace growth | unlimited (native) |

**2. Derivation of the NewRatio–Young relationship (2 marks):**

By definition, the heap is partitioned as $X_{mx} = Y + O$, where $Y$ is the Young Generation size and $O$ is the Old Generation size. `NewRatio` is defined as $N = O / Y$, so $O = N \cdot Y$. Substituting:

$$X_{mx} = Y + N \cdot Y = Y \cdot (N + 1)$$

Therefore:

$$Y = \frac{X_{mx}}{N + 1}$$

For example, with $X_{mx} = 8$ GB and $N = 2$:

$$Y = \frac{8\,\text{GB}}{3} \approx 2.67\,\text{GB}, \quad O = N \cdot Y = 5.33\,\text{GB}$$

**3. Why `-Xms = -Xmx` in production (1 mark):** Heap resizing is a *stop-the-world* operation requiring a full GC and re-allocation of all live objects. By setting them equal, the JVM requests the full heap in a single mmap at startup, avoiding all resizing pauses. Trade-off: longer startup, loss of elasticity, but **stable RSS and predictable latency** — critical for production.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes on this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "symptom location" with "root cause"** — In a "Caused by" chain, the *innermost* (last) "Caused by" is the root cause. The first frame is *where the exception was thrown*, not *why it happened*. **[−2 marks typical]**
>
> 2. **Saying "Permanent Generation" in Java 8+ answers** — PermGen was *removed* in Java 8. Any reference to `PermGen`, `-XX:PermSize`, or `-XX:MaxPermSize` in modern Java answers is an automatic **out-of-date knowledge** penalty. Use **Metaspace**. **[−1 mark]**
>
> 3. **Confusing `-Xmn` and `-XX:NewRatio`** — `-Xmn<size>` is an *absolute* size that *overrides* `NewRatio`. If both are set, `-Xmn` wins. Students often write both in an answer, which contradicts itself. **[−1 mark]**
>
> 4. **Confusing Shallow Heap and Retained Heap in heap dump analysis** — *Shallow* is the object's own footprint. *Retained* is what would be freed by GC if the object were removed. Saying "the object is leaking 4 GB" when "4 GB is the retained heap of a *collection* it dominates" loses a mark.
>
> 5. **Recommending "increase `-Xmx`" as the universal fix for `OutOfMemoryError`** — This is *never* the first recommendation. The first step is to capture a heap dump and identify the *leak suspect*. Increasing `-Xmx` only delays the OOM. **[−1 mark if given as the only fix]**
>
> 6. **Forgetting to set `-XX:MaxMetaspaceSize`** — Unbounded Metaspace will eventually consume all native memory and crash with a native OOM (e.g. `Cannot allocate memory`). Production JVMs must cap metaspace.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read Twice Before the Exam**

- **Stack trace** = thread + exception class + message + frame list. Read **top-down**. The first frame is the *symptom*; the innermost "Caused by" is the *root cause*.
- **Heap dump** = binary snapshot of heap. Trigger with `-XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError`. Analyze in **Eclipse MAT** using Leak Suspects and Dominator Tree.
- **Thread dump** = every thread's stack. Trigger with `jstack` or `kill -3`. `jstack` auto-detects **deadlocks** and prints the cycle.
- **GC log** = every GC event. Use `-Xlog:gc*` (Java 9+). Key numbers: pause time, promotion rate, GC overhead.
- **JVM crash log** = `hs_err_pid<PID>.log`. File a bug with Oracle/OpenJDK; do not ignore.
- **Heap** = Young (Eden + 2 Survivors) + Old. **Non-heap** = Metaspace + Code Cache. **Per-thread** = Stack (`-Xss`).
- **`-Xms = -Xmx`** in production to eliminate resizing pauses. Trade-off: longer startup.
- **`-Xmn`** (absolute) overrides **`-XX:NewRatio`** (relative). Don't set both.
- **Young Gen size formula:** $Y = X_{mx} / (N + 1)$ where $N = $ `NewRatio`.
- **Eden size formula:** $E = Y \cdot S / (S + 2)$ where $S = $ `SurvivorRatio`. Each Survivor is $Y / (S + 2)$.
- **Permanent Generation** was removed in **Java 8**; use **Metaspace** with `-XX:MaxMetaspaceSize`.
- **Default GC since Java 9:** **G1 GC** (`-XX:+UseG1GC`). For sub-millisecond pause SLA, use **ZGC** or **Shenandoah**.
- **G1 pause target:** `-XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=100` (default 200 ms).
- **Throughput** = $T_{app} / (T_{app} + T_{GC})$. Target $\ge 95\%$.
- **Lock-ordering deadlock fix:** always acquire locks in a *globally consistent order* (e.g. by ID).
- **Tuning discipline:** change *one* flag at a time, measure, compare to baseline. Never tune without a GC log.
- **Always enable** `-XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError -XX:HeapDumpPath=/var/log/heapdumps/` and `-Xlog:gc*:file=gc.log` in production.
- **Three artifacts solve 80% of production Java issues:** stack trace + heap dump + GC log.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Collections and Data Structures: Lists, Sets, and Maps
ArrayList
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT611 - OBJECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING IN JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** ArrayList

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **`ArrayList`** in Java is a **resizable**, **index-based** implementation of the `java.util.List` interface, backed internally by a **dynamic array** (`Object[] elementData`). It is part of the **Java Collections Framework (JCF)** and belongs to the package `java.util`.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus, `ArrayList` is classified under the **Collection Framework → List interface hierarchy** and is widely used when **frequent read operations (random access)** are required, while insertions/deletions in the middle are comparatively slower.

### Key Characteristics (Board-Exam Critical)
- **Dynamic Resizing**: Grows automatically when capacity is exceeded.
- **Allows Duplicates**: Maintains insertion order with duplicate elements permitted.
- **Null-Friendly**: Permits multiple `null` values.
- **Non-Synchronized**: Not thread-safe by default (unlike `Vector`).
- **Generic-Supported**: Type-safe via **Generics** (e.g., `ArrayList<String>`).
- **Initial Capacity (JDK 1.7+)**: **Empty array** (`{}`) is used initially. Array is created on the first `add()` call.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy Snapshot** (KTU Favourite):
> `Iterable` → `Collection` → `List` → `ArrayList` (implements `Serializable`, `Cloneable`, `RandomAccess`).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy 🧠

Imagine a **train with removable coaches**:
- Initially, you book just **1 coach** (capacity = 1, size = 0).
- As passengers arrive, they occupy seats inside (size grows).
- When a coach is **full**, the railway doesn't refuse passengers — instead, it **attaches a new, larger coach** and transfers everyone seamlessly.
- The new coach is **~1.5× the size** of the old one (in Java 8+).
- You can board at any **coach number** (index), but inserting someone in the **middle** requires shifting all passengers to the right (costly).

This train is the **`ArrayList`** — an auto-expanding array of objects!

## 1.3 When to Use ArrayList? (KTU Board Tip)

| Scenario | Recommended? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Random access via index (`get(i)`) | ✅ Yes | **O(1)** time complexity |
| Frequent read, rare modification | ✅ Yes | Best fit |
| Frequent insertions/deletions in the middle | ❌ No | **O(n)** shifting overhead |
| Multithreaded environment | ❌ No | Use `Collections.synchronizedList()` or `CopyOnWriteArrayList` |
| Fixed-size primitives performance | ❌ No | Use plain arrays |

## 1.4 Declaration Syntax

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;

// Non-generic (raw type) — KTU 2024 deprecates this
ArrayList list = new ArrayList();

// Generic (Type-safe) — PREFERRED
ArrayList<String> names = new ArrayList<String>();

// Diamond operator (JDK 7+) — MOST COMMON
ArrayList<Integer> marks = new ArrayList<>();
```

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture — The Dynamic Array

`ArrayList` internally maintains a **transient `Object[] elementData`** and an **`int size`** field tracking the number of valid elements.

```java
// Simplified internal structure (conceptual)
transient Object[] elementData;  // The actual array
private int size;                 // Logical count of elements
```

### Default Capacity Rules (KTU Viva Hot Topic 🔥)

| JDK Version | Initial Capacity on Creation | First `add()` Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| JDK 1.6 and earlier | **10** elements | Array pre-allocated |
| **JDK 1.7+ (and 2024 syllabus)** | **0** (empty array `{}`) | Array of capacity **10** lazily allocated on first `add()` |
| **JDK 8+** | Empty array | Capacity 10 created on first `add()` |

## 2.2 The Growth Formula (HIGH-YIELD ⭐)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Whenever the array is **full** and a new element is to be added, Java creates a **new larger array** and copies the old contents into it using `Arrays.copyOf()`.

### Capacity Growth Strategy

| JDK Version | Growth Formula | New Capacity Formula |
|---|---|---|
| JDK 1.6 and earlier | `(oldCapacity * 3/2) + 1` | $C_{new} = \lfloor 1.5 \cdot C_{old} \rfloor + 1$ |
| **JDK 1.7+ / 8+ / 2024 Default** | `oldCapacity + (oldCapacity >> 1)` | $C_{new} = C_{old} + \lfloor C_{old} / 2 \rfloor$ |

**Plain English growth** (Java 8+): *"New capacity is 1.5 times the old capacity"* (ignoring integer truncation).

### Example Growth Sequence
Starting with capacity 10, on subsequent overflows:
- 10 → 15 → 22 → 33 → 49 → 73 → 109 → 163 ...

## 2.3 Time Complexity Cheat Sheet (Board Exam Favourite)

> [!NOTE]
> |Operation|Method|Time Complexity|Why|
> |---|---|---|---|
> | Random Access | `get(int index)` | **O(1)** | Direct index into array |
> | Update by Index | `set(int index, E e)` | **O(1)** | Direct assignment |
> | Add at End | `add(E e)` | **O(1)** amortized | Occasional resize O(n) |
> | Add at Index | `add(int i, E e)` | **O(n)** | Shifts elements right |
> | Remove by Index | `remove(int i)` | **O(n)** | Shifts elements left |
> | Remove by Object | `remove(Object o)` | **O(n)** | Linear search + shift |
> | Search | `contains(Object o)` | **O(n)** | Linear traversal |
> | Size | `size()` | **O(1)** | Returns `int` field |

## 2.4 Core Methods — KTU Reference Table

| Method | Signature | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `add(E e)` | `boolean` | Appends element at end |
| `add(int i, E e)` | `void` | Inserts at specified index |
| `addAll(Collection c)` | `boolean` | Appends all elements |
| `get(int i)` | `E` | Returns element at index |
| `set(int i, E e)` | `E` | Replaces element at index |
| `remove(int i)` | `E` | Removes by index |
| `remove(Object o)` | `boolean` | Removes first occurrence |
| `size()` | `int` | Returns number of elements |
| `isEmpty()` | `boolean` | Checks if empty |
| `contains(Object o)` | `boolean` | Checks if element exists |
| `indexOf(Object o)` | `int` | Returns first index or -1 |
| `lastIndexOf(Object o)` | `int` | Returns last index or -1 |
| `clear()` | `void` | Removes all elements |
| `toArray()` | `Object[]` | Converts to array |
| `iterator()` | `Iterator<E>` | Returns iterator |
| `forEach(Consumer)` | `void` | Lambda-based traversal (Java 8+) |

## 2.5 Iterating an ArrayList — Four Ways (KTU PYQ Favourite ⭐)

1. **For loop (index-based)**
2. **Enhanced for-each loop**
3. **`Iterator<E>` interface**
4. **Lambda + `forEach()` (Java 8+)**

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Data Buffers**: Storing variable-length sensor data in IoT systems.
- **UI Component Lists**: Dynamic lists in Android (`ArrayAdapter`).
- **Database Result Mapping**: Caching rows of `ResultSet`.
- **Undo/Redo Stacks**: As the underlying container for `Stack` operations.
- **Sorting Pipelines**: `Collections.sort(arrayList)` works seamlessly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Walkthroughs

## 3.1 Derivation: Why 1.5× Growth is Better than 2× Growth

In JDK 7+, the growth factor is **1.5×** instead of **2×** (which was the case in `Vector`). Here's the **amortized cost analysis** (a board-favourite derivation):

### Step 1: Define Amortized Cost
When we double the array (**2× growth**), a single expensive resize is rare, but the **wasted memory** is high.

### Step 2: Calculate Memory Wasted
With **2× growth**: Total memory used over $n$ insertions is approximately $2n$ (because each block is half-filled on average).
With **1.5× growth**: Total memory used is approximately $n \cdot (1 + 1/2 + 1/4 + \ldots)$ → converges to $2n$ but with **better distribution** across resizes.

### Step 3: Amortized Time (Conclusively)
The **amortized cost of `add()` remains O(1)** regardless of growth factor, because:

$$
T_{amortized} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{\text{Total cost of } n \text{ insertions}}{n}
$$

$$
T_{amortized} = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{O(n) + O(\text{fewer resizes})}{n} = O(1)
$$

### Step 4: The 1.5× Advantage
1.5× growth allows **reuse of old array memory** during garbage collection — the JVM can reclaim the previous array without forcing a full GC, because its size differs from the new array (unlike 2× growth where some sizes repeat).

## 3.2 Worked Example: Capacity Growth Walkthrough

Suppose we keep adding elements and track capacity after each resize:

$$
C_0 = 0 \text{ (initial empty array, JDK 8+)}
$$

$$
C_1 = 10 \text{ (allocated on first add)}
$$

$$
C_2 = C_1 + \left\lfloor \frac{C_1}{2} \right\rfloor = 10 + 5 = 15
$$

$$
C_3 = 15 + \left\lfloor \frac{15}{2} \right\rfloor = 15 + 7 = 22
$$

$$
C_4 = 22 + \left\lfloor \frac{22}{2} \right\rfloor = 22 + 11 = 33
$$

### General Formula

$$
C_{n+1} = C_n + \left\lfloor \frac{C_n}{2} \right\rfloor = \left\lceil 1.5 \cdot C_n \right\rceil
$$

## 3.3 Algorithm: `add(E e)` (Appending at End)

```
Step 1: ensureCapacityInternal(size + 1)
        → If size + 1 > elementData.length, grow() the array
Step 2: elementData[size++] = e
Step 3: return true
```

**Amortized cost**: O(1) — proven via the aggregate method above.

## 3.4 Algorithm: `add(int index, E element)` (Insertion in Middle)

```
Step 1: rangeCheckForAdd(index)         → throws IndexOutOfBoundsException
Step 2: ensureCapacityInternal(size + 1)
Step 3: System.arraycopy(elementData, index, elementData, index+1, size-index)
        → shifts all elements from index onwards, one position right
Step 4: elementData[index] = element
Step 5: size++
```

**Worst-case cost**: O(n) — when inserting at index 0, all `n` elements must shift.

## 3.5 Algorithm: `remove(int index)` (Deletion by Index)

```
Step 1: rangeCheck(index)
Step 2: modCount++                       → used by Iterator for fail-fast
Step 3: oldValue = elementData[index]
Step 4: int numMoved = size - index - 1
        If numMoved > 0:
            System.arraycopy(elementData, index+1, elementData, index, numMoved)
            → shifts elements from index+1 onwards, one position left
Step 5: elementData[--size] = null       → allows GC of old reference
Step 6: return oldValue
```

**Worst-case cost**: O(n) — same reasoning as middle insertion.

## 3.6 Sample Program — Full Demonstration (KTU Practise Favourite)

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.Iterator;

public class ArrayListDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Creating a generic ArrayList of Strings
        ArrayList<String> fruits = new ArrayList<>();

        // 1. Adding elements
        fruits.add("Apple");
        fruits.add("Banana");
        fruits.add("Cherry");
        fruits.add("Date");
        System.out.println("After adds: " + fruits);

        // 2. Inserting at specific index
        fruits.add(1, "Blueberry");
        System.out.println("After insert at 1: " + fruits);

        // 3. Accessing elements
        System.out.println("Element at index 2: " + fruits.get(2));

        // 4. Updating an element
        fruits.set(0, "Avocado");
        System.out.println("After set(0): " + fruits);

        // 5. Removing by index
        fruits.remove(2);
        System.out.println("After remove(2): " + fruits);

        // 6. Removing by object
        fruits.remove("Date");
        System.out.println("After remove Date: " + fruits);

        // 7. Searching
        System.out.println("Contains Cherry? " + fruits.contains("Cherry"));
        System.out.println("Index of Blueberry: " + fruits.indexOf("Blueberry"));

        // 8. Size and empty check
        System.out.println("Size: " + fruits.size());
        System.out.println("Is empty? " + fruits.isEmpty());

        // 9. Iteration - For loop
        System.out.print("For loop: ");
        for (int i = 0; i < fruits.size(); i++) {
            System.out.print(fruits.get(i) + " ");
        }
        System.out.println();

        // 10. Iteration - Enhanced for
        System.out.print("For-each: ");
        for (String f : fruits) {
            System.out.print(f + " ");
        }
        System.out.println();

        // 11. Iteration - Iterator
        System.out.print("Iterator: ");
        Iterator<String> it = fruits.iterator();
        while (it.hasNext()) {
            System.out.print(it.next() + " ");
        }
        System.out.println();

        // 12. Iteration - Lambda forEach (Java 8+)
        System.out.print("Lambda: ");
        fruits.forEach(f -> System.out.print(f + " "));
        System.out.println();
    }
}
```

### Expected Output

```
After adds: [Apple, Banana, Cherry, Date]
After insert at 1: [Apple, Blueberry, Banana, Cherry, Date]
Element at index 2: Banana
After set(0): [Avocado, Blueberry, Banana, Cherry, Date]
After remove(2): [Avocado, Blueberry, Cherry, Date]
After remove Date: [Avocado, Blueberry, Cherry]
Contains Cherry? true
Index of Blueberry: 1
Size: 3
Is empty? false
For loop: Avocado Blueberry Cherry 
For-each: Avocado Blueberry Cherry 
Iterator: Avocado Blueberry Cherry 
Lambda: Avocado Blueberry Cherry 
```

## 3.7 Example: ArrayList with User-Defined Class (Generic)

```java
class Student {
    String name;
    int rollNo;
    Student(String name, int rollNo) {
        this.name = name;
        this.rollNo = rollNo;
    }
    @Override
    public String toString() {
        return rollNo + " - " + name;
    }
}

public class StudentList {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        ArrayList<Student> students = new ArrayList<>();
        students.add(new Student("Alice", 101));
        students.add(new Student("Bob", 102));
        students.add(new Student("Charlie", 103));

        for (Student s : students) {
            System.out.println(s);
        }
    }
}
```

## 3.8 Example: `addAll()`, `removeAll()`, `retainAll()`

```java
ArrayList<Integer> list1 = new ArrayList<>();
list1.add(1); list1.add(2); list1.add(3);

ArrayList<Integer> list2 = new ArrayList<>();
list2.add(3); list2.add(4); list2.add(5);

// Union
ArrayList<Integer> union = new ArrayList<>(list1);
union.addAll(list2);
// Result: [1, 2, 3, 3, 4, 5]

// Intersection
ArrayList<Integer> intersection = new ArrayList<>(list1);
intersection.retainAll(list2);
// Result: [3]

// Difference (list1 - list2)
ArrayList<Integer> difference = new ArrayList<>(list1);
difference.removeAll(list2);
// Result: [1, 2]
```
