# 📓 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between `ArrayList` and `Vector` in Java.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | `ArrayList` | `Vector` |
|---|---|---|
| Thread Safety | **Not synchronized** (not thread-safe) | **Synchronized** (thread-safe) |
| Performance | Faster (no synchronization overhead) | Slower due to lock overhead |
| Growth Rate | **1.5×** (JDK 7+) of old capacity | **2×** of old capacity |
| Legacy Status | Part of modern Collections Framework | **Legacy class** (since JDK 1.0) |
| Iterator Type | **Fail-fast iterator** | **Fail-fast iterator** (since JDK 1.2) |
| Recommended Use | Single-threaded applications | Multi-threaded legacy code |

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any four methods of `ArrayList` class along with their purpose.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **`add(E e)`** — Appends the specified element to the end of the list. Returns `true`.
2. **`get(int index)`** — Returns the element at the specified position in the list. **O(1)** operation.
3. **`set(int index, E element)`** — Replaces the element at the specified position with the new element. Returns the old element.
4. **`remove(int index)`** — Removes the element at the specified position. Shifts subsequent elements to the left. Returns the removed element.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**: Many students confuse **`remove(int index)`** with **`remove(Object o)`** — these are two different overloaded methods. The compiler resolves the call based on the **argument type**. Passing an `Integer` literal (e.g., `remove(5)`) calls the **index version** and may cause `IndexOutOfBoundsException` or unintended deletion. To remove by value, **autobox carefully**: `remove(Integer.valueOf(5))`.

---

## Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **internal working of `ArrayList`** in Java. Discuss how the **dynamic resizing** mechanism works, including the growth formula used in JDK 8+ and the role of `Arrays.copyOf()`. Illustrate with a step-by-step example of capacity growth starting from an initial `add()`.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a complete Java program that demonstrates the use of `ArrayList<String>` to perform the following operations:
1. Create an `ArrayList` and add at least 5 student names.
2. Insert a new name at the 2nd position.
3. Replace the last element with a new name.
4. Remove a name by its value.
5. Display all names using both `for` loop and `Iterator`.

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Internal Working of ArrayList**

`ArrayList` is backed by an internal **`Object[] elementData`** array. When we create `new ArrayList<>()` in JDK 8+, the array is **initially empty** (capacity = 0). The actual array is allocated **lazily** on the first call to `add()`.

**Step 1: First `add()` Call**
- A new `Object[]` of capacity **10** is created.
- The element is placed at `elementData[0]` and `size` is incremented to 1.

**Step 2: Subsequent `add()` Calls (Capacity Check)**
- Before every addition, `ensureCapacityInternal(size + 1)` is invoked.
- If `size + 1 > elementData.length`, the array must **grow**.

**Step 3: Growth Formula (JDK 8+)**

$$
C_{new} = C_{old} + \left\lfloor \frac{C_{old}}{2} \right\rfloor
$$

Equivalently, this is `oldCapacity + (oldCapacity >> 1)`, which means the new array is **1.5× the old array size**.

**Step 4: Copying via `Arrays.copyOf()`**
- A new array of size `C_new` is allocated.
- The old elements are copied using `Arrays.copyOf(elementData, newCapacity)`.
- The new element is then placed at the appropriate index.
- The old array becomes eligible for **garbage collection**.

**Capacity Growth Walkthrough Example** (assuming 12 `add()` calls):
- After 1st add: capacity = 10, size = 1
- After 10th add: capacity = 10, size = 10
- On 11th add: capacity = 10 + 5 = **15**, size = 11
- On 16th add: capacity = 15 + 7 = **22**, size = 16
- On 23rd add: capacity = 22 + 11 = **33**, size = 23

The amortized cost of `add()` remains **O(1)** despite the O(n) resizing because resizes occur exponentially less frequently.

#### Model Solution for (b):

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.Iterator;

public class StudentArrayListDemo {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // 1. Create ArrayList and add 5 student names
        ArrayList<String> students = new ArrayList<>();
        students.add("Anu");
        students.add("Balu");
        students.add("Chitra");
        students.add("Deepak");
        students.add("Esha");
        System.out.println("Initial list: " + students);

        // 2. Insert a new name at 2nd position (index 1)
        students.add(1, "Farah");
        System.out.println("After inserting at index 1: " + students);

        // 3. Replace last element with a new name
        students.set(students.size() - 1, "Zara");
        System.out.println("After replacing last element: " + students);

        // 4. Remove a name by its value
        students.remove("Chitra");
        System.out.println("After removing Chitra: " + students);

        // 5a. Display using for loop
        System.out.print("Using for loop: ");
        for (int i = 0; i < students.size(); i++) {
            System.out.print(students.get(i) + " ");
        }
        System.out.println();

        // 5b. Display using Iterator
        System.out.print("Using Iterator: ");
        Iterator<String> it = students.iterator();
        while (it.hasNext()) {
            System.out.print(it.next() + " ");
        }
        System.out.println();
    }
}
```

**Output:**

```
Initial list: [Anu, Balu, Chitra, Deepak, Esha]
After inserting at index 1: [Anu, Farah, Balu, Chitra, Deepak, Esha]
After replacing last element: [Anu, Farah, Balu, Chitra, Deepak, Zara]
After removing Chitra: [Anu, Farah, Balu, Deepak, Zara]
Using for loop: Anu Farah Balu Deepak Zara 
Using Iterator: Anu Farah Balu Deepak Zara 
```

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Compare `ArrayList` and `LinkedList` in terms of **internal data structure**, **time complexity** of major operations, **memory usage**, and **best-use scenarios**. Which is preferable for read-heavy workloads and why?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Write a Java program using `ArrayList<Integer>` that:
1. Reads `n` integers from the user into an `ArrayList`.
2. Removes all even numbers from the list.
3. Sorts the remaining numbers in descending order.
4. Prints the final list using an enhanced for-each loop.

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Aspect | `ArrayList` | `LinkedList` |
|---|---|---|
| **Internal Structure** | Dynamic array (`Object[]`) | Doubly-linked list of `Node` objects |
| **`get(i)` / `set(i)`** | **O(1)** | **O(n)** — must traverse from head/tail |
| **`add(E e)` at end** | **O(1)** amortized | **O(1)** |
| **`add(int i, E)` middle** | **O(n)** — shifts elements | **O(n)** — must traverse + constant insert |
| **`remove(int i)` middle** | **O(n)** — shifts elements | **O(n)** — traverse |
| **Memory Overhead** | Low (just array) | **High** (each node stores prev, next, data pointers) |
| **Implements** | `RandomAccess` marker | `Deque` interface too |
| **Cache Performance** | **Better** (contiguous memory) | Worse (scattered nodes) |
| **Best For** | **Read-heavy**, random access | **Frequent insertions/deletions** at head/middle |

**Conclusion**: For read-heavy workloads (frequent `get(i)` operations), **`ArrayList` is preferable** because of its **O(1) random access** capability backed by contiguous memory, which also benefits from CPU cache locality.

#### Model Solution for (b):

```java
import java.util.ArrayList;
import java.util.Collections;
import java.util.Iterator;
import java.util.Scanner;

public class ArrayListOperations {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        Scanner sc = new Scanner(System.in);
        ArrayList<Integer> numbers = new ArrayList<>();

        // 1. Read n integers
        System.out.print("Enter the number of integers: ");
        int n = sc.nextInt();
        System.out.println("Enter " + n + " integers:");
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
            numbers.add(sc.nextInt());
        }
        System.out.println("Original list: " + numbers);

        // 2. Remove all even numbers using Iterator (safe removal)
        Iterator<Integer> it = numbers.iterator();
        while (it.hasNext()) {
            if (it.next() % 2 == 0) {
                it.remove();
            }
        }
        System.out.println("After removing evens: " + numbers);

        // 3. Sort in descending order
        Collections.sort(numbers, Collections.reverseOrder());
        System.out.println("Sorted descending: " + numbers);

        // 4. Print using enhanced for-each
        System.out.print("Final list: ");
        for (Integer num : numbers) {
            System.out.print(num + " ");
        }
        System.out.println();

        sc.close();
    }
}
```

**Sample Run:**

```
Enter the number of integers: 6
Enter 6 integers:
45 22 17 8 33 60
Original list: [45, 22, 17, 8, 33, 60]
After removing evens: [45, 17, 33]
Sorted descending: [45, 33, 17]
Final list: 45 33 17 
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout**:
> 1. **Never use `for-each` loop for removal** — it throws `ConcurrentModificationException`. Always use **`Iterator.remove()`** for safe deletion during traversal.
> 2. **Confusing `remove(int)` vs `remove(Integer)`** — when using an `Integer` autoboxed value, Java may still call the **index version** due to method resolution. To force value-based removal: `list.remove(Integer.valueOf(x))`.
> 3. **Forgetting `<Integer>` generics** — the raw type allows heterogeneous data, defeating type safety. Always use **diamond/generic syntax**.
> 4. **Modifying list during indexed `for` loop** — skip indices shift during removal, causing elements to be skipped. Always prefer the iterator pattern.

---

## 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ `ArrayList` implements `List<E>`, `RandomAccess`, `Cloneable`, `Serializable`.
- ✅ **Default initial capacity** (JDK 8+): Empty array; lazily grows to **10** on first `add()`.
- ✅ **Growth formula** (JDK 8+): $C_{new} = C_{old} + \lfloor C_{old} / 2 \rfloor$ (i.e., **1.5× growth**).
- ✅ **Random access (`get`)** is **O(1)**; middle **insertion/deletion** is **O(n)**.
- ✅ **Not synchronized** — not thread-safe; use `Collections.synchronizedList()` for thread safety.
- ✅ **Allows duplicates and `null` values** unlike `Set`.
- ✅ **Iterators are fail-fast** — concurrent modification throws `ConcurrentModificationException`.
- ✅ **Use `Iterator.remove()` or `removeIf()` (Java 8+)** for safe deletion during iteration.
- ✅ **Diamond operator** `<>` reduces verbosity (JDK 7+).
- ✅ **Auto-boxing** allows primitives in generic `ArrayList` (e.g., `ArrayList<Integer>`).
- ✅ **Convert to array** using `toArray()`; **convert from array** using `Arrays.asList()` or `new ArrayList<>(Arrays.asList(...))`.
- ✅ For **fixed-size, primitive-heavy, performance-critical** code, prefer **plain arrays** over `ArrayList`.
- ✅ For **thread-safe alternatives**, use `CopyOnWriteArrayList` (read-heavy) or `Collections.synchronizedList()`.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HashSet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEITT611 - OBJECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING IN JAVA
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** HashSet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**HashSet** is a concrete implementation class of the `java.util.Set` interface, part of the **Java Collections Framework (JCF)** introduced in **JDK 1.2**. It is fundamentally backed internally by a **`HashMap` instance** and uses a **hash table data structure** to store unique elements. The class guarantees that the collection contains **no duplicate elements** and permits at most **one `null` element**.

The contract of `HashSet` is governed by three primary mathematical/invariants:

1. **Uniqueness Invariant**: For any two elements `e1` and `e2` in the set, `e1.equals(e2) == false`.
2. **Hashing Invariant**: The internal storage position of an element is determined by `element.hashCode()`.
3. **Performance Invariant**: Basic operations (`add`, `remove`, `contains`, `size`) execute in **constant time O(1)** on average, assuming a well-distributed hash function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight**: HashSet is **not synchronized**. For thread-safe operations, students must use `Collections.synchronizedSet()` or `ConcurrentHashMap`-backed sets. The iteration order is **not guaranteed**; it depends on the hash codes of elements and the internal bucket structure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Declaration (Hierarchy)**:
> `java.lang.Object` → `java.util.AbstractCollection<E>` → `java.util.AbstractSet<E>` → `java.util.HashSet<E>`

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fingerprint Locker" Intuition

Imagine a large **office building with 16 lockers** (the internal `table` array), numbered `0` to `15`. Every employee (an element) is given a **unique fingerprint** (the `hashCode()`). The security guard computes a simple formula:

$$\text{Locker Number} = \text{fingerprint} \mod 16$$

- **Adding an Employee**: The guard computes the fingerprint, finds the locker, and places the employee inside. If a **second employee with the same fingerprint** tries to enter, the guard checks their face against the existing one — if they match (`equals()`), entry is denied (duplicate rejected).
- **Searching an Employee**: The guard does **not** scan all 16 lockers. He instantly computes the locker number from the fingerprint and walks directly to it. This is the **O(1) average-time access**.
- **Collisions**: If two different employees produce the same locker number, they are placed in the **same locker as a small chain** (a linked list or red-black tree bucket).

> [!TIP]
> **Why "Hash-Set" and not "Hash-List"?** A Set, by mathematical definition, contains unique elements. A List allows duplicates. The hash table is simply the *implementation technique* used to enforce this uniqueness efficiently.

## 1.3 When to Use HashSet (Engineering Use-Cases)

| Use-Case Scenario | Why HashSet is Optimal |
|---|---|
| Removing duplicates from a list | Built-in `add()` returns `false` on duplicate |
| Fast membership testing (`contains()`) | **O(1)** average lookup |
| Set arithmetic (union, intersection) | Works with `retainAll()`, `addAll()` |
| Tracking unique visitors/sessions | Insertion-time is constant |
| Deduplicating log entries | Single-pass filtering |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Internal Architecture — The HashMap Backbone

A `HashSet` internally creates a `HashMap` where:
- **Keys** = the elements you add to the set.
- **Values** = a single shared dummy object (a `static final Object PRESENT = new Object();`).

This is why every operation in `HashSet` actually delegates to its internal `HashMap`. For example, `set.add(element)` internally calls `map.put(element, PRESENT)`, which returns the previous value (or `null` if absent) — this is how duplicates are detected.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — The "Why" and "How"

**Why does HashSet use `hashCode()`?**
To convert an element into a numerical index, enabling direct array access in **O(1)** time, bypassing linear scans.

**How is the bucket index computed?**

$$\text{index} = (n - 1) \;\&\; \text{hash}(key)$$

where:
- $n$ = current table capacity (always a power of 2).
- $\text{hash}(key)$ = the **disturbed hash** computed by HashMap's `hash()` method (which applies a secondary spread using the key's own `hashCode()`).

> [!NOTE]
> The bitwise AND `(n-1) & hash` is equivalent to `hash % n` but is **~3x faster** on modern CPUs — a classic computer-science optimization KTU examiners love to test.

## 2.3 Capacity, Load Factor & Rehashing

- **Initial Capacity**: The default is **16** (i.e., table length = 16).
- **Load Factor (0.75)**: A threshold ratio. When `(size / capacity) > 0.75`, the table is **resized (doubled)** and all entries are **rehashed** into the new buckets.
- **Threshold**: `threshold = (int)(capacity × loadFactor)`. For default, `16 × 0.75 = 12`.

This 0.75 trade-off balances **time efficiency** (lower load factor = less collision) against **space efficiency** (higher load factor = smaller table).

## 2.4 Collision Resolution — Linked List → Red-Black Tree

When two keys hash to the same bucket:
- In **JDK 7 and earlier**: Stored as a **singly linked list**. Worst-case lookup = O(n).
- In **JDK 8+ (KTU 2024 default)**: When a bucket's list grows beyond **8 nodes** (`TREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 8`) **AND** the table has at least 64 buckets, the list is converted to a **Red-Black Tree**. Worst-case lookup drops to **O(log n)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is a frequently asked KTU question: "How does HashSet handle collisions in modern Java?" The answer is the **treeification** mechanism.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property / Operation | Formula / Value | Time Complexity (Average) | Time Complexity (Worst) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bucket Index | $(n-1) \,\&\, \text{hash}(k)$ | — | — |
| Disturbed Hash | $h \oplus (h \ggg 16)$ | — | — |
| Resize Threshold | $\text{capacity} \times 0.75$ | — | — |
| `add(e)` | Returns `false` if duplicate | **O(1)** | O(log n) [treeified] |
| `remove(o)` | Removes via hash lookup | **O(1)** | O(log n) |
| `contains(o)` | Tests membership | **O(1)** | O(log n) |
| `size()` | Returns map.size() | O(1) | O(1) |
| `iterator()` | Fail-fast via `modCount` | O(hash capacity) | O(n) |
| Default Initial Capacity | **16** | — | — |
| Default Load Factor | **0.75** | — | — |
| Max entries before treeify | **8** | — | — |
| Min table size for treeify | **64** | — | — |
| Allowed `null` elements | At most **1** | — | — |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

HashSet powers critical production systems:

- **Compilers & IDEs**: Tracking already-imported class names.
- **Web Crawlers**: Maintaining the set of visited URLs to avoid cycles.
- **Database Engines**: Implementing **Hash Joins** and uniqueness constraints.
- **Distributed Systems**: **Deduplication** in Kafka, Cassandra, and Elasticsearch.
- **Security**: Blocking previously-blacklisted tokens or signatures.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough — `HashSet.add(E e)` Operation

Let us trace what happens internally when a programmer writes `set.add("KTU")` for the first time on a default-constructed `HashSet`.

**Step 1 — Delegation to HashMap**

The `HashSet` source code contains:

```java
private transient HashMap<E,Object> map;
private static final Object PRESENT = new Object();

public boolean add(E e) {
    return map.put(e, PRESENT) == null;
}
```

So `add("KTU")` becomes `map.put("KTU", PRESENT)`.

**Step 2 — Computing the Disturbed Hash**

Inside `HashMap.put()`:
1. First, the key's `hashCode()` is called: `"KTU".hashCode()` = (a fixed integer, say $H$).
2. The `hash()` static method applies a secondary spread:

$$
\text{hash}(k) \;=\; h \,\oplus\, (h \ggg 16)
$$

where $h = k.\text{hashCode()}$ and $\ggg$ is the unsigned right shift by 16 bits. The XOR with the high bits distributes entropy into the low bits, mitigating poor hash functions.

**Step 3 — Computing the Bucket Index**

$$
\text{bucketIndex} \;=\; (n - 1) \,\&\, \text{hash}(k)
$$

With $n = 16$ (initial capacity):

$$
\text{bucketIndex} \;=\; 15 \,\&\, \text{hash}(k)
$$

This produces a value in the range $[0, 15]$, pointing to a slot in the internal `Node<K,V>[] table` array.

**Step 4 — Insertion Logic**

The `putVal()` method checks the bucket:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If bucket is empty} &\rightarrow \text{Insert new Node and return null (success)} \\
\text{If bucket has a node with } k.\text{equals}(existingKey) &\rightarrow \text{Overwrite value, return old value (failure for Set)} \\
\text{If bucket is a TreeNode} &\rightarrow \text{Insert via Red-Black tree logic} \\
\text{If bucket is a linked list} &\rightarrow \text{Traverse, insert at tail, treeify if size} > 8
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Size & Threshold Check**

After successful insertion, `size` is incremented. If:

$$
\text{size} \;>\; \text{threshold} \quad (\text{i.e., } \text{size} > 12 \text{ for default config})
$$

then `resize()` is triggered, doubling the table to 32 and re-bucketing all entries using the new mask $(32 - 1) = 31$.

**Step 6 — Return to Caller**

Since `map.put()` returned `null` (no prior mapping for `"KTU"`), `add()` returns `true`. If `"KTU"` were already present, `put()` would return the old `PRESENT` object (non-null), causing `add()` to return `false`.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Numerical Trace

Assume `hashCode("KTU") = 2666` (illustrative). Default capacity $n = 16$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
h &= 2666 \\
\text{Binary of } h \text{ (low 16 bits)} &\approx 0000\,1010\,0110\,1010 \\
h \ggg 16 &= 0 \\
\text{Disturbed hash} = h \oplus (h \ggg 16) &= 2666 \\
\text{Index} = (16 - 1) \,\&\, 2666 &= 15 \,\&\, 2666 = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

The element `"KTU"` is placed in `table[10]`. A subsequent `set.add("KTU")` will:
1. Compute the same index = 10.
2. Find the existing node.
3. Call `"KTU".equals("KTU")` → `true`.
4. Return `false` (duplicate rejected).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Contract Reminder**: Two equal objects **MUST** produce equal hash codes. If you override `equals()` in a custom class but not `hashCode()`, HashSet will fail catastrophically — equal objects will land in different buckets, breaking the uniqueness invariant.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — HashSet vs. Other Sets

| Feature | **HashSet** | **LinkedHashSet** | **TreeSet** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Backing Structure | `HashMap` | `HashMap` + Doubly-Linked List | `TreeMap` (Red-Black Tree) |
| Ordering | None (hash-based) | **Insertion order preserved** | Sorted (natural / Comparator) |
| `add` complexity | **O(1)** | O(1) | O(log n) |
| `contains` complexity | **O(1)** | O(1) | O(log n) |
| `null` allowed | Yes (1 element) | Yes (1 element) | **No** (throws NPE) |
| Memory Overhead | Lowest | Higher (linked list) | Higher (tree nodes) |
| Use-Case | Pure deduplication | Cache-style LRU patterns | Sorted iteration |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip**: If a question states "preserve insertion order" or "no duplicates + sorted", the correct answer is **not** HashSet but **LinkedHashSet** or **TreeSet** respectively. KTU examiners commonly test this distinction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 HashSet Internal Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TB
    clientApp["Client Application Code"]
    hashSetAPI["HashSet Class API"]
    internalMap["Internal HashMap Instance"]
    dummyValue["Static Final Object PRESENT"]
    nodeArray["Node Array table of length 16"]
    bucket0["Bucket 0 LinkedList or TreeNode"]
    bucket1["Bucket 1"]
    bucket5["Bucket 5"]
    bucket10["Bucket 10"]
    bucket15["Bucket 15"]
    sizeVar["Integer size"]
    thresholdVar["Integer threshold equals 12"]
    loadFactor["Load Factor equals 0.75"]
    
    clientApp --> hashSetAPI
    hashSetAPI --> internalMap
    hashSetAPI --> dummyValue
    internalMap --> nodeArray
    nodeArray --> bucket0
    nodeArray --> bucket1
    nodeArray --> bucket5
    nodeArray --> bucket10
    nodeArray --> bucket15
    internalMap --> sizeVar
    internalMap --> thresholdVar
    internalMap --> loadFactor
```

## 4.2 Flowchart — `add()` Operation Lifecycle

```mermaid
graph TB
    startA["Start: set.add element e"]
    checkNull{"e is null?"}
    calcHash["Compute hash equals e.hashCode XOR h right shift 16"]
    calcIndex["Compute index equals capacity minus 1 AND hash"]
    checkBucket{"Bucket at index empty?"}
    insertNode["Insert new Node return true"]
    checkExisting{"Existing node equals e?"}
    overwrite["Overwrite value return false"]
    checkListType{"Bucket is TreeNode?"}
    treeInsert["Insert into Red Black tree"]
    listInsert["Append to linked list"]
    checkTreeify{"List size greater than 8 AND table size at least 64?"}
    treeify["Convert list to Red Black tree"]
    incSize["Increment size"]
    checkThreshold{"size greater than threshold?"}
    resize["Resize table double capacity and rehash"]
    endA["End: Return boolean to caller"]
    
    startA --> checkNull
    checkNull --> calcHash
    calcHash --> calcIndex
    calcIndex --> checkBucket
    checkBucket --> insertNode
    checkBucket --> checkExisting
    checkExisting --> overwrite
    checkExisting --> checkListType
    checkListType --> treeInsert
    checkListType --> listInsert
    listInsert --> checkTreeify
    checkTreeify --> treeify
    checkTreeify --> incSize
    treeify --> incSize
    treeInsert --> incSize
    insertNode --> incSize
    incSize --> checkThreshold
    checkThreshold --> resize
    checkThreshold --> endA
    resize --> endA
    overwrite --> endA
```

## 4.3 Collision Resolution Schematic

```mermaid
graph LR
    bucketIdx["Bucket Index equals 7"]
    head["Node K1 hash equals 7"]
    second["Node K2 hash equals 7"]
    third["Node K3 hash equals 7"]
    treeNode["TreeNode K1 hash equals 7"]
    leftC["Left Child"]
    rightC["Right Child"]
    
    bucketIdx --> head
    head --> second
    second --> third
    bucketIdx -. "If size greater than 8" .-> treeNode
    treeNode --> leftC
    treeNode --> rightC
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation**: A single bucket may contain multiple elements that hash to the same index. Pre-Java 8, this was a linked list (O(n) worst case). From Java 8 onwards, the list is converted to a balanced Red-Black tree when it exceeds 8 nodes, reducing worst-case to O(log n).

## 4.4 HashMap ↔ HashSet Mapping Table

| HashSet Method | Delegates To HashMap Method | Return Semantics |
|---|---|---|
| `add(e)` | `put(e, PRESENT)` | `true` if new, `false` if duplicate |
| `remove(o)` | `remove(o)` | `true` if removed, `false` otherwise |
| `contains(o)` | `containsKey(o)` | `true` if present |
| `size()` | `map.size()` | Element count |
| `clear()` | `map.clear()` | Empties the set |
| `iterator()` | `map.keySet().iterator()` | Iterator over keys |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain the internal working of `java.util.HashSet`. How does it ensure uniqueness of elements?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
`HashSet` is internally backed by a `HashMap` instance. When `add(element)` is called, it invokes `map.put(element, PRESENT)`, where `PRESENT` is a static dummy object. The `put` method computes the element's `hashCode()`, derives a bucket index using `(n-1) & hash`, and checks the bucket. If an element with `equals()` matching already exists, the put returns a non-null value, causing `add()` to return `false`. Thus, uniqueness is enforced through the **hashCode–equals contract**, providing average **O(1)** insertion and lookup time.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the role of the `hashCode()` and `equals()` methods in a `HashSet`? What happens if they are not overridden correctly in a custom class?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
`hashCode()` determines the bucket index where the element is stored, while `equals()` is used to compare elements for logical equality within a bucket. If a custom class overrides `equals()` but not `hashCode()`, two equal objects may land in different buckets, causing **duplicates to be stored** — violating the Set contract. Conversely, if `hashCode()` is overridden to always return a constant, all elements collide into a single bucket, degrading performance to **O(n)**. Both methods **must** be consistent: `a.equals(b) == true` ⇒ `a.hashCode() == b.hashCode()`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the internal architecture of `HashSet`. Discuss how collisions are handled in modern Java (JDK 8+). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write a Java program that demonstrates the use of `HashSet` to remove duplicate elements from an ArrayList of integers. Explain the time complexity of each operation used. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Internal Architecture & Collision Handling**

1. `HashSet` wraps an internal `HashMap<E, Object>`. All added elements become **keys**; the **value** is a shared dummy constant `PRESENT`.
2. The internal `HashMap` stores entries in a `Node<K,V>[] table` array of **default length 16**.
3. **Bucket Index Formula**: `index = (capacity - 1) & hash(key)`.
4. **Collision Handling in JDK 8+**:
   - When two or more keys hash to the same bucket, they are stored as a **linked list**.
   - If the linked list grows beyond **`TREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 8`** AND the table has at least **64 buckets**, the list is converted into a **Red-Black Tree** (`O(log n)` lookup).
   - If the table is too small, it is **resized** first.
5. The **load factor (0.75)** controls resizing: when `size > 0.75 × capacity`, the table is **doubled** and all entries are **rehashed**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (a)**
> - Students frequently forget to mention the **PRESENT dummy object** (lose 1 mark).
> - Confusing HashSet with HashMap architecture costs 2 marks.
> - Not specifying the **TREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 8** and **MIN_TREEIFY_CAPACITY = 64** loses 1 mark.
> - Diagrams must show the **bucket array and chained nodes**, not just a flowchart.

**Part (b) — Java Program to Remove Duplicates**

```java
import java.util.*;

public class RemoveDuplicates {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Step 1: Create ArrayList with duplicates
        ArrayList<Integer> listWithDups = new ArrayList<>(
            Arrays.asList(10, 20, 10, 30, 20, 40, 50, 30)
        );

        // Step 2: Create HashSet from the list
        HashSet<Integer> uniqueSet = new HashSet<>(listWithDups);

        // Step 3: Convert back to ArrayList (optional)
        ArrayList<Integer> listWithoutDups = new ArrayList<>(uniqueSet);

        // Step 4: Display result
        System.out.println("Original List   : " + listWithDups);
        System.out.println("After Deduplication : " + listWithoutDups);
    }
}
```

**Output:**
```
Original List   : [10, 20, 10, 30, 20, 40, 50, 30]
After Deduplication : [50, 20, 40, 10, 30]   // order is not guaranteed
```

**Time Complexity Analysis:**

| Operation | Time Complexity | Explanation |
|---|---|---|
| Creating `HashSet` from `ArrayList` | **O(n)** average | Each `add` is O(1) average; n elements → O(n) |
| `add(e)` per element | O(1) average | Hash lookup + bucket insert |
| `HashSet` → `ArrayList` conversion | O(n) | n elements copied |
| `System.out.println` | O(n) | Traverses the list |
| **Total Program Complexity** | **O(n)** | Linear in number of elements |

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Compare `HashSet`, `LinkedHashSet`, and `TreeSet` in terms of internal data structure, ordering, time complexity, and null acceptance. Provide a Java code snippet demonstrating iteration over a `HashSet`. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concepts of **initial capacity**, **load factor**, and **rehashing** in `HashSet`. How do these parameters affect performance? Provide a suitable Java example that customizes these parameters. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — Comparative Study of Set Implementations**

| Parameter | **HashSet** | **LinkedHashSet** | **TreeSet** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Backing Structure | `HashMap` | `HashMap` + Doubly-Linked List | `TreeMap` (Red-Black Tree) |
| Ordering | None | **Insertion order** | **Sorted** (natural/Comparator) |
| `add()` complexity | O(1) avg | O(1) avg | O(log n) |
| `contains()` complexity | O(1) avg | O(1) avg | O(log n) |
| Allows `null`? | Yes (1 element) | Yes (1 element) | **No** (throws NPE) |
| Iterator fail-fast? | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Use-Case | Pure deduplication | Cache/ordered deduplication | Sorted unique data |

**Java Code — Iterating a HashSet:**

```java
import java.util.HashSet;
import java.util.Iterator;

public class HashSetIteration {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        HashSet<String> courses = new HashSet<>();
        courses.add("Java");
        courses.add("Python");
        courses.add("C++");
        courses.add("Java");      // duplicate, ignored

        // Method 1: Enhanced for-loop
        System.out.println("Enhanced for-loop:");
        for (String s : courses) {
            System.out.println(s);
        }

        // Method 2: Iterator
        System.out.println("\nIterator:");
        Iterator<String> it = courses.iterator();
        while (it.hasNext()) {
            System.out.println(it.next());
        }
    }
}
```

**Part (b) — Capacity, Load Factor, and Rehashing**

1. **Initial Capacity**: The number of buckets in the underlying hash table at creation. Default is **16**. Larger initial capacity reduces rehashing but wastes memory.
2. **Load Factor**: A measure of how full the table is allowed to get before resizing. Default is **0.75**. A lower load factor (e.g., 0.5) reduces collisions but increases memory usage.
3. **Rehashing**: When `size > capacity × loadFactor`, a new table of double the size is created, and all existing entries are recomputed and reinserted. This is an **O(n)** operation but happens infrequently.

**Performance Impact Table:**

| Configuration | Collision Rate | Memory Usage | Rehashing Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|
| Capacity 16, LF 0.75 | Medium | Low | Frequent for large sets |
| Capacity 64, LF 0.75 | Low | Higher | Less frequent |
| Capacity 16, LF 0.5 | Lowest | Highest | Most frequent |
| Capacity 1024, LF 0.9 | High | Lowest | Rare |

**Java Example with Custom Parameters:**

```java
import java.util.HashSet;

public class CustomHashSet {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        // Custom initial capacity = 100, load factor = 0.8
        HashSet<Integer> set = new HashSet<>(100, 0.8f);

        for (int i = 1; i <= 80; i++) {
            set.add(i);
        }

        System.out.println("Set size: " + set.size());
        System.out.println("Threshold when resized: " + (int)(100 * 0.8)); // 80
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Question B**
> - Failing to draw a **comparison table** in part (a) costs 2 marks. KTU expects tabular comparisons.
> - For part (b), students often **omit the rehashing cost analysis** (O(n)) — this is a 2-mark deduction.
> - Not specifying that `HashSet` constructor takes `(initialCapacity, loadFactor)` as `int, float` loses 1 mark.
> - Avoid using `float` for load factor in code without the `f` suffix — common compile-time error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] `HashSet` implements `Set` interface and is **backed by a `HashMap`** internally.
- [x] Uses a **dummy `PRESENT` object** as the value for all entries.
- [x] **Default initial capacity = 16**, **default load factor = 0.75**.
- [x] Bucket index formula: `(n - 1) & hash(k)` (bitwise AND, equivalent to modulo).
- [x] **Disturbed hash**: `h ^ (h >>> 16)` — spreads entropy from high bits to low bits.
- [x] **Uniqueness guaranteed** via the `hashCode()` and `equals()` contract.
- [x] Allows **at most one `null` element**.
- [x] **Iteration order is NOT guaranteed** (use `LinkedHashSet` for insertion order).
- [x] **Not thread-safe** (use `Collections.synchronizedSet` or `ConcurrentHashMap.newKeySet()`).
- [x] **JDK 8+ treeification**: Linked list → Red-Black Tree when bucket size > 8 AND table ≥ 64.
- [x] **Worst-case complexity**: O(log n) per operation post-treeification; **O(n)** pre-Java 8.
- [x] Resizing doubles the table capacity and rehashes all entries (O(n) operation).
- [x] `HashSet` is **fail-fast**: concurrent modification throws `ConcurrentModificationException`.
- [x] **Key Distinction**: `HashSet` ≠ `HashMap` — the Set stores *keys only*; the Map stores *key-value pairs*.
- [x] Constructor signatures: `HashSet()`, `HashSet(int initialCapacity)`, `HashSet(int initialCapacity, float loadFactor)`, `HashSet(Collection<? extends E> c)`.
- [x] The `hashCode()` and `equals()` **must be overridden together** in any custom class used as a HashSet element.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Strategy**: For theory questions, always mention the **backing `HashMap`**, the **`PRESENT` dummy**, the **bucket index formula**, and the **JDK 8+ treeification**. For code questions, always include a **time complexity analysis** — it is a guaranteed 2-mark bonus.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: HashMap
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEITT611 - OBJECT-ORIENTED PROGRAMMING IN JAVA > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** HashMap # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **HashMap** is a class in the `java.util` package that implements the `Map` interface of the Java Collections Framework. It stores data in **key-value pairs** (also called *entries* or *mappings*) and uses a technique called **hashing** to store and retrieve objects in near-constant time. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > *"HashMap is a non-synchronized, hash-table based implementation of the Map interface which permits `null` values and one `null` key. It does not guarantee the order of its elements, especially over time, and provides constant-time performance O(1) for basic operations like `get()` and `put()` under ideal hash distribution."* ### Class Hierarchy of HashMap ``` java.lang.Object └── java.util.AbstractMap └── java.util.HashMap ├── implements Map ├── implements Cloneable ├── implements Serializable ``` ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Analogy: The Library Locker System** > > Imagine a library with **1000 lockers** numbered 0 to 999. When a student returns a book, the librarian doesn't search all 1000 lockers. Instead, she computes a small number from the book's **ISBN (key)** — say the last 3 digits — and goes directly to that locker number. This "magic number calculation" is **hashing**, the locker itself is the **bucket**, and the lookup is almost instant — exactly how a `HashMap` works. - **Key** → The ISBN of the book (used to locate) - **Value** → The book itself (the actual data) - **Hash Function** → The formula that converts ISBN → locker number - **Bucket** → The locker that holds the data - **Collision** → Two books sharing the same locker number ## 1.3 Key Characteristics of HashMap | Characteristic | Value / Description | |---|---| | **Underlying Data Structure** | **Hash Table (Array of Buckets / Nodes)** | | **Implements Interface** | `Map` | | **Allows Duplicate Keys?** | **No** (Keys are unique, overwrites on duplicate) | | **Allows Duplicate Values?** | **Yes** (Values can repeat) | | **Allows `null` Key?** | **Yes** (Only ONE null key allowed) | | **Allows `null` Values?** | **Yes** (Multiple null values allowed) | | **Thread-Safe?** | **No** (Use `Collections.synchronizedMap()` for thread safety) | | **Order Maintained?** | **No** (Use `LinkedHashMap` for insertion order) | | **Sorted?** | **No** (Use `TreeMap` for sorted keys) | | **Default Initial Capacity** | **16** | | **Default Load Factor** | **0.75** | > [!NOTE] > **Why is HashMap in KTU Module 4?** > Module 4 of OEITT611 covers the **Java Collections Framework (JCF)**. HashMap is a flagship class of the `Map` interface hierarchy and is one of the **most frequently asked topics** in KTU university exams, second only to `ArrayList` and `LinkedList`. ## 1.4 Basic HashMap Declaration Syntax ```java // 1. Generic form (Recommended - Type-safe) HashMap studentMap = new HashMap<>(); // 2. With initial capacity HashMap productMap = new HashMap<>(32); // 3. With initial capacity and load factor HashMap wordMap = new HashMap<>(16, 0.75f); // 4. From another Map HashMap newMap = new HashMap<>(existingMap); ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Always use the **diamond operator `<>`** (Java 7+ feature) for type inference. Older answers using `new HashMap()` on the right side may lose style marks. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Internal Architecture of HashMap (Java 8+) Internally, a `HashMap` is an **array of buckets**, where each bucket is a **Node** (a static inner class). When a collision occurs, Java 8+ uses a hybrid structure: - If a bucket has ≤ **8 nodes** → stored as a **Linked List** - If a bucket grows beyond **8 nodes** → converted to a **Red-Black Tree** (O(log n) performance) ``` ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐ │ HashMap (Internal Table - default 16) │ │ ┌──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┬──────┐ │ │ │ [0] │ [1] │ [2] │ [3] │ ... │ [15] │ │ └──┬───┴──────┴──┬───┴──────┴──────┴───┬───┘ │ │ │ │ │ null null LinkedList / Tree └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘ ``` ## 2.2 The Node Static Inner Class ```java static class Node implements Map.Entry { final int hash; // Cached hash to avoid recomputation final K key; // Key (immutable) V value; // Value Node next; // Pointer to next node (for chaining) } ``` ## 2.3 The Four Pillars of HashMap Operations ### Pillar 1: Computing the Hash (`hash()` method) $$h_1 = \text{key}.\text{hashCode}()$$ $$h_2 = h_1 \oplus (h_1 \ggg 16)$$ The **`hash()`** method of HashMap **spreads the high bits into the low bits** to reduce collisions when the table size is small. This is called **"扰动函数" (disturbance function)**. ### Pillar 2: Computing the Bucket Index $$i = (n - 1) \;\&\; h_2$$ Where: - $n$ = current table capacity (always a power of 2: 16, 32, 64…) - $\&$ = bitwise AND - $h_2$ = the disturbed hash > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why `&` instead of `%`?** > Since $n$ is always a power of 2, $(n-1)$ produces a **bitmask of all 1s**. Using `&` is **significantly faster** than `%` (modulo), and mathematically equivalent: $i = h \bmod n$. ### Pillar 3: Insertion — `put(K key, V value)` **Step-by-step algorithm:** 1. Compute `hash(key)` → returns disturbed hash code 2. Calculate index: `i = (n - 1) & hash` 3. Check if `table[i]` is `null` - If **null** → create new `Node` and place at index `i` - If **not null** → collision occurred; traverse the chain/tree 4. If a node with the **same key** exists → **overwrite the value** and return the old value 5. If no matching key → append new `Node` to the end of the chain 6. If chain length > 8 → convert to **Red-Black Tree** 7. Increment `size` 8. If `size > capacity × loadFactor` → **rehash** (resize the table to double) ### Pillar 4: Retrieval — `get(Object key)` **Step-by-step algorithm:** 1. Compute `hash(key)` 2. Calculate index: `i = (n - 1) & hash` 3. Check first node at `table[i]` - If `null` → return `null` (not found) - If `key.equals(firstNode.key)` → return `value` 4. If first node is a **TreeNode** → traverse the Red-Black Tree (O(log n)) 5. Otherwise → traverse the **Linked List** and compare keys using `equals()` (O(n)) 6. If found → return `value`; else return `null` ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master this table — these are the "must-know" facts for KTU exams.** | Concept | Formula / Rule | Default Value | KTU Significance | |---|---|---|---| | **Initial Capacity** | $n_0$ | **16** | Always a power of 2 | | **Load Factor (α)** | $\alpha$ | **0.75** | Time-space trade-off | | **Threshold** | $T = n \times \alpha$ | 12 (at start) | Triggers rehash | | **Bucket Index** | $i = (n - 1) \;\&\; h$ | — | Bitwise AND for speed | | **Disturbed Hash** | $h' = h \oplus (h \ggg 16)$ | — | Spreads high bits to low bits | | **Resize Multiplier** | $n_{new} = 2 \times n_{old}$ | — | Always doubles | | **Treeify Threshold** | List length ≥ **8** | 8 | LinkedList → Red-Black Tree | | **Untreeify Threshold** | Tree shrinks to ≤ **6** | 6 | Tree → LinkedList | | **Time Complexity (Average)** | $O(1)$ | — | `put`, `get`, `remove` | | **Time Complexity (Worst)** | $O(\log n)$ | — | Tree mode in Java 8+ | | **Time Complexity (Pre-Java 8)** | $O(n)$ | — | Worst case with linked list | > [!NOTE] > **Why Load Factor = 0.75?** > - Higher (e.g., 1.0) → less memory, more collisions → slower lookups > - Lower (e.g., 0.5) → less collisions, more memory → wasteful > - **0.75** is a Poisson-distribution optimum used in OpenJDK — it balances **collision probability** and **space cost**. ## 2.5 Collision Resolution in HashMap When two different keys produce the same bucket index, this is a **collision**. HashMap resolves collisions using **Separate Chaining (Closed Addressing)**. **Three Ways Collisions Are Resolved:** 1. **Linked List Chaining (Java 7 and small buckets)** - Each bucket holds a linked list of nodes - Worst case: O(n) traversal 2. **Red-Black Tree Chaining (Java 8+, when list length ≥ 8)** - Self-balancing binary search tree - Worst case: O(log n) traversal - Triggered by the constant `TREEIFY_THRESHOLD = 8` 3. **Rehashing (When load factor exceeded)** - Table capacity is **doubled** - All existing entries are **re-distributed** to new buckets - Triggered when `size > threshold` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Utility in Real World** > - **Databases** use hashing for fast index lookups > - **Caching systems** (Redis, Memcached) use HashMap principles > - **Compilers** use symbol tables built on hash maps > - **Network routers** use hash tables for routing tables > - **Blockchain** uses Merkle trees built on hash principles # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Walkthrough: `put("Kerala", 1956)` Let us **exhaustively** trace the insertion of the key `"Kerala"` with value `1956` into a fresh HashMap. ### Step A: HashCode Computation The `String.hashCode()` algorithm in Java is: $$h = \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} s_i \cdot 31^{n-1-i}$$ For `"Kerala"` (6 characters: K, e, r, a, l, a): - ASCII: K=75, e=101, r=114, a=97, l=108, a=97 $$h_1 = 75 \cdot 31^5 + 101 \cdot 31^4 + 114 \cdot 31^3 + 97 \cdot 31^2 + 108 \cdot 31^1 + 97 \cdot 31^0$$ $$h_1 = 75(28629151) + 101(923521) + 114(29791) + 97(961) + 108(31) + 97(1)$$ $$h_1 = 2147186325 + 93275621 + 3396174 + 93217 + 3348 + 97$$ $$h_1 = 2247378782 \quad \text{(actual Java computation: } h_1 = 1107744926 \text{ after int overflow handling)}$$ > [!NOTE] > Java `String.hashCode()` uses **32-bit `int`** arithmetic with **natural overflow** (wraparound), so the actual numeric result is computed modulo $2^{32}$. For exam purposes, showing the formula is sufficient. ### Step B: Disturbance Function (Spreading Bits) $$\text{hash}(\text{key}) = h_1 \oplus (h_1 \ggg 16)$$ - $h_1$ = original hash code (32 bits) - $(h_1 \ggg 16)$ = upper 16 bits of $h_1$ shifted to the lower 16 positions - $\oplus$ = bitwise XOR ``` h_1 = AAAA AAAA BBBB BBBB CCCC CCCC DDDD DDDD h_1 >>> 16 = 0000 0000 0000 0000 AAAA AAAA BBBB BBBB ───────────────────────────────────────────────────── XOR result = AAAA AAAA BBBB BBBB (AAAA^CCCC) (BBBB^DDDD) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is this needed?** > When the table is small (e.g., capacity = 16), only the **last 4 bits** of the hash are used in `(n-1) & hash`. Without disturbance, keys with similar low bits would always collide. The XOR spreads upper bits down to influence the bucket index. ### Step C: Bucket Index Calculation $$i = (n - 1) \;\&\; h_2$$ With $n = 16$: $$i = (16 - 1) \;\&\; h_2 = 15 \;\&\; h_2 = (1111)_2 \;\&\; h_2$$ This extracts the **last 4 bits** of $h_2$ (since $15 = 0b1111$), giving an index in $[0, 15]$. **Example:** Suppose $h_2 = 0b...1101\,0100$ - $(n-1) = 0b...0000\,1111$ - AND result: $0b...0000\,0100 = 4$ - Therefore $i = 4$ — the entry will be placed at **bucket index 4**. ### Step D: Insertion into the Bucket 1. Check `table[4]` 2. If `table[4] == null` → create new `Node` with `hash`, `key="Kerala"`, `value=1956`, `next=null` 3. Place this node at `table[4]` 4. `size = 1` (now $1 \leq 12$, threshold not exceeded) ## 3.2 Exhaustive Collision Resolution Example Suppose we now call `put("Delhi", 1947)`, and the bucket index happens to also be $i = 4$. **Trace:** 1. Compute `hash("Delhi")` → some value $h_D$ 2. Index: $i = (15) \;\&\; h_D = 4$ 3. `table[4] != null` → **collision!** 4. Traverse the chain at bucket 4: - First node: key = `"Kerala"`. Check `key.equals("Delhi")` → **false** (keys are different) 5. Append new `Node` to end of chain: - `"Kerala"`.next → `new Node("Delhi", 1947)` 6. `size = 2` (still $\leq 12$) **Updated chain at bucket 4:** ``` table[4] → [hash_K | "Kerala" | 1956 | next] → [hash_D | "Delhi" | 1947 | null] ``` ## 3.3 Exhaustive Rehashing Example Suppose we keep inserting until `size = 12` and the next insertion would make `size = 13$. **Trigger condition:** $$\text{size} > \text{capacity} \times \text{loadFactor}$$ $$13 > 16 \times 0.75 = 12 \quad \checkmark \text{ (true)}$$ **Rehash steps:** 1. Create a new table with `newCapacity = 2 × oldCapacity = 32` 2. Recalculate threshold: $T_{new} = 32 \times 0.75 = 24$ 3. For each existing node: - Recompute `hash(key)` (cached, no recomputation needed) - Recalculate index: $i_{new} = (32 - 1) \;\&\; h_2 = 31 \;\&\; h_2$ - Place into new bucket 4. Old table becomes eligible for **garbage collection** > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Exam Insight:** Rehashing is an **expensive O(n) operation**. In production, if you know the approximate number of elements upfront, **always pre-size** your HashMap to avoid frequent rehashes. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Method-by-Method Complexity | Operation | Method | Average | Worst (Pre-Java 8) | Worst (Java 8+) | Returns | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Insert | `put(K, V)` | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | Old value or `null` | | Retrieve | `get(K)` | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | Value or `null` | | Remove | `remove(K)` | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | Removed value or `null` | | Contains Key | `containsKey(K)` | $O(1)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(\log n)$ | `boolean` | | Contains Value | `containsValue(V)` | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | `boolean` | | Size | `size()` | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | `int` | | Is Empty | `isEmpty()` | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | $O(1)$ | `boolean` | | Clear | `clear()` | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | `void` | ## 3.5 Algebraic Proof: Why $(n-1) \;\&\; h \equiv h \bmod n$ when $n = 2^k$ **Given:** $n$ is a power of 2, so $n = 2^k$ for some integer $k \geq 0$. **Step 1:** $n - 1 = 2^k - 1$ **Step 2:** In binary, $2^k - 1$ is a number with exactly $k$ trailing 1s. For example, $n = 16 = 2^4$: - $n - 1 = 15 = (0000\,1111)_2$ **Step 3:** When we compute $(n-1) \;\&\; h$, the bitwise AND zeroes out all bits of $h$ above position $k-1$ and preserves the bottom $k$ bits. **Step 4:** The bottom $k$ bits of any integer $h$ represent the value $h \bmod 2^k$ — which is exactly $h \bmod n$ since $n = 2^k$. **Conclusion:** Therefore, $(n-1) \;\&\; h \equiv h \bmod n$. ∎ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is a classic **KTU 2-mark question**: *"Why does HashMap use `&` instead of `%` to find the bucket index?"* The answer above (using the power-of-2 property) is the full-mark expected response. # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Internal Architecture of HashMap (Block Diagram) ```mermaid graph TB classDef header fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef table fill:#3b82f6,stroke:#1e40af,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef nullBucket fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#9ca3af,color:#374151 classDef node fill:#fbbf24,stroke:#b45309,color:#1f2937,stroke-width:2px classDef treenode fill:#10b981,stroke:#047857,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px HM["HashMap Object
